title: Portable Electrochemical Platform for Point-of-Care Bilirubin Sensing for Liver Diagnostics_supp2-3693203.mp4
authors: Sanket Goel
abstract: Objective: Elevated bilirubin serves as a subtle yet early indicator of liver dysfunction, highlighting the need for a rapid, accessible, and reliable point-of-care solution. To the best of our knowledge, this study presents the first portable electrochemical platform for point-of-care bilirubin sensing for liver diagnostics. The portable device features onboard power management and a customized analog front end with precise signal acquisition, conditioning, and filtering to support Cyclic Voltammetry (CV), Differential Potential Voltammetry (DPV), and amperometric techniques for the electrochemical detection of any analyte. Herein, the optimised gold nanoparticles/SPCE enables effective bilirubin adsorption and subsequent oxidation at ∼0.35 V. An analyte-selective nafion–chitosan membrane suppressed interferences, achieving a 0.3 μM detection limit at pH 7.4. The on-device detection was evaluated by utilising the standard electrochemical setup, followed by high selectivity, repeatability, and reproducibility. Real-sample testing revealed ∼9.5% deviation from clinical values, confirming strong agreement with conventional methods. The on-device bilirubin detection through the amperometry technique delivers results both on-device and via a wireless user interface. In the future, the developed device can potentially detect other bioanalytes with minor modifications in terms of suitable chemistry.

title: Analysis and Mitigation of Power System Harmonics in Industrial Vfd Applications Using Passive Filter Configurations
authors: Md Shahin Munsi, Agboola B. Alao, Bharath Kumar Dasari, Yared Brhane Tadese, Bibek Dhakal, Manohar Chamana, Olatunji M. Adeyanju, Stephen Bayne
publish: 2026 IEEE Texas Power and Energy Conference (TPEC)
abstract: Harmonic distortion caused by non-linear industrial loads such as variable frequency drives (VFDs) poses major challenges to power quality issue and equipment reliability. This paper investigates harmonic mitigation techniques for an industrial distribution network comprising two transformers operating in parallel and supplying VFD driven loads. A simulation model was developed in MATLAB-Simulink to evaluate the effectiveness of passive filter configurations under realistic load and transformer conditions. The study analyzes critical filter design parameters, tuning frequency, and quality factor, and examines optimal placement of filters. Comparative results between filtered and unfiltered cases demonstrate substantial reductions in total harmonic distortion (THD) of current and voltage waveforms, confirming compliance with IEEE 519-2022 limits. The findings highlight that properly designed passive filters remain a cost-effective and reliable solution for mitigating harmonics in industrial facilities with VFDs, while emphasizing practical challenges such as resonance, component losses, and optimal placement.

title: Short-Circuit Blackbox Modeling of Inverter-Based Resources: Development, Validation and Key Insights
authors: Dol Raj Kunwar, Mohammad Zadeh, Yuhao Zhou
publish: 2026 IEEE Texas Power and Energy Conference (TPEC)
abstract: The fault characteristics of Inverter-Based Resources (IBRs) are highly dependent on their internal control strategies, which can vary among manufacturers. As a result, the existing generic Short-Circuit (SC) models often struggle to accurately represent the fault responses of IBRs from different vendors. To address this limitation, efforts are underway between IBR and SC program vendors towards the development of a proprietary Blackbox DLL (Dynamic Linked Library) model for steady-state SC analysis. This paper represents the first attempt to implement the SC Blackbox DLL concept. In this paper, Blackbox SC models are developed for five different IBR-vendors by studying the fault responses of their Electromagnetic Transient (EMT) models. The developed models were implemented in a SC software, and the accuracy of the developed model's response in SC software tool was validated by comparing the SC simulation results against EMT benchmarks. Throughout this study, valuable insights into the key attributes of the fault characteristics of IBRs were gained and shared in this paper aiming to contribute to a deeper understanding of fault behavior of IBRs. Moreover, the paper also briefly introduces a new Norton's/Thevenin's equivalent circuit-based current source model of an IBR that demonstrates significantly better convergence tendency as compared to the conventional current source model.

title: Self-Healing of Microgrids or Distribution Systems Using Radio Pulse Injected on a Neutral Wire
authors: Nathaniel Wallace, Olga Lavrova, Michael Ropp
publish: 2026 IEEE Texas Power and Energy Conference (TPEC)
abstract: Self-healing power systems offer significant resilience benefits. Most existing self-healing concepts depend on high-speed communications for data sharing, which can be cost-prohibitive or unreliable during emergency events. Greater benefit could be achieved if the system could autonomously identify the relative locations of the line relays. Using lower-bandwidth and less expensive communication methods. This paper presents and analyzes an approach using a radio-frequency pulse transmitted over the neutral line of a microgrid to enable line relays to automatically determine their electrical relationships and enhance self-healing capability.

title: Diffusion Bridge-Based Joint Estimation and Detection Design for Superimposed-Pilot OFDM Receiver
authors: Rui Zeng, Jingbo Tan, Xu Shi, Jintao Wang, Yifeng Zhang
abstract: Superimposed-pilot (SP) transmission improves spectral efficiency but poses significant challenges for receiver design due to pilot–data interference, which conventional algorithms struggle to address effectively under fast-fading conditions. To address this issue, this paper proposes a diffusion bridge–based receiver for joint channel estimation and data detection. By formulating the received signals as one endpoint distribution and the transmitted data together with the channel impulse response as the other endpoint distribution, the conditional probability path between them is learned while implicitly exploiting OFDM structural constraints. To enable more stable and efficient training, the network is designed to directly predict the clean output, and the corresponding flow-velocity loss function for data detection is derived. Furthermore, a two-stage factor scheduler is introduced to balance the training objectives of channel estimation and data detection. Simulation results show that the proposed method achieves lower channel estimation mean square error (MSE) and data detection bit error rate (BER) than existing schemes, while exhibits sufficient generalization ability at higher subcarriers and modulation orders, demonstrating its effectiveness as a data-driven solution for reliable communication in dynamic environments.

title: Impact of Geographically Dispersed Solar Farms on System Reliability
authors: Anh Nguyen, Thi Ha Nguyen
publish: 2026 IEEE Texas Power and Energy Conference (TPEC)
abstract: Despite the environmental and economic benefits, high penetrations of solar photovoltaic generation with intermittent output and low inertia introduce more fluctuations into modern power grids. Due to the nondispatchable characteristic, developing appropriate methods to control the integration of photovoltaic energy is important to ensure system reliability. This paper proposes an advanced method for aggregating solar power plants across diverse geographic locations and taking advantage of the difference in time zones to reduce net variability and ramping magnitudes, thereby increasing system reliability while smoothing out and advancing PV integration. Using irradiance data from the National Solar Radiation Database, the proposed method proves that spatial and temporal diversity among solar farms reduces short-term variability, suppresses extreme ramps, and increases the effective capacity of solar farms. The IEEERTS 79 system and Monte Carlo simulation are used to assess the reliability of the power grid to prove the efficacy of the method.

title: Resource Adequacy in United States Power Markets During the Digital Era: A Comparative Review of Principles and Current Practices
authors: Etornam Komla Ahorsu, Mahdi Mehrtash, Muhammad R. Hammad, Dillon Seward, Abdelwahab D. Bebars, Poria Fajri, Hanif Livani
publish: 2026 IEEE Texas Power and Energy Conference (TPEC)
abstract: This paper provides a comparative review of resource adequacy mechanisms across major U.S. electricity market operators as they address contemporary challenges including data center demand growth, increased renewable integration, and climatic variability. By examining standards, modeling tools, and reserve margin requirements, this work elucidates key differences among Electric Reliability Council of Texas (ERCOT), Midcontinent Independent System Operator (MISO), New York Independent System Operator (NYISO), ISO New England (ISO-NE), Pennsylvania-New Jersey-Maryland (PJM), Southwest Power Pool (SPP), and California Independent System Operator (CAISO). The review synthesizes recent market operator, regulatory, and reliability assessment documents to highlight the evolving criteria and practices adopted by market operators. The results offer a comprehensive basis for understanding how varying regional approaches to resource adequacy collectively shape the reliability landscape of the U.S. electric grid.

title: Efficient Ambiguity Resolution for BDS-3/INS positioning with Multi-frequency Measurements
authors: Kai Xiao, Yuzhe Wang, Runyi Shi, Lundong Zhang, Wanli Li, Jiansheng Li, Guorui Xiao, Peiyuan Zhou
abstract: The global BeiDou navigation system/Inertial Navigation System (BDS-3/INS) has been widely applied. Ambiguity resolution (AR) is critical for achieving centimeter-level positioning accuracy for BDS-3/INS. This study proposes a novel inertial aided multi-frequency ambiguity resolution method (I-MAR), which addresses challenges in reliability and efficiency when existing methods are used for BDS-3/INS. First, the state and measurement equations for BDS-3/INS are introduced. Then, the mathematical model of I-MAR was established, based on which optimal measurement combinations for triple-, quad-, and five-frequency scenarios were investigated. The performance of I-MAR was compared with typical methods such as INS-aided LAMBDA and MCAR through vehicle test data. The AR correct rate of I-MAR is comparable to INS-aided LAMBDA but with significantly higher efficiency. Moreover, the BDS-3 signal loss-recovery scenario was simulated, and instantaneous single-epoch ambiguity recovery was realized after a signal loss of up to 40 s. Finally, the accuracy of position, velocity, and attitude was assessed; the position error in the east, north, and up directions were 0.028m, 0.02m, and 0.035m, respectively. This further verifies the suitability and superiority of the I-MAR when applied to BDS-3/INS.

title: Secure Data Aggregation for Oilfield Transport and Inspection Vehicles Using Onion-Routed Federated Learning
authors: Ramya Ganesh, Shruti Rana, Prushti Patel, Chinmay Trivedi, Jemi Kavar, Robin Bavarva, Lakshin Pathak, Devamm Patel
publish: 2026 IEEE Texas Power and Energy Conference (TPEC)
abstract: The safety and communication abilities of vehicular ad-hoc networks (VANETs) are highly dynamic and thus vulnerable to distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks, which exploit the network's dynamic topology. This paper proposes an onion-routed federated learning framework for privacy-preserving, real-time DDoS detection in VANETs deployed in the oil and gas industry. In such industrial environments, fleets of vehicles transport hazardous materials and equipment across remote and high-risk sites, where secure and reliable communication is vital for operational safety. A TinyML-based model is trained locally on each vehicle's network traffic, and the model updates are securely encrypted and anonymized using onion routing before aggregation, ensuring that raw data never leaves the node. A 5G-enabled communication layer guarantees low-latency and dependable update transfer among vehicles, roadside units, and the central aggregator. Intensive testing on the LR-HR DDoS 2024 data indicates that the suggested framework can attain a global model precision of 0.9988 and a global loss of 0.043, which guarantees a high level of accuracy and efficiency in detecting the low-rate and high-rate DDoS attacks. The findings demonstrate that the framework can achieve the privacy and security, and real-time performance balance in highly dynamic oil and gas VANET environments.

title: RadioYNet: Efficient 3D Path Loss Map Prediction with Height-Aware Feature Fusion
authors: Chengkai Liu, Guozhen Xia, Zeyu Cui, Shiyang Jiang, Xinyu Liao, Yuqiang Zhang, Jia Shi, Chen Zhou, Zan Li
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: Path loss (PL) map prediction is an important task for wireless propagation modeling and network planning. Neural network (NN)-based approaches have been widely studied due to their higher efficiency than ray tracing (RT). However, most existing methods focus on two-dimensional (2D) PL maps. To address the three-dimensional (3D) PL map prediction problem, this letter proposes a multi-altitude PL map prediction framework based on an NN architecture. The proposed model adopts a dual-encoder design to extract heterogeneous features from the environment and the transmitter (Tx) location, and uses a height-aware feature fusion module to embed altitude conditioning into the fused features, enabling the network to differentiate PL patterns across altitude planes. Experimental results show that the proposed model improves prediction accuracy over representative baselines, and substantially reduces inference time compared with RT.

title: Curiosity-Driven Reinforcement Learning for Autonomous Navigation in Magnetically Controlled Capsule Endoscopy
authors: Soheil Dehghani, Parastoo Mohsenipour, Farhan Pahlevani Kiya, Mahshad Mohammadi, Raha Farahani, Borhan Beigzadeh
abstract: Gastrointestinal disorders affect approximately 40% of the global population, presenting diagnostic challenges that conventional endoscopic procedures struggle to address due to invasiveness and miss rates ranging from 6% to 28%. While magnetically controlled capsule endoscopy (MCCE) offers a minimally invasive alternative, achieving comprehensive anatomical coverage remains challenging due to sample inefficiency and limited robustness in reinforcement learning-based navigation systems. This paper introduces Adaptive Spectral Exploration - Proximal Policy Optimization (ASE-PPO), a curiosity-driven reinforcement learning framework combining Random Network Distillation (RND) with spectral parameter space noise for autonomous gastric navigation. The primary novelty lies in the synergistic integration of three contributions: spectrally structured colored noise enabling coherent directional exploration, direct coupling between RND-derived novelty and parameter perturbation amplitude, and an O(1) incremental normalization framework for scalable deployment. Validation in VR-Caps demonstrates that ASE-PPO achieves 95.4% gastric coverage at 120 seconds — representing 4.15–6.17% improvements over baseline PPO — with ablation studies confirming RND’s contribution beyond spectral noise alone. ASE-PPO additionally demonstrates 18.6% standard deviation reduction and 21.8% coefficient of variation improvement, with statistically significant coverage gains at 120 seconds (p = 0.035), establishing curiosity-driven reinforcement learning with structured exploration as a promising paradigm for autonomous gastrointestinal examination systems.

title: An In-Loop Filter PLL with Fixed-Frequency Enhanced ROGI Prefilter for Three-Phase Grid-Connected Converters
authors: Sapram Giridhar, Chandrasekaran S, Ashwani Kumar, Ishan Sharma
publish: 2026 IEEE Texas Power and Energy Conference (TPEC)
abstract: Accurate and fast estimation of grid voltage attributes namely phase, amplitude, and frequency is very challenging particularly under non-ideal and dynamically varying grid conditions. This paper presents an improved fast estimation technique based on an fixed-frequency enhanced reduced-order generalized integrator (FF-EROGI) as pre-filter for harmonic rejection and orthogonal signal generation. Moving average filter with phase-lead compensation incorporated within the phaselocked loop (PLL) mitigates the effects of grid disturbances. However, fixed-frequency structures introduce estimation errors under off-nominal frequency conditions, necessitating an effective correction mechanism with a fast settling time. To address this limitation, the proposed FF-EROGI technique implements a linear regression curve-fitting method to correct the amplitude and phase errors. The proposed approach significantly improves the dynamic response and robustness of the estimator under adverse grid conditions. Experimental validation is carried out using a dSPACE DS1104 real-time controller board interfaced with MATLAB/Simulink demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed technique.

title: Unified Energy Consumption Modeling for Heterogeneous UAVs: An Adaptative Incremental Learning-Based Framework
authors: Liangqi Wang, Zhiwei Wei, Bing Li, Yu Fu, Rongqing Zhang
abstract: Accurate energy consumption modeling is a prerequisite for reliable mission scheduling and network coverage in the low-altitude economy. However, the proliferation of heterogeneous Uncrewed Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) introduces significant challenges: existing models are typically platform-specific, failing to generalize across diverse aerodynamic structures, while retraining for every new UAV type is computationally prohibitive. To address these issues, we propose UECM-IL, an incremental learning-based framework that establishes a unified energy consumption model. First, to explicitly capture both the sequential evolution of flight states and the intrinsic dependencies among features, we develop a Knowledge Graph guided Temporal-Graph Network (KG-TGN). This dual-channel architecture utilizes a Temporal Convolutional Network (TCN) to capture sequential flight dynamics and a Graph Convolutional Network (GCN) guided by a domain-knowledge graph to model inter-feature correlations. Second, to enable cross-domain generalization without catastrophic forgetting, we introduce an incremental learning strategy termed Overfitting-aWare learning rate scheduling and Experience Replay (OWER). This mechanism allows the model to sequentially adapt to new aerial platforms while retaining historical domain knowledge. Finally, we construct the Multi-scenario Heterogeneous UAV Energy Consumption Dataset (MH-UECD), collected via the AirFogSim platform across five distinct aerodynamic configurations. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the unified model achieves an average Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) of 2.0% across heterogeneous domains, offering a scalable solution for energy-aware low-altitude management.

title: MalElves: Reinforcement Learning-Driven Adversarial Example Generation for Evading Cross-Platform ELF Malware Detection
authors: Zhangbo Long, Letian Sha, Yan Lin, Peijie Sun, Haiping Huang, Fu Xiao, Zhiquan Liu
abstract: Adversarial Example (AE) generation is a key instrument for stress-testing and hardening malware detectors, yet most existing techniques target Portable Executable (PE) files and do not transfer cleanly to Executable and Linkable Format (ELF) binaries prevalent in Internet of Things (IoT) environments. We propose <monospace xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">MalElves</monospace>, a reinforcement learning-driven AE generation framework for cross-platform ELF malware. <monospace xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">MalElves</monospace> makes three core technical contributions. First, a code-data-aware manipulation framework unifies obfuscation and rewriting across ARM, ×86, and ×64 architectures while preserving functionality. Second, a sample-efficient state design reduces 2,350 raw ELF features to a compact 21-dimensional input. Third, a shaped multi-detector reward uses fully disclosed PPO settings for full reproducibility. We evaluate <monospace xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">MalElves</monospace> on 161,414 malware samples and 74,260 benign samples. We test against four static detectors and a behavioral-ensemble stress test. The method achieves average ASRs of 89.9%, 85.3%, 63.8%, 60.6%, and 24.7% across detectors. The overall average ASR reaches 64.8%. Each successful evasion requires 2.24 interaction rounds on average.

title: Battery Health Prognosis Using Physics-Informed Neural Network with Quantum Feature Mapping
authors: Muhammad Imran Hossain, Md Fazley Rafy, Sarika Khushalni Solanki, Anurag K. Srivastava
publish: 2026 IEEE Texas Power and Energy Conference (TPEC)
abstract: Accurate battery health prognosis using State of Health (SOH) estimation is essential for the reliability of multiscale battery energy storage, yet existing methods are limited in generalizability across diverse battery chemistries and operating conditions. The inability of standard neural networks to capture the complex, high-dimensional physics of battery degradation is a major contributor to these limitations. To address this, a physics-informed neural network with the Quantum Feature Mapping(QFM) technique (QPINN) is proposed. QPINN projects raw battery sensor data into a high-dimensional Hilbert space, creating a highly expressive feature set that effectively captures subtle, non-linear degradation patterns using Nyström method. These quantum-enhanced features are then processed by a physics-informed network that enforces physical constraints. The proposed method achieves an average SOH estimation accuracy of 99.46% across different datasets, substantially outperforming state-of-the-art baselines, with reductions in MAPE and RMSE of up to 65% and 62%, respectively. This method was validated on a large-scale, multi-chemistry dataset of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{3 1 0, 7 0 5}$</tex> samples from 387 cells, and further showed notable adaptability in cross-validation settings, successfully transferring from one chemistry to another without relying on target-domain SOH labels.

title: Mixture Density Networks Improve Inter-Sensor Consistency of Optical Water Type Classification
authors: Davide Lomeo, Stefan G. H. Simis, Mark A. Warren, David Moffat, Anne D. Jungblut, Emma J. Tebbs
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: In aquatic remote sensing, Optical Water Type (OWT) pre-classification has been used to map suitable algorithms to optically diverse targets and over optical gradients. However, current methods can yield inconsistent type assignments across sensors with different spectral capabilities. Probabilistic neural networks are increasingly used for constituent retrieval in optically complex inland water bodies because they can deliver higher accuracy than empirical or semi-analytical algorithms, while also quantifying estimation uncertainty. However, their interpretability within bio-optical theory remains elusive. To address the limitations brought by these two methodological streams, we train Mixture Density Networks (MDN) to predict OWT membership distributions. OWTs serve as an optical-biogeochemical interpretable scaffold, alongside associated classification uncertainty, while the classification misalignment between Sentinel-3 Ocean and Land Colour Instrument (OLCI) and Sentinel-2 Multispectral Instrument (MSI) is reduced. Using > 29,000 co-located OLCI and MSI observations from 59 lakes, MDN ensembles trained to reproduce OLCI-derived OWT membership scores achieved 99% overall accuracy for OLCI and improved MSI cross-sensor OWT agreement from 58% to 73%, compared to using spectral angle as OWT distance metric. The MDNs enabled identification of aleatoric (upstream bias) and epistemic (lack of representative data) components of the uncertainty envelope, with post-hoc uncertainty recalibration used to adjust uncertainty magnitudes to reliable confidence intervals, reducing miscalibration by 93% for OLCI and 80% for MSI, for both 68% and 95% intervals. Application of trained MDN ensembles across optically diverse systems confirmed that they learned generalisable optical relationships, improving OWT classification agreement across sensors, and providing diagnostics for bias identification. This approach preserves the interpretability of OWTs while enabling uncertainty aware, cross-mission processing, identifying likely sources of classification uncertainty, particularly useful for less capable sensors, which can guide appropriate post-processing and suitable algorithm selection and blending.

title: Disturbance Perception and Accurate Modeling of Magnetic Suspension Flywheel Battery Based on Dynamic Coupling Preview Matrices
authors: Weiyu Zhang, Yao Wu
abstract: The magnetic suspension flywheel battery is a multisubsystem integrated entity, with multiple inherent cross-coupling effects within it. Under complex road conditions, these effects can become unknown disturbances that affect model parameters and reduce control accuracy. Due to the complex and variable nature of the coupling effects, it is difficult to completely decouple them. Therefore, in contrast to the traditional approach of considering them as disturbances that need to be suppressed or decoupled, the coupling effects disturbances are treated as effective modeling information and the disturbance perception and accurate modeling method based on the dynamic coupling preview matrices are proposed in this article. Through the analysis of coupling mechanism, the influence of the disturbance of the multiphysics fields coupling effects on the parameters of the flywheel battery model is perceived, and the dynamic coupling preview matrices that characterizes the variation of parameters under disturbance is proposed in this article. Then, based on preview matrices, an accurate suspension force model that can anticipate the influence of the coupled effect disturbance and update the parameters according to the working conditions is established to improve the control accuracy of the system. Finally, the results of the validity and performance experiments verify the accuracy of the proposed model and the stability of the overall system constructed based on this model.

title: COIVis : Eye-tracking-based Visual Exploration of Concept Learning in MOOC Videos
authors: Zhiguang Zhou, Ruiqi Yu, Yuming Ma, Hao Ni, Guojun Li, Li Ye, Xiaoying Wang, Yize Li, Yigang Wang, Yong Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Massive Open Online Courses (MOOCs) make high-quality instruction accessible. However, the lack of face-to-face interaction makes it difficult for instructors to obtain feedback on learners' performance and provide more effective instructional guidance. Traditional analytical approaches, such as clickstream logs or quiz scores, capture only coarse-grained learning outcomes and offer limited insight into learners' moment-to-moment cognitive states. In this study, we propose COIVis, an eye-tracking-based visual analytics system that supports concept-level exploration of learning processes in MOOC videos. COIVis first extracts course concepts from multimodal video content and aligns them with the temporal structure and screen space of the lecture, defining Concepts of Interest (COIs), which anchor abstract concepts to specific spatiotemporal regions. Learners' gaze trajectories are transformed into COI sequences, and five interpretable learner-state features—Attention, Cognitive Load, Interest, Preference, and Synchronicity—are computed at the COI level based on eye tracking metrics. Building on these representations, COIVis provides a narrative, multi-view visualization enabling instructors to move from cohort-level overviews to individual learning paths, quickly locate problematic concepts, and compare diverse learning strategies. We evaluate COIVis through two case studies and in-depth user-feedback interviews. The results demonstrate that COIVis effectively provides instructors with valuable insights into the consistency and anomalies of learners' learning patterns, thereby supporting timely and personalized interventions for learners and optimizing instructional design.

title: From Rules to Reasoning: Bridging Rule-based and LLM-based Machine Translation
authors: Jinyi Zhang, Dashuai Deng, Haosen Lou, Yingfeng Luo, Ye Tian, Tong Xiao, Tadahiro Matsumoto
publish: IEEE Transactions on Audio, Speech and Language Processing
abstract: In the evolution of machine translation, rule-based machine translation (RBMT) played a foundational role because of its systematic structure and interpretability, yet its knowledge has rarely been reused directly in large language model (LLM)-based machine translation. This paper bridges rule-based and neural paradigms by converting symbolic translation rules from a real-world RBMT system into instruction data for LLM fine-tuning. We construct a pipeline covering rule extraction, component analysis, prompt conversion, and rule-guided training. Using Japanese<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\rightarrow$</tex-math></inline-formula>Chinese rules from the jaw/Chinese system developed by the Ikeda–Matsumoto Laboratory at Gifu University, we fine-tune four LLMs of different scales and evaluate rule-based instruction fine-tuning across Japanese<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\leftrightarrow$</tex-math></inline-formula>Chinese, Chinese<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\leftrightarrow$</tex-math></inline-formula>English, and German<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\rightarrow$</tex-math></inline-formula>English translation. To our knowledge, this is the first systematic attempt to transform an operational RBMT rule base into instruction data for LLM-based machine translation. Experiments show that rule-based fine-tuning substantially improves small and medium-sized models in Japanese<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\rightarrow$</tex-math></inline-formula>Chinese translation, with Llama-3.2-3B gaining about 17 BLEU points and 5 COMET points, while larger models such as Gemma-2-9B obtain smaller BLEU gains but improved output consistency. Additional experiments on reverse, cross-lingual, and two-stage fine-tuning settings confirm that structured rules can provide useful guidance beyond ordinary parallel-data adaptation. The analysis indicates that, although stronger LLMs depend less on external rules, rule-based inputs still serve as linguistic scaffolds that improve syntactic alignment, terminology control, and translation reliability. These findings highlight the value of reusing RBMT knowledge for interpretable and controllable LLM-based machine translation.

title: On Information Theoretic Fairness: From Perfect To Bounded Demographic Parity
authors: Amirreza Zamani, Abolfazl Changizi, Mikael Skoglund
abstract: In this paper, we study the fundamental limits in the design of fair representations under different demographic (statistical) parity constraints through the lens of information theory. We first consider the design problem achieving perfect demographic parity. More specifically, an agent uses some useful data X to solve a task T. Since both X and T are correlated with some sensitive attribute or secret S, the agent designs a representation Y that has no information about the sensitive attribute S, i.e., satisfying perfect demographic parity constraint, implying that I(Y ; S) = 0. We then relax the perfect demographic parity and consider a bounded-parity constraint, implying that I(Y ; S) ≤ ϵ. Under perfect demographic parity, we consider two scenarios. First, we consider a design problem where we want to maximize the information I(Y ; T) that the representation contains about the task, while constraining the level of compression (or encoding rate), that is, ensuring that I(Y ;X) ≤ r. Second, inspired by the Conditional Fairness Bottleneck problem, we consider a design problem where we want to maximize the information I(Y ; T|S) that the representation contains about the task which is not shared by the sensitive attribute, while constraining the amount of irrelevant information, that is, ensuring that I(Y ;X|T, S) ≤ r. Under bounded demographic parity, we design Y that maximizes the mutual information I(Y ; T) about the task while satisfying a bounded compression (or encoding rate) constraint, that is, ensuring that I(Y ;X) ≤ r. Simultaneously, Y satisfies the bounded demographic parity constraint I(Y ; S) ≤ ϵ. To design Y , we use extended versions of the Functional Representation Lemma and the Strong Functional Representation Lemma which are based on randomization techniques and study the tightness of the obtained bounds in special cases. Every design problem studied in this paper can also be interpreted as a code design problem with either perfect privacy or a bounded leakage constraint and a limited rate, considering the sensitive attribute as a secret.

title: A Deterministic Boolean-Logic Switching Control for Grid-Connected Multi-Level PV Microinverters
authors: Abhishek Awasthi, Majid Pahlevani, Praveen Jain
abstract: Grid-connected flying capacitor (FC)-based multi-level converter is investigated as the DC/AC stage in single-phase PV microinverter applications. To achieve reliable performance in grid-connected applications, the control system must effectively achieve inverter current control and flying capacitor voltage balancing in higher-level topologies. Previous research has focused on complex and resource-intensive FC voltage balancing control methods that are not easily scalable to higher-level converter designs. Some of these methods typically employ cost function-based control algorithms, further complicating higher level implementation. This study introduces a deterministic Boolean-logic switching control strategy by employing structured logical decision rules to generate real-time switching vector sequences without requiring an online cost-function solution. The proposed system generates real-time switching vector fields that (a) restrict transitions to adjacent switching vectors to reduce switching losses, (b) avoid consecutive charging/discharging FC states to reduce stress, and (c) prioritize switching vector fields with a minimal number of FCs in series for all voltage levels, thereby minimizing equivalent series resistance (ESR) losses. The proposed switching control provides scalable and deterministic voltage balancing behaviour in DC-AC FC multilevel systems compared with conventional control strategies. Experimental results under steady-state and transient conditions validate simulation results, exhibiting a peak efficiency of 98.7% under nominal AC grid voltage operation.

title: Deep Spatio-Temporal and Frequency Guided Fusion Network for Event-to-Video Reconstruction
authors: Ramna Maqsood, Paulo Nunes, Caroline Conti, Luís Ducla Soares
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Signal Processing
abstract: Event-to-video (E2V) reconstruction has gained significant attention recently for its advantages in enabling high dynamic range and fast motion capture capabilities. However, event data encodes only relative brightness changes, lacking the absolute intensity information necessary for accurate reconstruction. Recent methods incorporate previously reconstructed images to provide intensity references but process them in the spatial domain where low- and high-frequency components are highly coupled. This spatial processing typically leads to the degradation of fine details and introduces artifacts such as over-smoothing, blurring and low contrast reconstruction. To address this, we propose a deep spatio-temporal and frequency guided fusion network for E2V reconstruction (DSTFN-E2V), featuring a dual-path architecture with two key components: i) a prior frequency decomposition module (PFDM), and ii) a spatio-temporal event-driven feature extraction module (STEM). The PFDM decouples low- and high-frequency information from previously reconstructed images and current event voxel grid via a 2D discrete wavelet transform, processing the low-frequency subband through residual blocks to preserve structural coherence and intensity references, while an edge-detail refinement module (ERM) enhances edge and texture details from high-frequency subbands. The frequency-specific features from PFDM and the spatio-temporal features from STEM are then integrated through the proposed event-image fusion blocks (EIFBs) that apply cross-attention across three encoder stages, enabling simultaneous structural preservation and detail recovery. Experiments on four real-world datasets demonstrate that DSTFN-E2V achieves state-of-the-art results with 12% SSIM improvements while being 50% faster than recent attention-based methods, with superior edge fidelity and reduced artifacts.

title: IMRA 2025 TOC
authors: N/A
publish: 2025 International Conference on Intelligent Manufacturing, Robotics and Automation (IMRA)

title: Frequency-Domain Scalar Message Passing Based Joint Target Detection and Localization for Distributed Passive MIMO Radar
authors: Bin Li, Hailong Kang, Jun Li, Qinghua Guo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: Joint target detection and localization using distributed passive MIMO radar has received much attention. However, existing methods are typically computationally expensive and exhibit severe performance degradation under the strong direct-path interference (DPI) and noise variance uncertainty. In this work, we address the challenges by proposing a frequency-domain scalar message passing based joint target detection and localization method. Firstly, we formulate the joint target detection and localization problem in a probabilistic way, and particularly a frequency-domain model is used, which enables low-complexity implementation. Then, a factor graph approach is proposed to efficiently solve the problem, where we take advantage of the frequency-domain model to develop a hybrid scalar message passing algorithm, leading to significant computational complexity reduction. Secondly, to deal with the unknown noise precision (i.e., the reciprocal of noise variance), its estimation is embedded in the proposed algorithm, allowing online noise precision estimation, i.e., the noise variance estimation is simultaneously performed with target detection and localization. Moreover, we prove that the proposed method exhibits the constant false alarm rate (CFAR) property against the unknown noise variances and DPI. Extensive simulation results validate the CFAR property and demonstrate the superior performance of the proposed method compared to state-of-the-art ones, while with lower computational complexity.

title: On the Convergence of the Fast Griffin-Lim Algorithm
authors: Alessandro Ilic Mezza, Massimo Cicognani, Roberto Leone Cicognani, Alberto Bernardini
abstract: The Fast Griffin-Lim Algorithm (FGLA) is one of the most widely used iterative methods for phase reconstruction and signal estimation from modified short-time Fourier transforms. Unlike the classic Griffin-Lim Algorithm (GLA), however, the convergence of FGLA has not yet been fully proved, with existing theoretical guarantees holding only for momenta significantly smaller than those found to perform best in practice. In this letter, we build upon the appendix of the original paper by Griffin and Lim and formulate GLA as a gradient descent algorithm. From this, we show that FGLA corresponds to an accelerated gradient descent method with constant momentum. We then derive a sufficient condition ensuring convergence over a broad range of momenta and formulate a criterion to assess its validity in numerical experiments, which we find is typically satisfied within the first few iterations.

title: Optimal Stealthy Attacks Under Periodic Detection in Cyber-Physical Systems With Round-Robin Protocol
authors: Jian-Xing Wang, Xiao-Guang Zhang, Xiu-Xiu Ren, Peng Gao
abstract: This article focuses on the design of an optimal stealthy attack strategy for cyber-physical systems operating under the round-robin protocol (RRP) in a more realistic setting, where the control period and the detection period are mismatched. The objective is to maximize the attack impact while maintaining stealthiness. In contrast to existing RRP-based attack strategies that assume coincident control and detection periods, the synchronous control-detection assumption is removed, and a novel protocol-aware optimal attack strategy is developed to coordinate actions across detection and nondetection instants. The proposed strategy effectively addresses the challenge posed by the RRP, under which only partial transmitted data can be manipulated at each time step. The optimal attack parameters are obtained by solving a convex optimization problem, where closed-form expressions are derived, avoiding numerical iterations and inequality-based extremum methods required by existing approaches. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed strategy induces more severe degradation in remote state estimation performance and validate the theoretical analysis.

title: Rotor Speed Estimation of Synchronous Generators Based on PMU Data and Tuning of Linearized Swing Equation Coefficients
authors: Thales Pedro S. Esperança, Tatiane Cristina C. Fernandes, Edson L. Geraldi, Guilherme G. Lage, Gustavo P. Eustáquio, Rodrigo A. Ramos
publish: IEEE Open Access Journal of Power and Energy
abstract: The rotor speed of Synchronous Generators (SGs) is a fundamental dynamic state that reflects the stored kinetic energy and the generator’s inertial response to system disturbances. Accurate rotor-speed estimation enables the detection of poorly damped electromechanical oscillations that may lead to instability. However, precise assessment of SG dynamics is often hindered by model inaccuracies and parametric uncertainties. With the increasing deployment of Phasor Measurement Units (PMUs), novel approaches have emerged to enhance post-event system diagnosis, particularly in low-inertia systems. This paper proposes a two-stage framework for estimating the rotor speed of SGs by tuning the coefficients of the linearized swing equation using data from PMUs, thus eliminating the need for the complete Electric Power System (EPS) model. In the first stage, the Morris sensitivity method identifies the most influential coefficients to refine the parameter-estimation domain. In the second stage, an Entropy-Based Genetic Algorithm (EB-GA) refines these coefficients by minimizing the mean squared error between measured EPS dynamics and the model response. Simulation results on the IEEE 68-bus system demonstrate that the proposed framework effectively captures SG rotor-speed dynamics, even under small perturbations and noisy PMU data. The EB-GA achieves more precise parameter tuning than conventional genetic algorithms, improving the representation of SG dynamics for post-event analysis and subsequent controller adjustment in EPSs.

title: Joint Service Caching and Resource Allocation in DT-Empowered Cloud-Edge Networks Via MARL With Hierarchical Knowledge Transfer_supp1-3691308.pdf
authors: Zheyi Chen
abstract: Through bypassing the long-distance transmission of cloud services, Mobile Edge Computing (MEC) reduces response delay and ensures Quality-of-Service (QoS). In resource-constrained MEC, due to the strong coupling between service provisioning and task execution, reasonable service caching and resource allocation face many challenges on 1) coordinating the limited storage resources to improve cache hit rate, 2) allocating limited computing resources under delay constraints, and 3) sample collection and model training in dynamic environments. To address these important challenges, we propose CAMART, a novel service Caching and resource Allocation framework via Multi-Agent Reinforcement learning (MARL) with hierarchical knowledge Transfer in Digital Twin (DT) empowered Cloud-Edge Networks (DTCEN). Specifically, we first construct a new DTCEN to realize the mapping from physical to virtual networks. Next, we decouple the joint optimization of service caching and resource allocation into two sub-problems. For the service caching sub-problem, we design an improved MARL-based method to capture global information via a shared-feature extraction network and optimize caching and offloading decisions via a dual-head feature processing network. For the resource allocation sub-problem, we convert it into 0-1 integer programming and design an improved branch-and-bound-based method to reduce computational complexity while guaranteeing a high-quality solution set. Finally, we develop an original DT-driven hierarchical knowledge transfer mechanism to realize cross-scenario knowledge reuse and convergence acceleration. Using real-world datasets, extensive experiments are conducted to validate the superiority of the proposed CAMART. Compared to the state-of-the-art methods, CAMART achieves higher rewards, task completion rate, and cache hit rate in different scenarios.

title: Dynamic Operating Envelope Calculation for Grid Integration of Large Controllable Digital Loads
authors: Masoud Barati
publish: 2026 IEEE Texas Power and Energy Conference (TPEC)
abstract: This paper introduces a dynamic operating envelope (DOE) framework for integrating large dynamic digital loads (LDDLs), such as hyperscale data centers, into transmission networks. We develop a tractable LDDL model capturing fast IT dynamics, thermal cooling inertia, on-site BESS, and inverter constraints. The DOE is computed via a 5 -minute-resolution, chance-constrained multi-period AC optimal power flow that maximizes envelope width while enforcing voltage-dependent Volt-Watt caps, network security, BESS energy limits, and explicit voltage margins under uncertainty. Case studies on the IEEE 68-bus system demonstrates trade-offs between voltage security and renewable utilization, with voltage standard deviation reduced by 29.1 %, outperforming baselines in reliability and flexibility.

title: An LMI Approach to Admissibility Analysis and H ∞ Observer-Based Control Design with Fault Estimation for Singular Fractional-Order Systems
authors: Arunagirinathan Srinivasan, PooGyeon Park, David Banjerdpongchai
abstract: This paper develops an improved theoretical framework for admissibility analysis and H∞ observer design of a singular fractional-order systems (SFOSs) in the presence of disturbances and faults. A novel bounded real lemma (BRL) is first established in terms of real-valued linear matrix inequalities (LMIs). Unlike previous BRL conditions for SFOSs, the proposed formulation eliminates equality constraints and avoids complex-valued free variables by constructing an equivalent real-domain representation. This leads to a convex optimization condition that guarantees admissibility and prescribed H∞ performance simultaneously, while significantly reducing conservatism. Building upon the derived BRL, both state-feedback control and observer-based control synthesis conditions are developed within a unified LMI framework, enabling simultaneous stabilization and robust performance enforcement for the SFOSs. An observer-based state and fault estimation scheme is then formulated accordingly. The introduction of additional decision matrices enlarges the feasible solution set and enables attenuation level optimization. A systematic minimization procedure for the disturbance attenuation index is established, providing improved robustness guarantees. Comparative numerical results confirm that the proposed theoretical conditions yield a significantly reduced attenuation gain and enhanced estimation performance relative to previous methods, thereby demonstrating the effectiveness and reduced conservatism of the improved theory.

title: Predicting Tactile Sensory Outcome of Physical Human-Robot Interaction through Embodied Learning Strategy_supp2-3692059.mp4
authors: Yuxi Lu
abstract: Estimation of robotic dynamic states in physical human-robot interaction (pHRI) is crucial for robots to handle real-world uncertainties. Since wearable tactile sensor arrays have been increasingly applied in robots, the tactile sensory outcomes (TSOs) during pHRIs would provide practical and ideal information about dynamic states of a robot as responses of its actions. In this work, an embodied learning strategy is introduced to promote the formation of a deep learning-based embodied haptic model (DL-EH), enabling robots to implicitly learn their tactile sensorimotor dynamics in pHRI. Functionally, DL-EH adopts a spatial-temporal assist module (embodied haptic embedding) to assist a compact forward predictor (multimodal mapping and sensory prediction) in learning TSO prediction. Then, it can realize an end-to-end real-time TSO prediction of robot actions in pHRI based on current tactile signals without historical sequences. In real-world experiments, the effectiveness of DL-EH is demonstrated, especially in long-term TSO predictions. The generalization test is performed to validate DL-EH’s generality in new pHRI scenarios. Finally, a robotic framework via the proposed method is proposed for proof-of-concept in the pHRI application.

title: Feasibility and Efficiency of Subliminal Warnings Against Phishing Attacks
authors: Henry Schulz, Feng Zhu
publish: Proceedings of the 2026 ACM Southeast Conference
abstract: None

authors: Adita Kulkarni

abstract: None

title:
Low-Energy Room-Temperature Ni-Functionalized ZnO–TiO
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    Ternary Nanocomposite Thin-Film Transistor Formaldehyde Sensor
authors: Suman Kumar, Subindu Kumar, Basanta Bhowmik
abstract: Fabrication of low-cost Ni–ZnO–TiO2 ternary nanocomposite thin-film transistor for selective formaldehyde detection at room temperature is investigated in the present paper. Dip coating of the zinc oxide solution on top of the hydrothermally synthesized titania nanostructure, followed by Ni functionalization was characterized by X-ray diffraction analysis and field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM). Room-temperature formaldehyde response of Ni-functionalized ZnO–TiO2 ternary composite in thin-film transistor configuration shows a maximum threshold voltage shift of 0.65 V compared to 0.45 V for ZnO–TiO2 sensor and 0.2 V for pristine TiO2 sensor, respectively. Drain current of the ternary nanocomposite sensor was found to be 2.52 times higher than that of the ZnO–TiO2 sensor and 4.32 times more compared to the pristine TiO2 sensor counterpart, respectively. The optimum HCHO response of ~96.43% was measured in the Ni-functionalized ZnO–TiO2 sensor compared to ZnO–TiO2 sensor (51%) and TiO2-based sensor (38%). The sensor exhibited a fast response time of 67 s and recovery time of 66 s for −100 ppm at room temperature operation, demonstrating its suitability for low-power sensing applications. Spillover mechanism of functionalized Ni nanoparticles with the trap-assisted synergistic effect of ZnO–TiO2 interfaces, possibly attributed to maximum accumulation of carriers at the sensing channel leading to higher threshold voltage (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${V}_{\textit {th}}\text {)}$ </tex-math></inline-formula> shifting and highest drain-current (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${I}_{d}\text {)}$ </tex-math></inline-formula> variation in the ternary composite sensor. Moreover, the optimal selectivity window (41%) with neighboring interfering species, as well as the highly stable response, make such a ternary sensor a suitable candidate for real-time monitoring applications.

title: A Multi-Voltage Method for Extracting Multi-Chips Junction Temperature Distributions in Power Electronics
authors: Lixin Wu, Erping Deng, Maoyang Pan, Wenbo Hua, Luhong Xie, Shengqian Xu, Yanhao Wang, Haotian Zhang, Lijian Ding
abstract: As the core of energy conversion, power modules play a critical role in determining the reliability and efficiency of energy systems, thereby exerting a significant impact on the broader energy economy. To address the current limitations wherein existing junction temperature measurement methods fail to obtain the internal junction temperature distribution of multi-chip modules both non-destructively and accurately, our research proposes a Multi-Voltage Method, based on the principle that the voltage across chips connected in parallel remains constant. During the junction temperature measurement process, a set of voltage with different amplitudes—equal in number to the parallel-connected chips—is applied independently. The junction temperature distribution of the module is then obtained by solving a system of nonlinear equations. In comparison with the conventional small-current method, tests were conducted on both single-chip and multi-chip configurations, and the resulting junction temperature deviations were found to be within 3 K and 5 K, respectively. Moreover, since a single chip is composed of numerous unit cells, the proposed method was further extended to extract the temperature distribution within a single chip. When compared with infrared thermographic measurements, the relative error was within 5.8%, and the maximum temperature error was further reduced as the division granularity increased.

title: A Tutorial on Cognitive Biases in Agentic AI-Driven 6G Autonomous Networks
authors: Hatim Chergui, Farhad Rezazadeh, Merouane Debbah, Christos Verikoukis
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Communications Society
abstract: The path to higher network autonomy in 6G lies beyond the mere optimization of key performance indicators (KPIs), requiring systems that perceive and reason over the network environment as it is. This can be achieved through agentic AI, where large language model (LLM)-powered agents utilize multimodal telemetry, memory, and cross-domain negotiation to achieve multi-objective goals. However, deploying such agents introduces cognitive biases inherited from human design, which can severely distort reasoning and actuation. This paper provides a comprehensive tutorial on well-known cognitive biases, detailing their taxonomy, mathematical formulation, emergence in telecom systems, and tailored mitigation strategies. We validate these concepts through two distinct use-cases in 6G management1. First, we tackle anchoring bias in inter-slice resource negotiation. To overcome the prohibitive execution delays of cloud-based LLMs, this use-case deploys a locally hosted 1B-parameter model on an RTX A4000 GPU, successfully achieving sub-second inference latencies compatible with near-real-time (near-RT) operations. By replacing fixed heuristic anchors with a Truncated Weibull randomized anchor strategy, the agents dismantle rigid biases, intelligently consume SLA slack, and dynamically double the system-wide energy savings (peaking at 25%) without violating strict latency limits. Second, we mitigate temporal and confirmation biases in RAN-Edge cross-domain negotiation by designing an unbiased collective memory. By integrating semantic/temporal decay and an inflection bonus that actively highlights past negotiation failures, agents are prevented from over-relying on recent data or repeating past mistakes. Grounding decisions in this richer, debiased historical context yields highly robust agreements, achieving a ×5 latency reduction and roughly 40% higher energy savings compared to memoryless baselines.

title: Computational Intelligence for Social Good: Advancing Cybernetics and Medical Systems
authors: Amir H Gandomi
publish: 2026 IEEE 13th International Joint Conference on Cybernetics and Computational Cybernetics, Cyber-Medical Systems (ICCC)
abstract: Computational Intelligence (CI) plays an increasingly important role in addressing complex societal challenges, particularly in cybernetic and medical systems where adaptability and robustness are essential. This plenary lecture, Computational Intelligence for Social Good: Advancing Cybernetics and Medical Systems, introduces Evolved Machine Intelligence (EMI), a class of biologically inspired CI methods based on evolutionary principles such as variation, selection, and adaptive learning. The talk outlines recent advances in evolutionary intelligence, with emphasis on genetic programming, automated learning, and optimisation in complex, nonlinear environments. Through representative case studies, including many-objective and large-scale optimisation problems, the lecture demonstrates how EMI-based approaches can outperform classical techniques in real-world applications. The session concludes by discussing hybrid CI frameworks that integrate evolutionary methods with complementary techniques, highlighting their potential to deliver robust and socially impactful solutions for cybernetic and medical systems.

title: Dot-Shaped Range-Angle Beampattern Synthesis for Frequency Diverse Array with Linear Frequency Offset_supp1-3692774.xlsx
authors: Xiaolong Xie
abstract: Frequency Diverse Array (FDA) suffers from inherent range–angle coupling, which causes ambiguous target localization and dispersed far-field energy. Existing decoupling approaches rely on nonuniform frequency offsets that are difficult to implement in hardware. In contrast, this letter introduces a feed-to-element random remapping architecture. The method randomly routes readily available linearly incremental frequency offset signals to individual array elements. This random routing strategy shifts the key implementation challenge from signal generation to signal routing. Because the routing function can be realized with mature, low-cost switch matrix technology, the overall system complexity is substantially reduced. The architecture is validated by applying it to measured five-channel data originally acquired from a photonics-based FDA system, confirming dot-shaped range–angle beampattern synthesis under realistic hardware conditions. The approach requires only the addition of a switch matrix to existing linearly incremental frequency offset sources, offering a practical pathway toward engineering implementation of dot-shaped range–angle beampattern synthesis.

title: Whisper in Focus: Parameter-Efficient Stuttering Disfluency Classification
authors: Huma Ameer, Mehwish Fatima, Esam Mohammed Asem Othman, Sana Mukhtar, Nor Shahida Mohd Jamail, Rabia Latif, Seemab Latif
abstract: Stuttering disfluency classification remains a complex challenge in speech processing due to the high variability of disfluency patterns. Transformer-based models such as Wav2Vec2.0 have demonstrated promising results but struggle with generalization across diverse speakers and incur substantial computational costs. In this study, we present a parameter-efficient adaptation of Whisper, an encoder-decoder transformer trained on 680,000 hours of labeled speech-text pairs, for automatic classification of stuttering disfluencies. We introduce three key contributions: (1) a refined SEP-28k dataset with improved quality and speaker diversity, (2) novel layer-freezing strategies within Whisper’s encoder to balance task-specific learning and computational efficiency, and (3) extensive evaluation on the FluencyBank dataset to assess model generalizability. Our experimental results show that Whisper achieves a weighted F1-score of 0.812, outperforming Wav2Vec2.0 and HuBERT while reducing trainable parameters by 46% and training time by 37%. Analysis reveals that deeper encoder layers encode critical task-specific representations necessary for robust disfluency detection.

title: ULL-RA: Unsupervised Learning-Based Location-Aware Random Access for Massive Machine-Type Communications
authors: Lan Lu, Wei Chen, Bo Ai, Yuxuan Sun, Guowei Shi
abstract: Grant-free (GF) random access has emerged as a promising solution for massive machine-type communications (mMTC). However, the non-uniform distribution of user equipment (UE) and real-world limitations on base station (BS) placement lead to random access imbalance among BSs and heavy access collisions in some cells. To this end, an unsupervised learning-based location-aware random access (ULL-RA) scheme is proposed in this paper to select the accessing BSs and channels simultaneously. Specifically, ULL-RA adopts a deep learning model named ULL-RA-Net, comprising parameter-shared feedforward neural network (FFNN) layers that enable each active UE to select a BS and an access channel to maximize the achievable rate. The model is trained in an unsupervised manner to maximize a designed differentiable objective, mapping UE locations to channel access probabilities. Notably, we propose a rate-collision loss tailored to the model architecture, which combines a collision-free channel capacity term and a sparsity-inducing collision penalty term to reduce access collisions and enhance the achievable rate. Experimental results using the Deep-MIMO dataset indicate that ULL-RA outperforms conventional GF access in both the average achievable rate and the access success rate.

title: Spray-Coated Silver as Backside Metal for III–V Photovoltaic Devices on GaAs and Ge Substrates
authors: Thales Borrely, Thien Truong, Theresa E. Saenz, Sarah A. Collins, Julian Wagner, Jennifer G. Selvidge, William E. McMahon, Sneh Sinha, Mitchell Smith, Melbs LeMieux, Brett Walker, Myles A. Steiner
abstract: The accelerated increase in demand for III–V space photovoltaics on GaAs and Ge substrates, as well as growing interests in terrestrial applications, motivate the development of cost-effective, high-throughput processing routes of these materials. Here, we assess spray-coated silver (Ag) back contact metallization as a substitute for electron-beam-evaporated metals currently used in industry. We find that the spray-coated Ag films are dense and continuous. By means of quantum efficiency, dark current–voltage, and illuminated current–voltage characterizations, we show that spray-coated GaAs and Ge solar cells perform similarly to baseline devices with electroplated Au, including under high current densities. We estimate that the thresholds for specific contact resistance below which back contacts do not significantly contribute to resistive loss are <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\bm{2.1\times 10^{-1}\Omega }\cdot$</tex-math></inline-formula>cm<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\bm{^{2}}$</tex-math></inline-formula> for GaAs and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\bm{4.7\times 10^{-2}\Omega }\cdot$</tex-math></inline-formula>cm<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\bm{^{2}}$</tex-math></inline-formula> for Ge. We experimentally confirm that our spray-coated samples meet these requirements. Peel tests show that the adhesion of plain spray-coated Ag films to the back of p-type Ge substrates used in III–V solar cells is currently insufficient, whereas adhesion to p-type GaAs substrates is outstanding and requires no further optimization.

title: A Semantic Digital Twin Framework for Predictive Failure Analytics in IoT
authors: Prem Kumar Ramesh, Koundinya Koorapati, Sairam Veeraswamy, Usha Narasappa
abstract: The proliferation of IoT devices in Software-Defined Data Center (SDDC) environments has made large-scale, real-time failure prediction both critical and complex. Traditional monitoring systems remain reactive, generating disconnected alerts with limited context. In this paper, we present a novel, digital twin-driven framework that unifies semantic modeling, machine learning, and real-time telemetry to deliver intelligent, predictive maintenance for IoT infrastructures. Built upon an extensible ontology derived from the Semantic Sensor Network (SSN) standard and enriched with dynamic battery metadata, our system integrates classification and regression models, such as Random Forest and XGBoost, to forecast device failures and estimate Remaining Useful Life (RUL). These insights are contextually embedded into a live semantic layer, enabling expressive SPARQL queries for impact analysis and proactive fault management. To further democratize access, we integrate a Large Language Model (LLM) interface that translates natural language queries into SPARQL, bridging the gap between technical complexity and user accessibility. Validated on NASA’s battery datasets, our approach demonstrates superior explainability, operational relevance, and scalability—pushing the boundaries of predictive analytics in IoT-SDDC ecosystems. We also showcase case studies with five practical scenarios where our framework can be readily integrated and used.

title: An Emotion - Driven System Offering Personalized Activities and Lifestyle Recommendations
authors: K. Priya, K. Omkar, Shaik Sulthan, M. Kishore
abstract: Emotional context plays a significant role in shaping individual lifestyle preferences, decision making, and daily well being. This presents a multimodal Emotion Based Lifestyle Recommendation System designed to provide adaptive suggestions through facial expressions, speech input, and text interaction. The proposed framework integrates real time facial emotion analysis using DeepFace with audio and text based emotion interpretation to capture user mood across multiple modalities. The system processes camera input, microphone recordings, uploaded audio files, and manual text entries to detect emotional states such as happiness, sadness, anger, fear, surprise, and neutrality, along with extended emotional cues including stress, anxiety, and boredom. Based on the detected emotion, the platform generates personalized recommendations including music playlists, food suggestions, activities, travel ideas, and mental wellness guidance. The application is developed using Streamlit to enable an interactive and responsive user interface. Results demonstrate that combining visual and linguistic emotion signals improves engagement and enables context aware recommendations in real time environments. The proposed architecture highlights the potential of emotion aware computing to support personalized lifestyle assistance and enhance user experience through intelligent adaptive systems.

title: Spatio-Temporal Siamese Ensemble with Multi-Level Feature Fusion for Video-Based Person Re-Identification
authors: Roshni Singh
abstract: Person re-identification (Re-ID) on video datasets plays a vibrant role in surveillance systems but remains challenging due to angle variation, background clutter, occlusion, noisy frames, and inadequate training data. Existing approaches either depend on high-temporal embeddings while neglecting spatial cues or on computationally expensive architectures. To solve these issues, we introduced a spatiotemporal siamese ensemble network that mutually integrates multi-level spatial features, lightweight temporal modeling, and statistical aggregation of all features. The proposed method leverages patch-based, fine-tuned CNN backbones to preserve fine-grained local cues, dual-branch temporal modeling that combines gated recurrent units with statistical mean-variance for robust sequence representation while the multi-model ensemble approach is followed by triplet and pairwise regression losses to enhance discrimination across each angle. The summation-based similarity fusion mechanism further integrates information from multiple temporal and spatial features for reliable identity matching. The extensive experiments conducted on standard benchmarks demonstrate that the proposed spatio-temporal model achieves superior accuracy and computational efficiency compared to state-of-the-art methods, contributing towards real-world video surveillance applications.

title: Construction of Occupational Interest Profiles Based on Deep Learning (CNN-LSTM): Precise Matching Algorithms Integrating Multi-Source Behavioral Data
authors: Mingqian Shi, Zhonglou Lang, Qingqing Chen
abstract: The present-day techniques in creating career interest profiles are troubled with inadequate data sources, inadequate extraction of interest measures, and the low accuracy of matching, which leads to minimal talent recommendation and job matching. In a bid to solve these problems, this paper suggests a model of construction of career interest profile that is founded on Convolutional Neural Network-Long Short-Term Memory (CNN-LSTM) that incorporates the multi-source behavioral data to produce dynamic modeling and precise matching of personal interest. The special processes involve: (1) feature fusion module to encode and vectorize the text and the behavioral data to reflect the multi-dimensional association of features; (2) local interest pattern features are obtained by use of CNNs; (3) the temporal feature of behavioral patterns are obtained by use of LSTM; (4) the multi-dimensional similarity between individual and job profiles is obtained by use of the matching layer. The sample used to validate the experiment was a sample of 20,000 users of an online career learning platform. The results show that the model achieves an accuracy of 92.4 % in the interest classification task, with an overall MAP index of 0.861. The method presented in this paper effectively improves the personalization and dynamic adaptability of career interest profiles, providing new ideas for the refinement and intelligence of career recommendation systems.

title: Learning Gold Futures Price Dynamics Using Attention-Guided Temporal Deep Learning
authors: Akanksha Sharma, Indu Rani, Chandan Kumar Verma
abstract: Gold futures prices are difficult to forecast because they move in a highly nonlinear manner, fluctuate at multiple time scales, and respond quickly to changes in the macroeconomic environment. Many standard forecasting methods can capture a part of this behavior, but they often miss either the shortterm swings, the longer cycles, or the economic signals that push the market. This study proposed a novel forecasting model that combines Variational Mode Denoising with an attentionbased Temporal Convolutional Network and an LSTM layer. The attention-TCN stage then extracts the most useful temporal information by focusing on the most relevant inputs, and the LSTM models the sequential structure to generate the final forecasts. We compare the proposed method with established baselines using RMSE, MAE, and R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> and verify the robustness through 5-fold cross-validation. Finally, SHAP is used to interpret the model and to identify the macroeconomic variables that contribute most to the predictions.

title: Simulation Research of Frequency-Selective Fading Channels for Deterministic Networks
authors: Chen He, Lixiang Ruan, Junxiao Shi, Jianxin Chu, Daojian Hong, Hui Wu
abstract: Driven by the rapid evolution of mobile communication technology, mobile communication systems have seen extensive deployment across various domains, including personal communications, the Internet of Things (IoT) together with intelligent transportation systems. However, due to the complexity of wireless channels, signals are affected by multipath effects, fading, and shadowing during transmission, which severely limits system performance. In urban environments, obstacles for instance, structures like buildings and natural elements such as vegetation can obstruct line-of-sight (LoS) paths, causing signals to primarily reach the receiver through scattering, reflection, and diffraction. This results in complex random signal characteristics and prominent fading phenomena. In order to tackle the crucial problem of frequency - selective fading channels, this research presents a wireless channel. model grounded in the Clarke model framework. The Clarke process assumes that the statistical characteristics of the received signal are primarily determined by scattering effects, which align with the typical features of urban environments where LoS paths are absent. In this model, the signal is represented as the superposition of multiple plane waves arriving via different paths, each with independent amplitude, frequency offset, and random phase. Based on this foundation, the Jakes simulator is applied to calculate the Doppler shift parameters of the channel, and subsequently, a deterministic channel simulation model is formulated. Simulation outcomes indicate that the suggested model accurately replicates the fading behaviors observed in practical communication channels, providing a theoretical foundation for optimizing modulation techniques, error correction coding schemes, and multiple access strategies.

title: GST Smart Filing Assistance for SMES
authors: Deebika R K, Dharshni G, Sunmitha P, Sushithra Devi S R
abstract: The introduction of the Goods and Services Tax (GST) system has created a structured taxation to combine multiple indirect taxes across India. However, this process of filing the GST Returns remains a very time-delayed task, especially for small to medium-sized businesses (SMEs). Many SMEs continue to depend on manual spreadsheets for maintaining their financial records and filing GST returns. The common issue faced by SMEs is the difficulty in understanding GST rules due to Frequent policy updates and dynamic GST rules. Most of the GST filing is done through manual entry which causes problems like data mismatch and errors in calculation. The GST Smart Filing Assistant focuses on automating the process of formatting GST Returns. The system validates the invoice for further processing and maps the data into structured GST formats. The system validation comprises Anomaly Detection uses pattern recognition to identify incorrect invoice entries that may the indicate errors, mismatches, and other potential issues. After the user verification, the GST return reports like GSTR-1, GSTR-3B, GSTR-4 and GSTR-9 are generated based on the category. The generated GST-compatible report allows the user to directly upload to the portal. In conclusion, our system makes the filing process effective in generating accurate reports and efficiently by reducing the complexity with the implementation of AI. The system displays a dashboard enhancing the ease of use by providing users with a clear and real-time overview of their GST activities.

title: Control Co-Design Framework for Joint Optimization of PID Governor and Hydraulic Dynamics for a Hydropower Plant
authors: Bidur Poudel, Sang Won Seo, Daniel R. Herber, Sanjeev Pannala
publish: 2026 IEEE Power and Energy Conference at Illinois (PECI)
abstract: This paper presents a control co-design (CCD) framework tailored to the hydraulic and mechanical subsystems of hydropower systems, aimed at enhancing their dynamic performance and responsiveness to grid demands. Conventional sequential design approaches often fall short in capturing the coupled interactions between plant dynamics and control objectives. The proposed CCD methodology enables simultaneous optimization of key hydro plant parameters—such as governor settings, penstock characteristics, and valve control—with the control system architecture. Results indicate that both CCD and sequential design approaches achieve comparable transient and steady-state performance, with minor discrepancies arising from differences in their optimized parameters. However, CCD demonstrates a distinct advantage by tripling the water time constant (T<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">w</inf>) while maintaining similar dynamic performance to the sequential approach. This increase in T<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">w</inf> enables optimization of penstock dimensions, as it is directly influenced by penstock design, thereby contributing to overall cost optimization.

title: A Hardware-in-the-Loop Platform for Evaluating MQTT-based Intelligent Traffic Light Systems
authors: Maulana Fauzan Athalla Halinda, Endra Joelianto, Faqihza Mukhlish, Herman Sutarto
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Technology, Informatics, Management, Engineering and Environment (TIME-E)
abstract: The validation of adaptive traffic control systems (ATCS) is often confined to software simulations, which overlook performance degradation from real-world network latency and jitter. This research proposes to bridge that gap by developing a high-fidelity hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) platform to evaluate IoT-based ATCS. The platform integrates the SUMO microscopic simulator, a Raspberry Pi edge controller, and ESP32/NodeMCU actuators using an MQTT framework. Three controller architectures were evaluated: (1) Fixed Time as the baseline, (2) adaptive Slotted Max Pressure based on time slots, and (3) adaptive Cycle-based Max Pressure based on event-driven logic. The experimental results revealed a critical trade-off: the Fixed Time controller failed to manage congestion, marked by uncontrolled vehicle accumulation. The Slotted MP controller successfully managed congestion but at the cost of extremely high end-to-end (E2E) latency (~1440 ms) and massive jitter (~3153 ms std. dev.). The Cycle-based MP controller emerged as the optimal solution, successfully managing congestion while maintaining low and stable E2E latency (~414 ms), proving its viability for real-world implementation.

title: Evaluating Cycle and Non-Cycle Traffic Control Approaches in Intersection Networks Under Sensor Cyber Attacks
authors: Gabriel Francesco Hostka, I Putu Agus Ananda Giri Putra, Endra Joelianto, Faqihza Mukhlish, Herman Y. Sutarto
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Technology, Informatics, Management, Engineering and Environment (TIME-E)
abstract: This paper develops a traffic signal control method using reinforcement learning to improve efficiency and resilience in Surabaya's traffic system, which currently relies on fixed-time control due to sensor failures in its previous adaptive system. Two approaches are proposed: cycle-based and non-cycle-based control, implemented using conventional algorithms and a Deep Q-Learning Network (DQN).
