title: A Novel Sliding Mode Differentiator-Based Feature for EMG-Based Hand Gesture Characterization
authors: Frank Kulwa, Tolulope T. Oyemakinde, Pengrui Tai, Xiaoqian Deng, Doreen S Sarwatt, Jiaming Huang, Oluwarotimi W Samuel, Xiangxin Li, Guanglin Li, Hai Li, Yongcheng Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Systems and Rehabilitation Engineering
abstract: Motor intent (MI)-based muscle computer interfaces (MCIs) have been widely explored for prosthetic control as a means of restoring functionality to the lost limb in amputees. However, persistent issues remain, such as insufficient robustness of the current features, the presence of coherent noise, and the low spatial resolution of electromyography (EMG) sensors. Subsequently, these results in decreased movement characterization performance. Therefore, this study introduces a novel feature extraction technique that utilizes a Sliding Mode Differentiator (SMD) to extract unique patterns from EMG signals, followed by the deployment of symmetric positive definite matrices (SPD) to efficiently leverage the spatial-temporal properties of the EMG signal. The average classification results of 98.7±3.0% and 97.9±5.2% for 21 able-bodied subjects and 15 amputees respectively, suggests an improvement in accuracy for characterizing 13 hand gestures, thereby outperforming other state-of-the-art feature methods. Further, the channel optimization analysis shows that the number of channels can be reduced by 75% (from 24 channels to 6 channels) without compromising the performance of the proposed technique. This justifies the potential of the proposed technique in both high-density and sparse-density EMG electrode configurations. Additional analysis of the performance of the techniques in the presence of noise indicates that the proposed method can significantly outperform other methods. Therefore, the findings of this study have the potential to significantly improve the control performance of prostheses, rehabilitation assistive robots, and hand gesture-related games.

title: Automatic Cattle BCS Estimation Using Point Cloud Surface Features from a Single Top-View ToF RGB-D Camera
authors: Pyae Phyo Kyaw, Thi Thi Zin, Masaru Aikawa, Ikuo Kobayashi, Pyke Tin
abstract: Body Condition Score (BCS) is a key indicator of nutritional status, productivity, and reproductive performance in dairy cows; however, manual assessment is subjective, labor-intensive, and unsuitable for continuous monitoring. This study proposes an automated and contactless BCS estimation framework using a single top-view time-of-flight (ToF) RGB-D camera and 3D dorsal region point cloud analysis. The cow back surface is reconstructed into a point cloud, and both edge-based geometric features and Fast Point Feature Histograms (FPFH) are integrated into a modified Dynamic Graph Convolutional Neural Network (DGCNN) for robust learning of fat-related surface patterns. By combining handcrafted 3D descriptors with graph-based deep feature learning, the proposed method significantly improves strict BCS matching performance. Experiments conducted on 95 Holstein cows across two commercial farms achieve accuracies of 69.2% at tolerance 0.0 and 94.85% at tolerance 0.25, outperforming existing 2D and depth-based approaches. The system enables scalable, objective, and non-invasive BCS monitoring, supporting proactive health interventions, optimizing feed efficiency, and enhancing transition-cow management in precision dairy farming.

title: Spatially Consistent RIS-Aided Multi-User Communications: Channel Modeling and Analysis
authors: Yuan Yuan, Ruisi He, Bo Ai, Ruifeng Chen, Zhengyu Zhang, Zhuoyin Li, Zhicheng Qiu
abstract: In this paper, a 3D geometrical cluster-based channel model is developed for reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS)-assisted multi-user communication systems. Herein, spatial consistency of multi-user channels is considered to guarantee smooth evolution of large-scale (i.e. Ricean K-factor, shadow fading, and number of clusters) and small-scale (i.e. distance and angle of multipath components) channel parameters for base station (BS)-receiver (Rx) and BS-RIS-Rx links of different users. Two different optimization objectives for multi-user channels are presented, namely, maximizing sum of channel gains (O1) and maximizing channel capacity (O2). Then, projected gradient ascent algorithm is applied to solve RIS phase shift matrix under both optimization objectives. Furthermore, some channel statistical characteristics, namely, correlation coefficient, singular value spread, channel capacity, and root mean square (RMS) delay spread are derived and analyzed. At the same time, impacts of solved RIS phase matrices under different optimization objectives, as well as locations of RIS and users, on channel characteristics are compared and investigated. Simulation results demonstrate that the role of RIS under O1 is to align path phases of BS-RIS-Rx link with those of BS-Rx link as much as possible, whereas under O2, the RIS aims to enhance power while reducing user channel correlation. In addition, solved RIS phase under O2 can increase channel capacity and reduce channel correlation between different BS antennas, whereas O1 leads to a reduction in channel capacity. Location of RIS is also found to affect channel response impulse amplitude of different users. Furthermore, accuracy of the proposed model is verified by comparing RMS delay spread with measured data. These observations will provide a foundation for developing reconstructed wireless propagation environment of future multi-user communication systems.

title: Multi-UAV Collaborative ISAC with Dynamic Resource Allocation: A Hierarchical Graph Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning Approach
authors: Jinyu Wang, Xianchao Zhang, Zhiqing Wei, Fengsong Sun, Yiheng Li, Zhiyong Feng, Jun Lu
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Signal Processing
abstract: Integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) enables low-altitude wireless networks (LAWN) to operate in complex 3-D environments. However, single unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) architectures suffer from limited sensing coverage and communication throughput, and multi-UAV collaborative systems face substantial challenges in resource coordination. To address these challenges, we propose a multi-UAV collaborative ISAC framework for dynamic LAWNs. First, synthetic aperture processing enhances range resolution via bandwidth utilization on dedicated sensing subcarriers. A multi-objective optimization model is then formulated to jointly minimize the Cramér-Rao lower bound (CRLB) for positioning and maximize communication throughput. To efficiently evaluate collaborative positioning performance, the equivalent Fisher information matrix (EFIM) is derived, enabling dimensionality reduction by eliminating per-UAV Fisher information matrix (FIM) computations. Furthermore, a hierarchical graph-based multi-agent reinforcement learning (HGMRL) approach is developed using graph neural networks (GNNs). In this framework, a coordinating agent manages global bandwidth allocation and user association, while distributed UAV agents collaboratively optimize subcarrier selection, power control, and trajectory planning. Simulations demonstrate that HGMRL adapts effectively to LAWN topological evolution, achieving dynamic trade-offs between sensing accuracy and communication performance.

title: Adaptive Observer-Based Resilient Containment of Multi-Agent Systems With Hybrid Cyber-Attacks
authors: Yanpeng Shi, Jiangping Hu, Yanzhi Wu, Lei Shi
abstract: This study investigates the resilient containment problem in linear multi-agent systems, specifically focusing denial-of-service (DOS) attacks of the communication network and deception attacks of the sensors. In this case, a novel network framework is introduced to analyze the intricate nature of the communication network with hybrid attacks. By modifying the coupling relationships, a novel containment error is obtained to accomplish the containment objective, guaranteeing the prescribed trajectory within a safe region. Firstly, utilizing an average dwell-interval condition to handle the intermittent communication problem, a distributed hybrid communication scheme under DOS attacks is developed. Secondly, regarding the unknown sensor attacks, an adaptive dynamic observer is designed for each agent to estimate the external unknown attack signals. Subsequently, an adaptive distributed hybrid controller is proposed to achieve resilient containment, ensuring the uniform ultimate boundedness. In addition, by tackling the intermittent communication with non-periodic intervals, a novel criterion of the exponential stability is derived, which leads to a non-periodic intermittent DOS attack rate. By the simulation example, the effectiveness is demonstrated for the developed adaptive observer-based hybrid controller, showcasing its ability to overcome the challenges posed by DOS and sensor attacks.

title: A Label-Efficient Semi-Supervised Learning Approach for Fine-Grained Voltage Sag Classification Using FreeMatch and Vision Transformers
authors: Guhankumar T, Sreshtamol K G, Rahul Satheesh, Pavan Penkey, Vamsi Chitturi
publish: 2026 IEEE Texas Power and Energy Conference (TPEC)
abstract: Voltage sags (dips) are transient reductions in RMS voltage <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\mathbf{1 0 - 9 0 \%}$</tex> of nominal for <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{0. 5}$</tex> cycles to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1}$</tex> minute), represent one of the most critical power quality disturbances affecting industrial operations, causing equipment misoperation and significant economic losses. Accurate classification of voltage sags into the seven IEEE 1159 types (A-G) is essential for precise fault diagnosis, root cause analysis, and targeted mitigation strategies. However, traditional supervised learning approaches for voltage sag classification require extensive labeled datasets, which are expensive and time-consuming to obtain, limiting practical deployment in real world power systems. This paper introduces a semi-supervised learning framework combining FreeMatch with Vision Transformer architecture for label efficient voltage sag classification. By employing adaptive confidence thresholding and self adaptive fairness mechanisms, our approach effectively leverages unlabeled voltage sag waveform data while preventing class imbalance. Using synthetic three phase waveforms following IEEE 1159 and IEEE 1564 specifications, the method achieves competitive 84.14% accuracy with only 15% labeled data, approaching fully supervised performance while dramatically reducing annotation costs by 85%. This represents the first application of FreeMatch to voltage sag classification, specifically, demonstrating that advanced semi-supervised techniques enable practical deployment of fine-grained sag type identification in resource constrained industrial environments where expert labeling of power quality events remains a persistent bottleneck.

title: Plasmonic Optical DAC for Coherent PAM and IM/DD ASK Transmission
authors: Samuel Hess, Daniel Rieben, Konstantinos Moschopoulos, Chenrui Xu, Marcel Destraz, Wolfgang Heni, Moshe Nazarathy, Ioannis Tomkos, David Moor, Juerg Leuthold
abstract: Optical digital-to-analog converters (DAC) offer power-efficient amplitude multiplexing in the optical domain. They allow simplification to complex electronic circuits as the binary signals are directly fed to the Mach-Zehnder modulators (MZM). Drive circuits with a large effective number of bits (ENOB) and complex power-hungry electronic predistortion of the MZMs nonlinear transfer characteristic are eliminated. This results in a power advantage and smaller electronics, ultimately delivering an improved signal-to-noise and distortion ratio (SNDR). As oDACs can be operated by means of direct digital drives (DDD) with minimal signal processing, they are ideal for highest speed operation. When implemented with terahertz-bandwidth plasmonic modulators, they may provide highest symbol rates on a small footprint with the least power consumption. We demonstrate the first plasmonic oDAC implementation for IM/DD and coherent transmission, operating at up to 420 Gb/s line rate without SNR penalty. We show energy-efficient amplitude multiplexing in the optical domain at symbol rates up to 180 GBd.

title: Multi-Population Ensemble Genetic Programming via Cooperative Coevolution and Multi-View Learning for Classification_supp1-3668624.pdf
authors: Amir H. Gandomi
abstract: This paper introduces Multi-population Ensemble Genetic Programming (MEGP), a computational intelligence framework that integrates cooperative coevolution and the multi-view learning paradigm to address classification challenges in high-dimensional and heterogeneous feature spaces. MEGP decomposes the input space into conditionally independent feature subsets, enabling multiple subpopulations to evolve in parallel while interacting through a dynamic ensemble-based fitness mechanism. Each individual encodes multiple genes whose outputs are aggregated via a differentiable softmax-based weighting layer, enhancing both model interpretability and adaptive decision fusion. A hybrid selection mechanism incorporating both isolated and ensemble-level fitness promotes inter-population cooperation while preserving intra-population diversity. This dual-level evolutionary dynamic facilitates structured search exploration and reduces premature convergence. Experimental evaluations across eight benchmark datasets show that MEGP consistently outperforms a baseline GP model in fitness, diversity, and generalization, although convergence-rate and runtime results are more mixed. Comprehensive statistical analyses validate significant improvements in Log-Loss, Precision, Recall, <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$F_{1}$</tex-math></inline-formula> score, and AUC. MEGP also exhibits stronger diversity retention and improved fitness progression, although the current implementation increases runtime and is evaluated with two views only. By unifying population-based optimization, multi-view representation learning, and cooperative coevolution, MEGP contributes a structurally adaptive and interpretable framework that advances emerging directions in evolutionary machine learning.

title: Enhancing Oxide Film Thickness Measurement Accuracy via Temperature Drift Compensation With GAN-Augmented Data
authors: Jiarui Feng, Chaofeng Ye, Ming Li, Guozheng Zhou, Jingqi Jiao
abstract: Accurate measurement of oxide film thickness on nuclear fuel cladding is critical for ensuring reactor safety, as excessive oxidation can degrade thermal conductivity and increase the risk of structural failure. Eddy current testing (ECT) is the predominant technology for measuring oxide film thickness due to its high sensitivity and suitability for in-situ inspections. However, the presence of temperature fluctuations during measurement introduces drift in the ECT signal, compromising its accuracy. This article proposes a deep learning-based framework for temperature drift compensation. A generative adversarial network is used to augment limited experimental datasets by synthesizing physically consistent temperature drift profiles. These augmented datasets are then used to train a random forest (RF) model that performs nonlinear compensation of the ECT signal. A correction approach is developed to mitigate the effects of temperature measurement delay, enhancing compensation performance. Experimental validation demonstrates that the proposed method reduces the maximum measurement error from 28.38 μm to 0.98 μm and the average error from 26.76 μm to 0.62 μm. Repeated trials confirm the robustness and reliability of the method. The study provides an innovative technique for accurate measurement of oxide film thickness on nuclear fuel cladding, which can also be extended to other small-sample sensing tasks.

title: SageIQ: Scene-Graph-Guided Blind Image Quality Assessment
authors: Renwei Yang, Zhengjie Yang, Yun Wang, Dapeng Oliver Wu, Shiqi Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Emerging Topics in Computational Intelligence
abstract: Conventional Blind Image Quality Assessment (BIQA) methods typically assess the entire image quality, which is suboptimal for tasks like autonomous driving that concern specific Task-Aligned Region (TAR). Moreover, we observe that advanced Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM)-based BIQA models exhibit bias when evaluating small regions, leading to inaccurate perceptual judgments. To address these issues, we propose SageIQ (Scene-graph-guided Evaluation for Image Quality), a pipeline SageIQ-P for TAR localization, an approach consisting of an MLLM-based BIQA model SageIQ-M, and a dataset SageIQ-D. SageIQ-P is designed to automatically identify and evaluate TARs, with the advantages of being training-free and allowing plug-in integration of off-the-shelf BIQA models without retraining. It operates in three stages: scenegraphbased triplet construction, LLMdriven triplet analysis, and integration of weighted BIQA scores into a final assessment. Since SageIQ-P can produce small-sized TAR crops that may encounter small-region scoring bias in existing BIQA models, we propose SageIQ-M to alleviate this bias by injecting scale information through scale-aware images and size-prompted cues, achieving size awareness across both visual and textual modalities. In addition, we develop a fully automated approach to construct a region-level test set SageIQ-D, significantly reducing the human effort needed. Experimental results demonstrate that our methods achieve superior BIQA performance.

title: Physics-Informed Unsupervised Learning for Denial-of-Service Attack Detection in Load Frequency Control of Multi-Area Power Systems
authors: Kouhyar Sheida, Mehdi Farasat
publish: 2026 IEEE Texas Power and Energy Conference (TPEC)
abstract: The growing interconnectedness of power grids using sophisticated information and communication technology has led to efficiency gains but also created new cyber threats. Denial-of-Service (DoS) attacks, in particular, pose a severe risk to multi-area load frequency control (LFC) systems, as they can interfere with communication between key control variables. This paper proposes a hybrid physics-informed approach using an <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$H_{\infty}$</tex> observer model along with a Long Short-Term Memory Autoencoder (LSTM-AE) neural network as an independent model for the detection of DoS attacks in LFC systems without the use of any attack training data. The proposed approach employs the Gilbert-Elliott Markov model to simulate stochastic attacks of varying intensities and durations. Results show that the proposed framework achieves the highest detection accuracy, reaching an Area Under the Curve (AUC) of 0.94, compared to 0.89 for the Standard Autoencoder and 0.86 for the One-Class Support Vector Machine, confirming its superiority in detecting DoS attacks in LFC systems.

title: Multi-User Covert Communications via Intelligent Spectrum Control
authors: Yujie Ling, Zan Li, Lei Guan, Zheng Zhang
abstract: This letter investigates multi-user covert communications. Specifically, we propose an intelligent spectrum control (ISC) scheme that employs spectrum sensing-assisted dynamic control to generate time-frequency resource occupation matrices for multiple legitimate users. Guided by these matrices, the users can occupy different frequency slots irregularly for covert data transmission. By performing spectrum sensing in advance, the proposed scheme can also proactively avoid external interference and co-channel collisions among users. In addition, we consider an eavesdropper with wideband detection capability and derive the closed-form expressions for the minimum detection error probability (DEP) of the eavesdropper and the reliable transmission probability (RTP) of the legitimate users. We then optimize the transmit power to maximize the covert rate (CR), and further characterize the maximum number of legitimate users that can access the network covertly and concurrently. Simulation results demonstrate the superiority of the proposed ISC scheme in terms of covertness, reliability, and user capacity.

title: Cascading Failure Model for Power Systems with Large-Scale Data Center Load
authors: Shuchen Huang, Wei Gao, Feng Qiu, Junjian Qi
publish: 2026 IEEE Texas Power and Energy Conference (TPEC)
abstract: This paper develops a cascading failure simulation model to assess the risks of rapidly expanding large-scale data centers, modeling the load characteristics of data centers and their four operation modes. In the proposed cascading failure model, the impact of voltage disturbances on data center loads is approximated based on the voltage magnitude at data center buses and their electrical distances. The simulation also considers under-frequency load shedding (UFLS) and generator tripping triggered by frequency deviations caused by intense load changes in data centers. In addition, the proposed model includes line outages, wind turbine tripping, under-voltage load shedding (UVLS), and operator re-dispatch to generate realistic outages. Test results on the modified IEEE RTS-96 system show that data centers can increase cascading failure risks by causing more severe outages and load shedding, while ramp-rate limits help mitigate their adverse effects.

title: Application of Industrial Robot Drawing Function Based on RobotStudio Virtual-Physical Integration
authors: Yuezhu Mo, Xiaoping Xiao, Hongyu Ding, Faji He
publish: 2025 International Conference on Intelligent Manufacturing, Robotics and Automation (IMRA)
abstract: To meet the teaching progression requirements of higher education institutions’ industrial robot application and practice courses—where operable projects need to advance from simple to complex in a step-by-step manner—this paper takes the industrial robot drawing workstation as its research subject. Integrating campus culture and using the school emblem pattern as the starting point, it constructs a robot drawing workstation on the RobotStudio virtual simulation platform by leveraging AutoCAD software and SolidWorks three-dimensional modeling tools. Through creating dynamic Smart components for the drawing workstation, generating and optimizing graphic programming paths, offline programming and simulation debugging of the drawing function are achieved. Ultimately, the 1:1 replication and verification of the pattern were achieved on the actual robot drawing workstation, aligning with the virtual workstation. This scheme combines classroom teaching with engineering practice, enabling students to experience both virtual and real operation environments of industrial robots, thereby effectively enhancing learning outcomes and teaching quality.

title: Multi-Objective Optimization Framework for the Design of Wireless Power Transfer Systems
authors: Lidya M. Weldehawaryat, Subho Mukherjee, Wajiha Shireen, Nishanth Gadiyar
publish: 2026 IEEE Texas Power and Energy Conference (TPEC)
abstract: Maximizing the power density and efficiency of resonant wireless power transfer (WPT) systems is beneficial across multiple applications. The selection of optimal compensation network parameters to minimize the Volt-Ampere (VA) rating and thus maximize power density and efficiency of a WPT system by minimizing the competing objectives of coil currents, series capacitor voltages and total energy stored in the compensation network and coupled coils is non-trivial. This paper investigates WPT system design as an optimization problem. First, a multiobjective framework is developed to explore the design space of WPT systems and identify optimal designs. Next, the developed framework is used to explore the design space of a 100 kW wireless electric vehicle (EV) charger using LCC-CL and LCC<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$C C L$</tex> resonant tank topologies. The results show that the LCC<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$C C L$</tex> topology can lower the energy stored in the compensation network components by up to 63 % compared to the LCC-CL topology demonstrating the effectiveness of this framework to identify high-performance designs. Finally, an optimal design is selected and it's performance is verified through numerical simulations.

title: A Topography-Constrained Marine Gravity Field Modeling Method for Gravity Matching-Aided Navigation
authors: Yu Han, Fangjun Qin, Kailong Li, Yun Xiao, Tiangao Zhu
abstract: Gravity matching-aided navigation (GMAN) has become a critical technology for autonomous underwater navigation due to its high precision, strong autonomy, and long endurance. Significant short-wavelength signal attenuation in existing global marine gravity models leads to a fundamental mismatch with high-sensitivity gravity sensors, severely limiting high-precision GMAN applications. This paper proposes a topography-constrained multi-quadric (TMQ) optimization method for high-precision gravity field reference map construction. By exploiting the physical correlation between seafloor topography and gravity anomalies, the method recovers short-wavelength signals through topographic forward modeling in the frequency domain, and integrates topographic complexity as a physical constraint into multi-quadric functions for fusing sparse shipborne gravity data in the spatial domain. Experimental results show an overall accuracy improvement of 44.33% compared with EGM2008, reaching 51.84% in topographically complex regions. Gravity matching navigation experiments demonstrate that the gravity field reference map enhances system observability. The RMSE of the horizontal position error for GMAN-TMQ is reduced by approximately 45.42% to 65.60% compared to GMAN-EGM2008. The incorporation of physical constraints effectively improves both the accuracy of gravity field modeling and the performance of gravity matching-aided navigation for autonomous underwater navigation.

title: Adaptive-Based Control of a Step-Down Non-Isolated DC-DC Converter with High Reduction Ratio for Microgrid Applications
authors: Diego Langarica-Cordoba, Christopher J. Rodriguez-Cortes, Panfilo R. Martinez-Rodriguez, Gerardo Vazquez, Angel Hernandez-Gomez, Juan Antonio Villanueva-Loredo
publish: 2026 IEEE Texas Power and Energy Conference (TPEC)
abstract: This work presents a nonlinear control strategy for a high-step-down, non-isolated DC-DC converter designed for microgrid applications. The proposed multistage DC-DC buck converter achieves a significantly higher reduction ratio than conventional step-down converters, due to the concept of switched inductance implemented through a generalized <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$n$</tex>-cell configuration. The main goal of the controller is to ensure a stable output voltage under varying load and reference conditions by enforcing accurate current regulation over the converter inductors. The design employs two feedback loops: an outer loop that defines the current reference from the output voltage through a proportional-integral regulator, and an inner loop that enforces current tracking by exploiting the system passive properties. To enhance the robustness of the inner current regulation, an immersion and invariance technique is adopted for load estimation. Numerical simulation results validate the proposed approach, confirming its ability to handle load and voltage reference changes while preserving a well-regulated DC output voltage.

title: Analytical Relationship Between GFL Active-Power Step-Response Rise Time and Dynamic-Load Ramp-Rate Compensation
authors: Ahmad F. Abdelhadi, Simon Y. Foo
publish: 2026 IEEE Texas Power and Energy Conference (TPEC)
abstract: Dynamic load fluctuations in modern grids-especially from data centers, industrial processes, and other rapidly varying loads-require inverters to provide effective ramp-rate compensation at the point of common coupling. Grid-following (GFL) inverters equipped with battery energy storage systems (BESS) are widely considered the most practical solution for these grid-tied applications due to their fast active-power response and simple control structure. However, inverter capability is typically specified using active-power step-response rise time <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(10-90 \%)$</tex>, while actual grid disturbances occur as finite-duration ramps, creating a disconnect between how products are benchmarked and how dynamic load support is realized in practice. This paper addresses that gap by deriving a compact analytical relationship between GFL inverter rise time and ramp-rate compensation performance. By identifying the dominant contributors to rise time-including the outer-loop PI controller gains and the effective first-order plant dynamics-the paper formulates closed-form expressions linking (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$K_{p}, K_{i}$</tex>) and the measured rise time <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$t_{r}$</tex> to the fraction of a load ramp that is transmitted to the upstream grid. The resulting framework enables direct estimation of an inverter's smoothing (compensation) capability solely from its step-response characteristics, providing a practical engineering tool for benchmarking commercial GFL inverters and tuning control loops to meet dynamic-load support requirements.

title: Low-Iterative Fault Location Method for IBR-Connected Transmission Lines Considering System Nonhomogeneity
authors: Mahdi Ghotbi-Maleki, Majid Sanaye-Pasand
abstract: Novel fault location methods have relied on the assumption of system homogeneity, which becomes increasingly invalid in different scenarios, e.g., high-resistance faults. On the other hand, the IEEE 2800-2022 standard, addressing the interconnection of inverter-based resources (IBRs) to transmission networks, recommends the injection of negative sequence reactive current as a low-voltage ride-through requirement. However, adherence to this standard does not inherently ensure that IBRs exhibit behavior identical to that of traditional synchronous generators, to enhance the performance of fault location methods in the presence of IBRs. This article presents an enhanced local-based fault location method designed for lines connecting IBRs, especially suitable for nonhomogeneous networks. The proposed method effectively addresses challenges posed by high-resistance faults and remote-infeed effects, without relying on the assumption of system homogeneity. Moreover, the formulated methodology enhances fault location accuracy using a low-iterative procedure. The proposed method for two-phase-to-ground faults requires the zero sequence impedance of the remote-side network. This article introduces a novel method for estimating this impedance utilizing locally measured quantities obtained during the fault. Simulation results demonstrate a maximum calculated discrepancy of 0.10% in fault location estimation for both symmetric and asymmetric high-resistance faults, underscoring the robustness and precision of the proposed method.

title: Towards the Connection between Activation Sparsity and Flat Minima_supp1-3691379.pdf
authors: Yinghuan Shi
abstract: The observation that activation sparsity emerges in MLP blocks of standardly trained Transformers offers an opportunity to drastically reduce computation costs without sacrificing performance. To theoretically explain this phenomenon, existing works have shown that activation sparsity does not result from the data properties or data fitting but from the implicit bias of the training process. However, these connections are obtained with strong assumptions (e.g., shallow networks, a small number of training steps, and special training techniques), which cannot be applied to deep models standardly trained with a large number of steps. Different from these works, we find that the flatness of loss landscapes is also closely related to the MLP activation sparsity and can serve as a weaker assumption because it naturally emerges in the standard training of deep networks without the above strong assumptions. Specifically, we find that 1) the MLP activation sparsity equals a ratio between “augmented f latness” (a weighted sum of flatness measures) and the product of the input norm and activation gradient of the MLP. We empirically find that this ratio decreases during training, leading to sparse activations. 2) We also propose the notion of derivative sparsity, which reduces to activation sparsity under ReLU, but further enables pruning in the backward propagation and is more stable than activation sparsity. With the theoretical findings, we can further encourage activation sparsity by decreasing the numerator and increasing the denominator of the ratio: 1) To improve (lower) the flatness, we add different bias vectors to input tokens of MLP blocks to strengthen stochastic gradient noises that drive the model to a flat area. 2) We restrict the lower bound of affine parameters in LayerNorm to increase the input norm of MLPs. 3) To increase the activation sparsity, we propose an activation function JSReLU to encourage the search of parameters with sparse derivatives and sparse activations. These plug-and-play modifications can effectively reduce the ratio and produce sparser activations. Experiments on ImageNet-1K and C4 demonstrate relative improvements of at least 36% on inference sparsity and at least 50% on training sparsity over vanilla Transformers, indicating further potential cost reduction in both inference and training.

title: A Reconfigurable eHISL Phase Shifter With Transmission and Dual-Reflection Modes
authors: Jin Wu, Yuefeng Hou, Tingxue Yu, Chenlin Guo, Yang Yuan, Tao Chen, Daodan Deng, Kaixue Ma
publish: IEEE Microwave and Wireless Technology Letters
abstract: This letter presents a novel reconfigurable phase shifter with transmission and dual-reflection modes based on enhanced hybrid integrated suspended line (eHISL) technology. The proposed phase shifter comprises a reconfigurable coupler with loads connected to its through and coupled ports. First, a novel reconfigurable phase shifter topology is proposed with detailed analysis, enabling electronic switching between transmission mode and dual-reflection mode. In dual-reflection mode, high isolation between the two ports enables independent control of their reflection phases. Second, to reduce insertion loss (IL), an air-dielectric-based eHISL structure is adopted in the design. Third, the parallel <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$L$</tex-math> </inline-formula>–<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$C$</tex-math> </inline-formula> dual-resonant loads are employed to achieve 7-bit 360° phase shift in both transmission and dual-reflection modes. Finally, the proposed phase shifter operating at 2.45 GHz is designed, fabricated, and measured. In transmission mode, it achieves a full phase shift range (PSR) with an IL of 1.42–2.20 dB. In dual-reflection mode, both the two ports achieve PSRs of 360° and return losses of 0.73–1.36 dB and 0.77–1.31dB, respectively, while maintaining an isolation better than 19dB between the two ports. The proposed reconfigurable phase shifter offers a solution for constructing full-coverage, high-gain, and high-precision reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (RISs).

title: Iterative Reconstruction Performance on the NeuroEXPLORER: Evaluation with Static and Dynamic Human Imaging
authors: Yifan Hu, Jiazhen Zhang, Jean-Dominique Gallezot, Tommaso Volpi, Tianyi Zeng, Takuya Toyonaga, Richard E. Carson
publish: IEEE Transactions on Radiation and Plasma Medical Sciences
abstract: The NeuroEXPLORER (NX) is a next-generation, brain-dedicated PET scanner offering 10× higher sensitivity compared to the HRRT and < 2 mm spatial resolution. This work evaluated the impact of iterative reconstruction on the quantitative performance of NX using human <sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">18</sup>F-SynVesT-1 and <sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">11</sup>C-PHNO data with an injection dose of 167.2 ± 13.6 MBq and 275.7 ± 64.8 MBq, respectively. Both 100% count level and 20% down-sampled count level were evaluated. Images were reconstructed with ordered-subsets expectation maximization incorporated PSF and TOF (PSF-TOF-OSEM) using 5 subsets and up to 20 iterations. Quantitative performance of static imaging was evaluated by computing bias and noise from statistically independent list-mode replicates. Simulations incorporating iteration-dependent bias and noise of each dynamic frame were used to evaluate effects on kinetic parameters. In static imaging, 5 subsets × 10 iterations achieved ROI bias < 10% and noise < 1% for <sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">18</sup>F-SynVesT-1, and bias < 10% with noise < 10% for <sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">11</sup>C-PHNO. In dynamic imaging simulation, with 5 subsets and 15 iterations, kinetic parameter bias remained < 20% and noise < 5%. The results demonstrate quantitative stability and precision of NX across tracers and count levels.

title: Effects of High Penetration of Wind Energy on System Small-Signal Stability: A Case Study
authors: Alexandra M. Hopps-McDaniel, Dongliang Duan
publish: 2026 IEEE Texas Power and Energy Conference (TPEC)
abstract: Renewable energy sources (RES) are increasingly replacing conventional synchronous generators, which is expected to continue over then next decade. However, given the lack of reactive power support and minimal supply of inertia, the full impact of power systems with high levels of RES penetration is unknown. As a case study, this paper explores the impact on the IEEE 39-bus system as conventional generation is gradually replaced with wind energy at penetration levels ranging from <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\approx 4$</tex> to 20%. Power simulation studies provide network information during and after a single line fault which is then used to estimate the electromechanical modes of oscillation (EMO). Comparing the EMO for various simulations supports ongoing research that RES can negatively impact the small-signal stability of a power system. Our analysis show that RES penetration of above 15% would significantly modify the small-signal stability characteristics of the network.

title: DDPG-Based Beamforming Optimization for Optical Phased Arrays
authors: Dongyang Jiang, Jia Ye, Jiongbin Deng, Xiao Yu, Ningyuan Zhong, Yue Zhu, Lianshan Yan
abstract: To address the limited beam optimization capability of conventional methods in optical phased arrays, this paper proposes a beam optimization method based on the deep deterministic policy gradient (DDPG) algorithm. The proposed method adaptively adjusts the excitation amplitudes of array elements to optimize the radiation patterns under different beam steering angles. Simulation and experimental results show that, compared with the Hamming window method, the proposed approach achieves superior beam control and overall optimization performance at scanning angles of 0°, 15°, 30°, and 45°. These results demonstrate that deep reinforcement learning provides an effective solution for beam optimization in optical phased arrays and has promising potential for engineering applications.

title: Continual Test-Time Adaptation via Low-Frequency Modulation
authors: Boyuan Zhang, Jie Pan, Shuai Yang, Lichuan Gu
abstract: Continuous Test-Time Adaptation (CTTA) aims to adapt a pre-trained model to continuously evolving unlabeled target domain. Recent advances in this field predominantly adopt pseudo-label–guided self-supervised learning under teacher–student paradigms. However, under dynamic distribution shifts, such methods often suffer from noisy pseudo-labels, which leads to error accumulation and catastrophic forgetting. To alleviate this issue, we propose a novel Low-Frequency Modulation (LFM) algorithm for CTTA. Specifically, we introduce a low-frequency-guided sample selection strategy that filters out samples dominated by high-frequency components, guided by posterior prediction discrepancy. To further enrich the target distribution and reduce the model’s reliance on high-frequency cues, we employ a cross-class mixed-frequency augmentation method that selectively injects high-frequency components from other classes while preserving low-frequency content. In addition, we introduce a frequency-aware domain knowledge allocation strategy, which dynamically distributes domain-specific and domain-shared knowledge to maintain model stability in continuously evolving environments. Extensive experiments on four widely used benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of LFM in both classification and segmentation CTTA tasks.

title: In-Band Network Telemetry for End-to-End Service Performance Monitoring in Broadband Networks: A Survey and Deployment Feasibility Analysis
authors: Tanzeela Altaf, Mehran Abolhasan, Raymond Owen, Ingrid Barton, Thedin Guruge, Daniel R. Franklin
abstract: End-to-end performance monitoring is essential for ensuring service reliability and operational visibility in modern telecommunications networks. Traditional monitoring techniques often rely on out-of-band measurements, which provide limited visibility into real-time packet behavior across complex network infrastructures. In-band telemetry has emerged as a promising approach that enables performance information to be collected directly from packets as they traverse the network. This article presents a comprehensive survey of in-band telemetry techniques and evaluates their applicability to end-to-end performance monitoring in broadband networks. The survey first examines header stamping approaches, including mechanisms such as In-band Network Telemetry (INT), In-situ Operations, Administration, and Maintenance (IOAM), and related implementations. It then reviews packet marking and selective sampling techniques that aim to reduce measurement overhead while maintaining monitoring accuracy. Hybrid approaches that combine multiple telemetry mechanisms are also discussed. Building on this taxonomy, the paper analyzes the feasibility of deploying in-band telemetry across multi-segment telecommunications environments using broadband services as a representative use case. The analysis highlights several limitations in current mechanisms, including the absence of standardized Layer-2 telemetry support, the assumption of single-domain deployment, and the lack of mechanisms for sharing telemetry information across adjacent network segments. Finally, the paper identifies key open challenges and research directions required to enable scalable, interoperable, and operationally viable end-to-end telemetry solutions for future broadband networks.

title: Evaluating Transfer Learning Architectures for Robust Arecanut Grading Under Capture-Induced Image Variations: Impact on Model Performance
authors: Jyothi V Prasad, Roopa B Hegde
abstract: Arecanuts hold significant cultural, economic, and therapeutic value in Southeast Asia, where they are regarded as symbols of goodwill and wealth and serve as a principal economic crop. Ensuring consistent grading is essential for maintaining quality and market value. Automated grading methods using machine learning and deep learning provide scalable alternatives to manual inspection, reducing human error and labour costs. However, image classification accuracy is often influenced by background variability. This study evaluates the robustness of four transfer-learning architectures, such as InceptionNet, ResNet, VGG16, and EfficientNet, for binary arecanut quality classification using a smartphone-acquired dataset captured under white and black backgrounds. Models were assessed using five-fold stratified cross-validation, with results reported as mean accuracy and standard deviation to ensure reliability. Cross and mixed-background experiments were conducted to examine background dependence, while non-uniform brightness augmentation was applied to training data to simulate realistic capture variability which is done dynamically during training, giving natural effects on the images. Results reveal that while all models achieved strong accuracy under controlled conditions, robustness varied significantly across architectures. InceptionNet demonstrated superior generalization, achieving a mean accuracy of 90.98 ± 2.54 with low variance under heterogeneous backgrounds. In contrast, EfficientNet and ResNet showed notable performance degradation when background conditions changed. This highlights that high accuracy under uniform conditions does not, in itself, guarantee practical grading applicability. Whereas InceptionNet consistently outperforms other architectures in terms of stability and generalization, and looks like a critical factor in designing reliable, low-cost smartphone-based arecanut grading systems.

title: A Realistic Control Approach for Set Stabilization of Complex Biological Networks With Logical Models
authors: Jung-Min Yang, Namhee Kim, Kwang-Hyun Cho
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Biology and Bioinformatics
abstract: Driving cellular biological systems to one of desired long-term behaviors, an important subject in systems biology, can be interpreted as the set stabilization problem. This article presents a realistic control approach to set stabilization of complex biological networks modeled by Boolean networks (BNs) wherein only open-loop control is applied. First, the considered BN is transformed into an auxiliary BN having redundant state variables. Based on the similarity between desired attractors and Boolean algebra, a number of reduced BNs are constructed from the auxiliary BN in favor of set stabilization. Next, the feedback vertex set (FVS) control law is applied to reduced BNs for obtaining open-loop control inputs that take partial state variables toward desired values. This procedure continues in a recursive way until ensuring that the controlled BN converges to an unspecified desired attractor. The proposed scheme has the superiority that both fixed-point and cyclic attractors can be included in the problem setting, and that it can tackle complex BNs with moderate computational burdens. Extensive numerical experiments on random BNs and real biological systems are provided to demonstrate the applicability of the proposed scheme.

title: Switchable nonreciprocity of spin-wave interference patterns in in-plane/perpendicular-magnetic-anisotropy waveguide system
authors: Khalid A. Omari, Xingtai Chen, Matthew T. Bryan, Phil Meeson, Wenqing Liu
abstract: Wave-based computing paradigms exploit interference and nonreciprocal propagation as essential ingredients for non-Boolean information processing. In magnetic systems, spin-wave dynamics naturally provide access to such functionalities. Previously reported nonreciprocal spin-wave devices generally lack reconfigurability. Here, we demonstrate through micromagnetic simulations that exchange-coupled bilayers combining in-plane anisotropy and perpendicular magnetic anisotropy (PMA) form a reconfigurable waveguide enabling switchable nonreciprocal SW interference patterns. The nonreciprocal SW interference patterns arise from quasi-domain-wall nanochannels due to competing anisotropies, and the nonreciprocity depends on the switchable magnetization polarity of the PMA layer. This provides additional degrees of freedom in the design of magnonic computing devices.

title: Joint Terminal Identification and Channel Estimation of Asynchronous Grant-Free Access for LEO Satellite-IoT Network
authors: Jiaqi Huang, Lixia Xiao, Wenhao Zheng, Jiaxi Zhou, Chengwen Xing, Tao Jiang
abstract: In this paper, the joint active terminal identification (ATI) and channel estimation (CE) problem is investigated for the asynchronous grant-free random access system in the context of low-earth orbit (LEO) satellite Internet of Things (IoT) network. An asynchronous grant-free model is established to characterize the implications of delay and Doppler caused by LEO satellites. Concurrently, a generalized approximate message passing-aided structured joint detection (SJD) scheme with Rician parameters learning (RPL) is proposed for joint ATI and CE. The prior mean and variance of the Rician channel are treated as hyperparameters and updated via the expectation-maximization algorithm. Furthermore, to alleviate the modeling mismatch, we develop an off-grid model following Taylor expansion, accompanied by a mismatch error parameter learning (MPL) framework to boost the accuracy of joint detection. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed RPL-SJD scheme outperforms existing approaches in terms of normalized mean square error with 4dB and activity detection error rate with 7dB.

title: Design and Implementation of a Low-Frequency in Single-Phase High-Power Inverter Using Arduino Mega 2560 for Modular Pico-Hydro Energy Systems
authors: Edfan Kurniawan Hartono, Leonardus Heru Pratomo
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Technology, Informatics, Management, Engineering and Environment (TIME-E)
abstract: High-efficiency power conditioning being an economical solution that can handle low-frequency AC inputs, it is important for the utilization of pico-hydro modules with a capacity below 5 kW so that they can be distributed to rural areas. The application of this hardware describes the workings and design of single-phase inverters designed for modular pico- hydro with a high capacity of up to 3kW and operating at low frequencies (50 Hz). These inverters operate with the low- frequency output of a hydro turbine adapted to commonly used high-frequency inverters. The system offers real-time and smooth adaptability to changing water flow conditions, thanks to the use of the Arduino Mega 2560 microcontroller. The system is capable of providing programmable PWM signals with high accuracy. The results obtained showed that this inverter performed well, both under resistive and inductive loads, was able to generate power between 1 and 3 kW, and provided frequency stability at 50 Hz as well as voltage control of 2%. When compared to previous alternatives, the use of the Arduino Mega 2560 offers the ability to operate on a multi-unit scale and reduces the cost and complexity of the system by up to 40%. The gap in pico-hydro is very important in this context, and can be further explored with the feasibility of microcontrollers that can be applied to high-capacity renewable energy systems.

title: A Comparative Video-Gait Analysis Using AI-Based Pose Estimation to Assess Driver Fatigue in Pre-Work and Post-Work Conditions
authors: Fikri Rida Pebriansyah, Endra Joelianto, Arjon Turnip
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Technology, Informatics, Management, Engineering and Environment (TIME-E)
abstract: Driver fatigue is a significant contributor to traffic accidents, underscoring the urgent need for objective, non-intrusive fatigue screening. Current methods are often subjective (questionnaires) or too intrusive for routine screening (e.g., EEG). This study explored the potential of video-based gait analysis as a non-intrusive biomarker for fatigue. We propose a framework utilizing AI-based pose estimation to extract gait signal parameters from video recordings. This study applied a comparative design to 30 professional land transportation drivers (DAMRI Bandung), where gait data was recorded under two conditions: pre-work and post-work. Features were extracted from the time domain (peak analysis) and the frequency domain (periodogram and spectrogram). A paired t-test was used to identify features with statistically significant differences (p < 0.05) between the two conditions. The results revealed 12 significant features. Spectral-domain features were found to be the most dominant, with Knee Angle (spectral power ratio, p = 0.000031; spectral entropy, p = 0.000039) and step length (spectral entropy, p = 0.000355) showing the highest significance. These findings imply that fatigue not only alters lower extremity kinematics but also manifests as subtle changes in the dynamics, regularity, and fluidity of the gait cycle. This study validates that video-based gait analysis using AI pose estimation is a promising approach for developing an objective fatigue screening system for drivers.

title: Power Quality Improvement in Grid-Tied PVG-UAPF Using MSOGI-NF
authors: Pydikalva Padmavathi, Bsanthoshkumar, J Valli, K Tanusha, C Sai Deepthi, D Sai Teja
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Trends in Material Science and Inventive Materials (ICTMIM)
abstract: The penetration of grid-tied photovoltaic generation systems is increasing in the electricity sector, having brought about major power quality problems for the distribution network considerations such as nonlinear loads, grid disturbances and the intermittent nature of solar energy. To overcome such problems, in this paper, an improved power quality control methodology for grid- connected PVG-Unified Active Power Filter is proposed. The proposed strategy uses the combination of Super- Twisting Sliding Mode Controller, modified Second-Order Generalized Integrator and Modulated by notch filter to achieve voltage and current compensation fulfils the voltage and current compensation under disturbed operating conditions. The modified SOGI allows the extraction of the fundamental components of voltage and current to be performed correctly even in presence of harmonics and unbalanced conditions, furthermore, the notch filter is efficient to suppress predominant low order harmonics without affecting dynamical response. The STSMC provides very good DC link voltage regulation and smooth compensating current control with lesser chattering. The efficacy of the proposed method is tested by careful investigation simulation under the conditions of grid voltage distortion, voltage sag and swell, variable solar irradiance and unbalanced load condition. The results show improved harmonic mitigation, stable operation of the dc link and improved overall power quality of the system which verifies the suitability of the proposed control strategy for grid tied PVG- UAPF applications.

title: Research on Acute Myocardial Infarction Risk Prediction Model Based on FT-Transformer and MLP Dual-Stream Heterogeneous Feature Fusion
authors: Yunke Wang, Ganglong Duan
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Advanced Algorithms and Control Engineering (ICAACE)
abstract: Acute Myocardial Infarction (AMI) is a cardiovascular emergency with high mortality. Early and accurate risk stratification is crucial for improving clinical outcomes. Existing single-modal models often suffer from inductive biases when processing heterogeneous electronic health record (EHR) data. This paper proposes a dual-stream deep learning framework combining FT-Transformer and Multilayer Perceptron (MLP). The FT-Transformer branch captures global feature interactions via multi-head self-attention, while the MLP branch models local nonlinear patterns. A Stacking ensemble strategy with a logistic regression meta-learner dynamically fuses the outputs of both streams to enhance generalization. Experiments on a large-scale clinical dataset of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 0, 0 0 0}$</tex> records show that the proposed model achieves an accuracy of 98.2%, recall of 95.5%, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{F 1}$</tex>-score of 95.4%, significantly outperforming traditional machine learning models and single deep learning architectures. Furthermore, we discuss the handling of missing values and conduct sensitivity analysis to assess the impact of different imputation methods on model performance. The results demonstrate the robustness and practical value of our approach for clinical decision support.

title: AD5941-Enabled Digital Active Electrode for Electrical Impedance Tomography
authors: Yukang Huang, Dong Liu
publish: 2025 4th International Conference on Advanced Sensing, Intelligent Manufacturing (ASIM)
abstract: Electrical impedance tomography (EIT) is a functional imaging modality capable of reconstructing the conductivity distribution within the human body, which is challenged by limitations such as parasitic effects of long lead wires and system complexity. To address these issues, this paper proposes a novel digital active electrode architecture based on the AD5941, which mitigates signal degradation and simplifies system design by integrating all the analog front-end circuitry, including both excitation and measurement units, at the electrode site. An adaptive gain control mechanism is also introduced to improve the system’s noise suppression capability and inter-channel consistency. A 16-electrode EIT system based on the proposed DAE has been developed and tested. Experiments demonstrate that the system achieves a signal-to-noise ratio over 70dB at 62.5kHz with 21.33 frames per second and is capable of reconstructing the positions and shapes of embedded objects in the saline tank.

title: Machine Learning-Based Wind Speed and Power Forecasting Using Meteorological Data
authors: Taha Muftah Abuali, Abdussalam Ali Ahmed, Yasser Fathi Nassar, Mohamed Mohamed Khaleel, Hala El-Khozondar, Abdulgader Alsharif
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Maghreb Meeting of the Conference on Sciences and Techniques of Automatic Control and Computer Engineering (MI-STA)
abstract: Wind power forecasting is essential for reliable renewable energy integration. This paper investigates datadriven models for short-term wind speed and wind power prediction using supervisory control and data acquisition (SCADA) and meteorological features. We develop and evaluate several machine learning models, including linear regression, Random Forest (RF), and Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost), to forecast wind speed and turbine power output at a $\mathbf{1 0}$-minute horizon and a $\mathbf{1}$-hour horizon. We also propose a hybrid physics-informed approach that integrates a turbine power curve with an RF model to improve power forecast accuracy. Experiments are conducted on a real wind farm dataset with 134 turbines over two years. Results show that for very short horizons (10 min), all models achieve comparable accuracy, with a naive persistence baseline (using the last observed value) proving difficult to beat. At the 1-hour horizon, the machine learning models outperform persistence in some metrics, and the proposed hybrid model further reduces error compared to purely data-driven models. We analyze feature importance using SHAP (Shapley Additive Explanations) to interpret the models, confirming that recent turbine measurements (especially prior wind speed and power) are the dominant predictive features. The paper discusses the implications of these findings for multi-turbine forecasting and outlines future work to enhance forecast accuracy through meteorological data integration and advanced modeling techniques.

title: Design and Evaluation of an Artificial Intelligence-Based Sentiment Analysis Tool Using the Feelings Wheel and Real-World Data
authors: Monique Visser, Nicolaas Luwes, J. F. Ryk Lues, Wilhelmus Pretorius
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Technology, Informatics, Management, Engineering and Environment (TIME-E)
abstract: Sentiment analysis in the workplace helps organisations understand employee emotions, improve communication and enhance productivity. It is important to make data-driven decisions to foster a positive work environment. Sentiment analysis typically relies on employee feedback, which is written in their own words, making it challenging to analyse without a structured rubric and often based on subjective impressions. While Likert scales provide an easier method for evaluation, open-ended responses offer a more accurate representation of sentiment. Popular Artificial intelligence tools are versed in natural language processing, which could enable the interpretation of emotions within textual data. There are currently some AI-based sentiment analysis tools being used, but as a new science, there is space for new development or improvement. The Feelings Wheel, a psychological framework, offers a structured approach to categorizing emotions, enhancing the granularity of sentiment classification from verbs and action verbs. This study presents the design and evaluation of an AI-based sentiment analysis tool utilizing the Feelings Wheel and real-world data collected from factory managers. The proposed tool is benchmarked against existing popular AI sentiment analysis libraries, highlighting its advantages and limitations. Results indicate that while the Feelings Wheel-based approach provides a more detailed emotional breakdown, it also introduces challenges in classification accuracy and computational complexity. The study discusses the interpretability of findings and explores potential solutions to improve the model’s robustness, offering insights into refining AI-based sentiment analysis for industrial applications
