title: A Defect-Informed Phase-Field Model for Gate Oxide Breakdown in SiC MOS Structures
authors: Jinglin Li, Aditya Shekhar, Yaqian Zhang, Willem D. van Driel, Guoqi Zhang
publish: 2026 27th International Conference on Thermal, Mechanical and Multi-Physics Simulation and Experiments in Microelectronics and Microsystems (EuroSimE)
abstract: Silicon carbide (SiC) MOS structures are promising for high voltage and high temperature power electronics, but gate oxide breakdown remains a major reliability concern because of its stochastic nature and the limited accessibility of the underlying failure pathway. In this work, a defect-informed phase- field framework is developed to investigate oxide breakdown in SiC MOS structures. The model is initialized using a depth- resolved pre-existing defect profile extracted from capacitance and conductance frequency response, thereby linking electrical characterization with physics-based breakdown simulation. The results show that breakdown starts from localized nucleation at defect-rich weak points near the SiO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> /SiC interface, followed by directional growth, interaction, and coalescence of degraded regions into a through-thickness conductive path. Combined analysis of the phase-field variable and electric field distribution shows that local electric field redistribution plays a key role in guiding conductive path propagation and determining the final breakdown trajectory. Post-breakdown focused ion beam scanning electron microscopy (FIB-SEM) reveals a tortuous and spatially confined channel morphology, consistent with the defect-assisted and field-driven breakdown picture predicted by the model. The proposed framework provides a useful tool for analyzing breakdown trajectories and gate oxide reliability in SiC power devices.

title: The Effect of Flow Rate on Flow-Accelerated Corrosion of 2205 Stainless Steel and the Suitability of Data-Driven Model were Studied
authors: Xiaoyang Hai, Guan Wang, Yushan Ma, Cunrui Meng, Le Xu, Yanru Xu
publish: 2025 4th International Conference on Advanced Sensing, Intelligent Manufacturing (ASIM)
abstract: Flow-accelerated corrosion (FAC) poses a critical challenge to industrial pipeline systems, with flow velocity serving as a dominant influencing factor. This study investigates the interplay between flow velocity and corrosion kinetics through integrated numerical simulations and experimental measurements, while evaluating the role of multiphysics data in enhancing neural network-based corrosion prediction.

title: Automotive MIMO OFDM Radar Demonstrator with 3D-Printed Waveguide Antennas
authors: Benjamin Nuss, Christian Karle, Tobias T. Braun, Jan Schoepfel, Florian Vogelsang, Farhan Bin Khalid, Eva Waldner, Nils Pohl, Thomas Zwick
abstract: Digital radar sensors are becoming increasingly popular for various applications. On the one hand, they offer a wide range of possibilities for generating uncorrelated signals for massive multiple-input multiple-output systems as well as for the synchronization of radar networks. On the other hand, the high sampling rates and memory sizes still pose a major challenge for implementation. This paper describes an OFDM radar demonstrator for automotive applications at 79 GHz, which enables wide signal bandwidths of up to 2 GHz. In addition, an exchangeable 3D-printed waveguide antenna array with four transmit and receive channels is used. Exemplary radar measurements prove the functionality of the demonstrator and show its range and angle separation capabilities using real objects.

title: Sem-Wavemamba: Disentangling Noise and Texture in Low-Light Image Enhancement Via Semantic-Guided Texture Dialysis
authors: Chengxin Xu, Tieru Wang
publish: 2026 5th Asia Conference on Algorithms, Computing and Machine Learning (CACML)
abstract: Low-light image enhancement (LLIE) remains a challenging task, particularly in balancing noise removal and texture preservation under high-ISO conditions. While recent Transformer-based methods achieve impressive results, they often incur prohibitive computational costs, limiting their practical deployment. Furthermore, lightweight CNNs struggle to distinguish between high-frequency structural details and severe sensor noise, leading to grid artifacts, ringing, or color shifts. To address these issues, we propose Sem-WaveMamba (SWM), an efficient architecture that integrates a Wavelet-Mamba encoder with a novel Semantic-Guided Texture Dialysis module. Specifically, SWM leverages the frequency-aware nature of wavelet transforms and the global modeling capabilities of Mamba to eliminate color casts and structural artifacts. Meanwhile, the semanticguided module acts as a filter, explicitly disentangling noise from texture based on semantic priors. Extensive experiments on realworld benchmarks demonstrate that SWM achieves state-of-theart robustness against severe noise. Notably, on 4 K resolution data, SWM outperforms Restormer while requiring <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0} \times$</tex> fewer MACs.

title: Phase Shifting Transformer Protection Overview and Impact of ARS on Protection Scheme
authors: Glenn Callaghan, Jacob Billings, Jason Campisi
publish: 2026 79th Annual Conference for Protective Relay Engineers (CFPR)
abstract: As the modern power system continues to evolve with high load centers supplied with power from remote locations, there will be an increase in use of Phase Shifting Transformers to transfer real power to the appropriate locations of the power system. The Phase Shifting Transformer has unique attributes that need to be evaluated to develop a robust protection scheme. This paper focuses on high level evaluation of Phase Shifting Transformer protection and how a protection engineer can utilize familiar protection schemes for this device. Additionally, lessons learned from the integration of Advance-Retard-Switch and the impact on the amp-turn balance protection is shared.

title: A Calculus for Web Services Choreography: Formal Semantics, Projection, and Correctness
authors: Chang Yang
publish: 2026 5th Asia Conference on Algorithms, Computing and Machine Learning (CACML)
abstract: Existing choreography languages often lack a uniform formal semantics that enables provably correct projection from global specifications to local behaviours. To address this, we propose <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{Orc}_{\pi}$</tex>, a minimal calculus for web services choreography that integrates service coordination primitives with name-passing mobility within a strictly confined syntax of only six core primitives. Unlike approaches relying on complex global types or coherence proofs, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{Orc}_{\pi}$</tex> employs a lightweight linear typing disciplinedeliberately avoiding the overhead of complex multiparty session type subtyping and merging mechanisms-to enforce protocol correctness. We define operational and trace-based semantics to facilitate rigorous equivalence reasoning. The main results establish that for every closed, well-typed choreography, the projected endpoint processes are deadlock-free and trace-equivalent to the global specification. We evaluate the framework's formal guarantees and illustrate its applicability through canonical interaction patterns typical of Internet of Things (IoT) environments. The proposed calculus deliberately trades expressive power for simplicity, enabling concise correctness arguments and yielding a foundational model for reliable distributed execution.

title: Pathogenic Gene Prediction Model Based on L3-Enhanced Diffusion Networks
authors: Haoyu Wu, Chun Wang
publish: 2026 5th Asia Conference on Algorithms, Computing and Machine Learning (CACML)
abstract: Effectively identifying disease-gene associations can reveal disease pathogenesis at the microscopic level and provide strategies for precision medicine. With the deepening exploration of biological network medicine, pathogenic gene prediction methods based on heterogeneous graph learning have been widely proposed and applied. Specially, enhanced graph representation methods based on the L3 principle have been proven to effectively improve prediction performance. However, existing research overlooks the interference of noise data on embedding quality, which further limits the learning performance of models. To address this shortcoming, we propose L3DiffNet, a diffusion-based heterogeneous graph learning framework. By incorporating an adaptive denoising mechanism tailored to biological network characteristics, it enables the accurate extraction of information from protein enrichment networks, thereby significantly improving pathogenic gene association prediction. Extensive crossvalidation experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate that our model outperforms all baselines.

title: Modeling, Design and Measurement of a Compact mmWave Antenna Array with Radome for Biomedical Radar Sensing
authors: Thi Huyen Le, Ivan Ndip, Uwe Maass, Jens Schneider, Ulrike Ganesh
abstract: In this work, the design, performance analysis, and experimental validation of a compact mmWave 1×8 series-fed antenna array with radome effects for medical radar sensing operating in the 60 GHz ISM band are presented. The Dolph–Chebyshev synthesis method is employed in the array design to determine the amplitude coefficients for each element, thereby effectively suppressing side lobes. Furthermore, the impacts of various radome parameters such as material properties, thickness, and spacing between the array and radome on impedance matching, radiation characteristics, and overall efficiency are thoroughly investigated and analyzed. Finally, the fabricated antenna array was experimentally characterized under the examined radome conditions, demonstrating excellent agreement between simulated and measured results.

title: Detection of Phishing URLs Using Machine Learning and Multi-Level Feature Extraction
authors: Saumya Ramani, Darshan Rathod, Umesh Bodkhe, Nilesh Kumar Jadav, Ayush Gour
abstract: The rapid growth of online communication and the development of digital services have significantly increased users' exposure to malicious web resources, particularly phishing sites that use misleading URLs to steal sensitive data. Traditional blacklist- and rule-based detection systems are increasingly ineffective against dynamically generated, short-lived phishing URLs. The proposed article presents an implementation-based machine learning framework for automated phishing URL detection based on a multi-feature analysis. The proposed solution derives and combines the attributes of three complementary groups that include domain-based attributes capturing registration age and traffic-related anomalies, address-bar and lexical attributes modeling syntactic irregularities and redirection behavior, and HTML/JavaScript-based attributes predicting suspicious clientside code integrated into web pages. Then, the features are used to train and evaluate machine learning classifiers to accurately distinguish legitimate from malicious URLs. The experimental findings indicate that combining heterogeneous feature sets substantially improves detection accuracy (85.9%) and robustness compared with single-feature methods. The proposed system provides a viable, scalable, and configurable framework for real-time detection of phishing URLs, thereby enhancing security against evolving cyber threats in contemporary web environments.

title: Design and Development of a Low Cost 5 - DOF Robotic Arm for Surgical Robotics Education and Prototyping
authors: Naveeshwar B, Naveen C, R. Pandian
abstract: Robotic systems are increasingly used in surgical applications to enhance motion stability and reduce human fatigue during complex procedures. However, the high cost of commercial surgical robots limits their accessibility for education and early-stage research. This paper describes the design and development of a low-cost, 5-degree-of-freedom (5DOF) robotic arm intended as an educational and prototyping platform for surgical robotics. The proposed system is built using an ESP32 microcontroller, PCA9685 servo driver, and servo-based actuation, with a lightweight 3D-printed PLA structure. Control is achieved through a joystick and optional Wi-Fi interface. Experimental evaluation demonstrates stable joint motion, repeatable positioning with an angular deviation of approximately <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\pm 2^{\circ}$</tex> to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\pm 3^{\circ}$</tex>, and reliable object manipulation for training-oriented tasks. The developed prototype serves as an accessible platform for students and researchers to understand fundamental concepts of surgical robotic manipulation and control before transitioning to advanced clinical systems.

title: Human-Like Hiring at Scale: A Conversational AI and Computer Vision Approach
authors: Arnav Aggarwal, Silky Sachar, Harvinder Singh, Vishan Kumar Gupta, Alind, Harish Shakya
abstract: Artificial Intelligence (AI) is emerging as a major facilitator for recruiting today. Although human interviews are still helpful, they have become a bottleneck for recruiting due to uneven judgment, human availability, and unconscious bias towards less-qualified applicants. We also recognize that fairness and quality become increasingly difficult for HR teams as the number and cost of hires increase. To help mitigate this challenge, we built an AI-Powered Interview Bot to automate the entire process of interviewing using Google Generative AI to create questions dynamically, OpenCV for live face detection, Firebase for secure storage and authentication, and LangChain to provide context to the LLM. Streamlit is used for the interface delivery. The bot analyzes resumes and job descriptions, generates tailored questions, monitors behavior using facial ID and session tracking, and provides detailed interview summaries. This paper discusses design, implementation, testing, security, and potential extensions like sentiment analysis and hybrid interviewing.

title: Biquadratic Exchange Coupling Effect on the Magnetic Properties of (Fe/Ti) Multilayers
authors: M. Yactayo, H. S. Tarazona, O. Copie, J.-C. Rojas-Sánchez, J. Quispe-Marcatoma, C. V. Landauro
publish: 2025 IEEE Latin American Conference on Nanotechnology (LANANO)
abstract: This work explores the static and dynamic magnetic properties of weakly antiferromagnetically coupled Fe/Ti superlattices, emphasizing the link between magnetic behavior and structural characteristics. HRTEM and XRD analyses confirm alternating Fe and Ti layers with rough interfaces, especially in upper layers. Magnetic measurements reveal two-step hysteresis loops and a temperature- and thickness-dependent interlayer exchange coupling (IEC). A macrospin model incorporating bilinear and biquadratic coupling reproduces the experimental data and supports a phase diagram analysis identifying non-collinear configurations. The results underscore the impact of structural imperfections and highlight the crucial role of biquadratic exchange in Fe/Ti/Fe multilayers.

title: Machine Learning-Based Prediction of Metal Nanoparticle Toxicity in Human Cell Lines: A Preliminary Contribution to the Development of Nanotoxicology in Peru
authors: Leonardo Camarena, Luis Yauri, Jannina Romero, Nhikori Tovar, Juan Mondalgo
publish: 2025 IEEE Latin American Conference on Nanotechnology (LANANO)
abstract: The increasing use of metallic nanoparticles (NPs) in biomedicine has sparked interest due to their unique nanoscale properties, such as high surface-to-volume ratio and tunable functionalization. However, their potential toxicity in human tissues remains a critical barrier to safe clinical applications. This study presents a preliminary computational nanotoxicology approach using Machine Learning (ML) to predict the toxicity of metallic NPs in human cell lines, providing a foundation for future biomedical research. A dataset integrating physicochemical properties (material type, core size, hydrodynamic size, surface charge, formation enthalpy, among others) and exposure parameters (dose and time) from various cytotoxicity assays was employed. Data were standardized, encoded, and refined through outlier detection and treatment. Feature importance was assessed using ANOVA F-test and Random Forest, revealing that exposure dose (Exposure_dose_µg_per_mL) was the primary predictor of toxicity. To address class imbalance, the Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique (SMOTE) was applied. Four experimental workflows were designed: two with treated outliers and two with untreated outliers; in each case, one with SMOTE and one without. Six supervised classification models with optimized parameters were evaluated per experiment. Based on AUC, the best model from each case was selected, with Experiment 2 (outliers treated without SMOTE) and the XGBoost model achieving the best overall performance, named RonsocoNano Model, reaching an AUC of 0.963, an accuracy of 0.912, a precision of 0.910, a recall of 0.912, and a F1-score of 0.911.This work represents an initial step toward applying ML in human cell-based nanotoxicology, offering a cost-effective alternative to extensive and heterogeneous in vitro testing. The findings may serve as a foundation for the rational design of less toxic metallic NPs and, in the future, for their evaluation in oncological therapies in Peru, where biomedical nanotechnology remains underdeveloped but holds significant potential.

title:
Electronic and Magnetic Study of the Composition of Fe
                    <sub>75</sub>
                    Al
                    <sub>25</sub>
                    Prepared by arc Furnace
authors: R. Rocha Cabrera, R.M. Guzmán Arellano, C. Rojas Ayala
publish: 2025 IEEE Latin American Conference on Nanotechnology (LANANO)
abstract: The Fe<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">75</inf>Al<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">25</inf> composition was synthesized using the arc furnace technique and subsequently heat-treated at 600 °C. X-ray diffraction (XRD) and Mössbauer spectroscopy (MS) indicate the formation of the Fe<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf>Al intermetallic compound with a DO3-type structure and space group Fm-3m, an iron-rich Fe(Al) solid solution, both at room temperature and at 600 °C. Electronic structure calculations were performed using first-principles methods based on density functional theory (DFT). These calculations revealed the influence of the specific Fe-Al atomic configurations on the material's properties. Although DFT was useful for calculating the electronic structure. The results show good agreement between the structural and magnetic properties.

title: Sustainable Nanotechnology in Forensics: Utilizing Tea-Based Iron Nanoparticles for Latent Fingerprint Development
authors: Fathima Hafsa Thaha, Maria Folkestad Farsund, Amrutha Nambiar, Navjeet Kaur Lotey, Gursirat Singh Khokhar, Nrashant Singh
publish: 2025 IEEE Latin American Conference on Nanotechnology (LANANO)
abstract: Latent print detection is one of the main biometric components of forensic science and criminal investigation. Although a wide range of fingerprint powders are used in this process, most of them have low sensitivity and are toxic to the environment. The high reactivity and large surface area of nanoparticles make them a promising candidate for latent fingerprint detection. Studies have shown that the visualization of latent fingerprints by nanoparticles is size, shape, and stability dependent. In this study, we successfully green-synthesized iron nanoparticles (FeNPs) from the aqueous extracts of Camellia sinensis (black tea and green tea) using their rich content of phytochemicals as natural reducing and stabilizing agents. The tea extracts produced the uniform FeNPs under ambient conditions. The three different analytical techniques were used to characterize the biosynthesized particles. UV-Visible (UV-Vis) spectra of green-synthesized FeNPs show distinct characteristic absorption peaks at 268nm for black tea FeNPs and 232 nm for green tea FeNPs. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis shows mean hydrodynamic diameter (Dmean) of 25.5nm for black tea FeNPs and 17.8 nm for green tea FeNPs. FTIR spectrophotometry analysis confirmed the presence of surface functional groups of FeNPs. Application of activated FeNPs (with or without binder) to different porous and non-porous surfaces showed high adherence to the fingerprints. A clear ridge image with high contrast was developed, which showed better results than standard fingerprint powders. Overall, it is concluded that the tea-synthesized FeNPs, in particular, black tea-synthesized FeNPs, is nontoxic, cost-effective, and eco-friendly alternative to the commercial fingerprint powders.

title: A Novel Method for Appliance Identification, Swap Detection, and Incipient Fault Diagnosis for HEMS
authors: Ritik Rajput, Ankush Sharma, Abheejeet Mohapatra
publish: 2026 IEEE Texas Power and Energy Conference (TPEC)
abstract: Home Energy Management Systems (HEMS) often fail when an appliance is replaced, swapped with another appliance, or becomes faulty, leading to errors in scheduling, fault detection, and downstream analytics. This paper proposes a novel practical pipeline for appliance identity verification, program discovery, and detection of swapped, faulty, or behavior shifted devices using low rate active power measurements over short horizons. Two complementary views of each run; electrical features, which capture load shape and intensity, and temporal features, which capture usage routine. From seven days of reference data, discover programs per appliance via clustering on program reference vectors. For a new run, diagonal Mahalanobis distances to each program in both spaces are converted to chisquare <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$p$</tex>-values and fused. When temporal features agree but electrical features deviate, a Spearman rank correlation and multiplicative scale check distinguish coherent level shifts like faults or calibration drift from mismatched shapes like replacement or unknown device. The pipeline outputs interpretable labels such as same device, fault-suspect, replacement/unknown, or uncertain. This method is demonstrated on five common appliances using a publicly available household dataset, and is lightweight, operates directly on submetered plug streams, and deployable, avoiding complex waveform processing while enabling safe, thresholded actions in HEMS scheduling and fault handling.

title: AI and Digital Twin-Based Multi-Operator Collaboration for 6G Use Cases
authors: Berna Bulut-Cebecioglu, A. Almazi Bipon, Ahmad Soleymani, Raouf Abozariba, Adel Aneiba, Qingsong Han, Sa Xiao, Jintao Zhang
abstract: Sixth-generation (6G) networks must support immersive and mission-critical services that impose extreme and simultaneous requirements on data rate, latency, reliability, and connectivity. Meeting these stringent demands challenges the capabilities of traditional single-operator network operation, necessitating architectural enhancements. To address this, this paper investigates multi-operator collaboration as a scalable paradigm for 6G, enabled by the synergy of artificial intelligence (AI) and digital twins (DTs). We propose a novel, unified framework: per-operator DTs facilitate high-fidelity, predictive network modeling, while a federated multi-agent deep reinforcement learning scheme enables robust, privacy-preserving cross-operator optimization. A real-world case study, utilizing extensive measurement data from four major mobile network operators (MNOs) in the United Kingdom, demonstrates the framework’s efficacy. Results show that the proposed multi-MNO collaborative approach significantly reduces quality-of-service violations and improves achievable data rates compared to standalone deployments, particularly under the high-throughput demands characteristic of immersive applications. Finally, we conclude by discussing the key challenges and future research directions essential for realizing practical, AI-native 6G networks.

title: IB2MC: Information Bottleneck Inspired Balanced Multiview Clustering
authors: Shengzhao Guo, Jingyu Wang, Letian Zhang, Feiping Nie, Xuelong Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: Information bottleneck (IB) theory aims to compact coding while retaining task-relevant information, but the lack of prior knowledge in clustering poses challenges to its application. Besides, since most methods fail to emphasize clustering security, empty clusters caused by excessive preference can lead to significant performance degradation. Motivated by these, a scalable framework is proposed, named Information Bottleneck Inspired Balanced Multiview Clustering (IB2MC), which boasts clustering security, optimization synergy, and tuning simplicity. To begin with, the in-depth discussion on unsupervised IB theory reveals the balance principle via maximizing label entropy, which preserves the uncertainty of cluster distribution to overcome bias or neglect for clusters. On this basis, compressed descriptors are expected to evolve toward discriminability, thus facilitating label inference from them at the same time. Inspired by this, our method proposes dynamic cosine graph learning for label transmission, and achieves seamless label extraction to strengthen dual-level consistency. Moreover, the representation alignment under independent constraint can enhance cluster separability, while the trace ratio for self-balance clustering can further avoid extra hyperparameter tuning. In this way, the time complexity of our method is linear with sample size, while no pre- or post-processing is required for joint optimization. To demonstrate the effectiveness of our method, seventeen state-of-the-art methods are chosen as baselines and our method ranks first in average performance across all twelve real-world data sets.

title: Universal Graph Convolutional Network: Zero-Shot Transfer Across Power Grid Topology Reconfigurations
authors: Tong Wu, Anna Scaglione, Sandy Miguel, Daniel Arnold
abstract: This work addresses a fundamental challenge in applying deep learning to network topology-dependent power system applications: developing neural network models that transfer across significant system changes, including networks with entirely different topologies and dimensionalities, without requiring training data from unseen reconfigurations. We focus on situational awareness tasks where predictions inherently depend on network-wide spatial relationships through electrical connectivity. Despite extensive research, most ML-based approaches remain system-specific, limiting real-world deployment. This limitation stems from a dual barrier. First, topology changes shift feature distributions and alter input dimensions due to power flow physics. Second, reconfigurations redefine output semantics and dimensionality, requiring models to handle configuration-specific outputs while maintaining transferable feature extraction. Our core insight is treating topology as a learnable dimension through training on diverse reconfigurations, enabling the model to learn universal power flow physics rather than memorizing fixed networks. We achieve this through three synergistic mechanisms: scalar-weight graph convolutions with physics-aware operators handle variable input dimensions and distribution shifts, adaptive pooling maps topology-specific features to unified latent representations, and parallel transformer generates configuration-specific outputs using positional encoding. Our Universal Graph Convolutional Network (UGCN) achieves transferability to any reconfiguration or variation of existing power systems without any prior knowledge of new grid topologies or retraining during implementation. Experimental results on state forecasting and false data injection detection demonstrate that UGCN significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods in cross-system zero-shot transferability across both transmission and distribution networks.

title: IoT-Enabled Deep Learning Framework for Real-Time Fault Detection and Prediction in Photovoltaic System
authors: Tehseen Tariq, Ali Hamza, Zunaib Ali, Nicholas Christofides, Shabab Saleem, Komal Saleem, Sultan M Alghamdi
publish: 2026 IEEE Texas Power and Energy Conference (TPEC)
abstract: The global expansion of photovoltaic (PV) systems is a transformative shift toward a clean energy sector. However, their operational reliability and energy yield consistency remain critical challenges as PV panels are susceptible to partial shading, connection mismatches and environmental degradation, resulting in significant power losses. Conventional monitoring approaches like manual inspection, threshold-based alarms and IV curve tracing lack to provide online, scalable and accurate detection under time-varying conditions. To overcome these limitations, this paper proposes an IoT-enabled deep learning framework which employs Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) autoencoder for online anomaly and fault detection as well as system performance forecasting. The proposed sensor-based LSTM monitoring system achieved 95% fault detection accuracy and a forecasting root mean square error (RMSE) of 0.125. Furthermore, the temperature sensitivity analysis reveals a degradation factor of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$-0.69 \% / C^{\circ}$</tex>, demonstrating that it is feasible to quantitatively capture the impact of environmental stress on PV performance. A Raspberry Pi 4B integrated with an INA219 voltage and current sensor is employed for data acquisition, with measurements sampled every 5 seconds from a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$4 \times 1 \mathbf{P V}$</tex> array. TensorFlow Lite-based models are deployed on the Raspberry Pi for onboard inference and interfaced with the Blynk IoT platform for remote monitoring and real-time alert of faults. Experimental results demonstrate robust classification of shading and opencircuit faults, achieved a forecasting RMSE of 0.125 and an inference latency of less than <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5}$</tex> seconds.

title: Undergraduate Research on Physically Informed Transmission Line Design
authors: Abdoulaye Diop, William Yun, Jonathan M. Snodgrass, Thomas J. Overbye
publish: 2026 IEEE Texas Power and Energy Conference (TPEC)
abstract: Modern power system studies increasingly require transmission grid models that are not only electrically plausible but also physically consistent enough to support resilience and infrastructure-risk analysis. However, in many public synthetic cases, electrical parameters (R, X, B) and physical attributes (tower and conductor types, ratings, line lengths) are assigned largely independently, so thermal limits, impedances, and shunt terms do not always correspond to a realizable line design. This paper proposes a dual-path reconstruction framework that embeds standard transmission line physics and a library of typical ACSR conductors into synthetic models. Depending on which data are deemed more credible, the method treats either the thermal ratings (“Preserve MVA”) or the impedance set (“Preserve RXB”) as fixed, and then infers ACSR conductor size, bundle configuration, geometric mean radius (GMR), geometric mean distance (GMD), and line length to obtain a physically and electrically consistent design. Case studies on the EPIGRIDS 7k and ACTIVSg 2k Texas bus synthetic cases show that the reconstructed models closely match the original operating characteristics while supplying the additional design detail needed for tower- and weather-aware resilience studies. A validation was also conducted against the 2024 series ERCOT operating case.

authors: N/A

title: Impact of Forecasted Data on One-Day Ahead Solar Irradiance Forecasting
authors: Ninh Nguyen, Nga Nguyen
publish: 2026 IEEE Texas Power and Energy Conference (TPEC)
abstract: Renewable energy sources (RESs) have been increasingly integrated into the grid due to their abundant supply and environmental friendliness. However, due to their heavy dependence on weather conditions, predicting and controlling RES output power are essential for an appropriate grid schedule. Solar irradiance forecasting has been proven to have a significant impact on grid planning. While short-term forecasting achieves good accuracy for observation, long-term forecasting yields poor results due to a lack of forecasted data. Adding the clearness index as an input feature, lengthening the input sequence, or fine-tuning model hyperparameters have been used to improve long-term forecasting accuracy. To advance previous work, this paper proposes an approach for synthesizing forecasted data from historical data, incorporating reasonable errors via interpolation and an autoregressive (AR1) model. The interpolation technique is employed to construct a continuous interpolant that deviates only marginally from the observations, while retaining the underlying temporal patterns. Then, the AR1 method is used to add correlation noise, preserving the weather's random distribution. After that, the Monte Carlo method is used to fine-tune hyperparameters to improve forecasting accuracy. To prove the efficacy, the proposed method's accuracy will be compared with three deep learning (DL) models. The findings underscore the importance of the forecasted data in increasing solar irradiance forecasting accuracy.

title: SkyWin-SLAM: Robust Visual-LiDAR Fusion Integrating Swin Transformer Depth Completion and Semantic Sky Masking
authors: Jin Sun, Xue Shen, Jinwu Tong, Haitao Zhao
abstract: Visual-LiDAR fusion offers a promising solution to the limitations of pure visual Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) in challenging outdoor environments. However, existing Red, Green, Blue, Lime (RGB-L) systems often suffer from poor localization accuracy due to two primary issues: the inability of CNN-based depth completion networks to capture global geometry from sparse LiDAR data, and the “virtual wall” bias where sky regions are incorrectly predicted as finite depths. This paper proposes a robust RGB-L SLAM framework to address these challenges. First, we introduce Swin-PENet, a depth completion network incorporating Swin Transformer blocks to capture long-range dependencies and generate high-fidelity depth maps. Second, we propose a semantic decoupling strategy that explicitly filters out sky regions, preventing erroneous depth biases from corrupting the SLAM backend optimization. Experimental results on the KITTI Odometry Benchmark demonstrate that our system effectively eliminates scale contraction and rotational drift. The proposed framework achieves a 15.2% improvement in localization accuracy compared to the ORB-SLAM3 stereo mode.

title: Rivic: Reversible Image Hiding for Robust and Secure Visual Communication
authors: Haotian Zhu, Wei Wu, Haipeng Peng, Lixiang Li
abstract: With the widespread deployment of the Internet of Things (IoT) and distributed sensing systems, visual data are frequently transmitted over bandwidth-limited and unreliable links, where compression and noise degradation are inevitable. This poses significant challenges to secure, imperceptible, and reversible image communication. Existing steganographic methods often suffer from limited reversibility, noticeable statistical distortion, and weak resistance to steganalysis under realistic channel conditions. This paper proposes Rivic, a reversible image hiding framework for robust and secure visual communication. Rivic integrates wavelet-domain multi-frequency modeling, hierarchical spectral refinement, multi-attentive correction, and multi-domain synergistic recovery into an end-to-end invertible network, aiming to enhance embedding stability and noise-resilient reconstruction. Experimental results on ImageNet, COCO, and ImageHide demonstrate that Rivic achieves 34–35 dB PSNR and 0.93–0.95 SSIM in the cover–stego task, yielding a 5–7 dB PSNR improvement over the strongest CNN-based baselines. Under various channel degradations, including additive noise and JPEG compression, Rivic consistently maintains reconstruction quality above 30 dB. Moreover, when evaluated against advanced steganalysis networks such as SRNet, Yedroudj-Net, and SiaSteg-Net, Rivic reduces the average detection accuracy to 0.50–0.62, approaching the level of random guessing. These results indicate that Rivic enables high-fidelity, robust, and secure reversible visual communication in IoT sensing and control systems operating under practical transmission constraints.

title: A Novel Two-Stage Optimization Strategy for Solving Electric Field Inverse Problems in Multi-Circuit Overhead Transmission Lines
authors: Bangjie Xia, Yalong Mai, Jun Chen, Libing Tao, Yankai Xing, Daotian Pang, Haopeng An, Jian Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Non-contact electric field sensing has emerged as a key research area due to its inherent advantages of safety, flexibility, and real-time capability. However, utilizing electric field data to inversely reconstruct conductor voltages in Multi-Circuit systems presents significant challenges, including high dimensionality, parameter coupling, and numerical instability. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a two-stage optimization method that decomposes the inverse problem into two sub-problems: voltage estimation and position estimation. The approach enhances solution accuracy and stability through the application of constraint conditions and exploitation of system structural characteristics. Additionally, a sensor array is employed for spatially distributed measurements to reduce parameter coupling and improve observability. The effectiveness of the proposed method is validated using double-circuit transmission lines as a demonstration case. Simulation and scaled experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves voltage estimation errors within ±5% and position estimation errors within ±3% under various operating conditions, confirming its effectiveness for non-contact measurement applications.

title: Large AI Models for the Internet of Everything
authors: Feibo Jiang, Kezhi Wang, Xingqin Lin, Merouane Debbah, Zhu Han

title: Cavity-Independent Time-Frequency Interleaved Photonic ADC Enabled by Monolithic TFLN Electro-Optic Comb for 40-GSa/s Sampling
authors: Fujian Yu, Ke Yin, Tao Zhang, Wei Dong, Chao Zhou, Pan Han, Xiangai Cheng, Tian Jiang
abstract: Time-frequency interleaved photonic analog-to-digital convertor (TIPADC) is a promising architecture for scaling analog-to-digital conversion (ADC) bandwidth and sample rate, but constraining by cavity-dependent limitations intrinsic to the mode-locked lasers (MLLs). This work demonstrates a four-channel TIPADC using on-chip electro-optic comb pulses from a monolithic thin film lithium niobate (TFLN) device to solve these problems. The on-chip source generates a 10 GHz repetition rate comb with >100 lines spanning >9 nm (within 10 dB). Achieving 40 GSa/s sampling rate, 40 GHz bandwidth (modulator limited) with effective number of bit (ENOB) of 4.53 bits (17.49 GHz) and 3.52 bits (38.49 GHz). The cavity-independent source integration strategy overcomes a key barrier in TIPADC scaling, paving the way for fully monolithic PADCs deployable in 6G mmWave and electronic sensing systems.

title: EAR-Net: Edge-Guided Adapter-Enhanced Recursive Fusion Network for Small Object Detection in Remote Sensing Imagery
authors: Yan Zhang, Qiang Wang, Ruihan Bai, Hui Li
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Small object detection in remote sensing imagery is challenging because tiny targets often suffer from large scale variation, weak boundaries, and heavy background interference. Although modern one-stage detectors are efficient, repeated down-sampling often removes fine boundary details. As a result, dense tiny objects may become difficult to separate in deep feature maps. Meanwhile, background textures such as roads, roofs, and shadows may look similar to small targets, leading to false detections. To address these problems, we propose EAR-Net, an Edge-Guided Adapter-Enhanced Recursive Fusion Network built on YOLOv11n. EAR-Net improves small object representation from three aspects. First, the Multi-Scale Edge-Guided Feature Injection Module (MSEG) uses Sobel-initialized learnable edge operators to provide explicit boundary guidance. Second, the Adapter-Enhanced C2PSA module introduces lightweight bottleneck adapters into the attention branch to suppress high-frequency background noise while preserving global context. Third, the Recursive Feature Fusion mechanism introduces a recursive “focus-distribute-refocus-fuse” process to reduce cross-scale spatial misalignment. Experiments on NWPU VHR-10, DIOR, RSOD, and HRRSD show that EAR-Net improves detection accuracy and localization precision while maintaining a practical balance between accuracy and efficiency.

title: S2C-Net: SAM2-Based Dual-Domain Feature Reconstruction and Semantic Decoupling for Tiny Remote Sensing Object Counting
authors: Hailong Ning, Haojie Li, Wuxia Zhang, Tao Lei, Yanping Chen, Xiaopeng Cao, Asoke K. Nandi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Remote sensing object counting (RSOC) is a fundamental task with significant potential applications in urban traffic monitoring, road planning, and public security. However, accurate counting remains challenging since tiny objects are easily confused with complex backgrounds in remote sensing (RS) scenarios. Recent vision foundation models (VFMs) have brought new opportunities to RSOC owing to their powerful ability in learning general representations. Nevertheless, VFMs cannot be directly applied to RSOC because of significant dual-domain gaps in both task and data. To address these challenges, this study proposes a SAM2-based dual-domain feature reconstruction and semantic decoupling network (S2C-Net) for RSOC. To bridge the dual-domain gaps between SAM2 and RSOC, a difference-guided spatial-frequency feature reconstruction strategy (D-SFFR) is designed. The D-SFFR strategy adapt the SAM2 encoder to the RSOC task by reconstructing spatial and frequency domain features at critical layers. To improve the countability, a semantic object decoupling module (SODM) is further introduced for alleviating semantic confusion between tiny objects and background noise. By constructing a mutually exclusive mapping between object and noise in the semantic space, the SODM can explicitly disentangle object semantics and background semantics. In addition, a multi-scale adaptive feature fusion strategy is employed to integrate semantic and structural information across different scales, thereby enhancing the discrimination and representation capability for dense tiny objects. Extensive experiments conducted on several challenging RSOC benchmark datasets demonstrate that the proposed S2C-Net consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods in counting performance. The code is available at https://github.com/xuptLHj/S2C-Net.

title: CubeSat Networks for Planning-Grade Environmental Intelligence in Power Systems
authors: Md Khairul Islam, Farzad Ferdowsi
publish: 2026 IEEE Texas Power and Energy Conference (TPEC)
abstract: Power system planning increasingly relies on timely, local environmental information to support scheduling, reserve allocation, contingency assessment, and preparation. Current space-based sensing options are not optimized for these regional, sub-hourly needs. Geostationary Earth Orbit (GEO) satellites provide continuous but coarse observations, while traditional large Low Earth Orbit (LEO) satellites revisit specific locations infrequently and at mission costs unsuitable for utilityscale planning. This work surveys GEO, LEO, and CubeSat architectures; introduces a symbolic cost-cadence framework to assess suitability for planning-grade environmental intelligence; and applies the framework to conceptual data from each architecture. The framework captures forecast-uncertainty reduction and annualized mission cost using an analytical feasibility condition that enables qualitative comparison independent of empirical calibration. Conceptual results demonstrate that CubeSats occupy the most favorable region of the cost-cadence design space, offering low annualized cost and scalable revisit when deployed in small constellations. GEO and traditional LEO platforms remain essential for national meteorology but are economically and temporally misaligned with state-level planning needs. The framework provides a foundation for future empirical evaluation upon the availability of utility-specific forecasting error parameters.

title: Microseis-WCDD:Wavelet-Conditioned Diffusion Denoising for Microseismic Data via Adversarial Guidance
authors: Liangliang Yao, Ying Shi, Yan Zhang, Wei Cao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Microseismic monitoring is a key technology in hydraulic fracturing of unconventional oil and gas reservoirs, with high-quality data essential for accurate detection and effective evaluation. To improve microseismic signals reliability and enhance event recognition accuracy, efficient denoising is crucial. Although recent deep learning approaches have improved microseismic denoising through powerful feature learning, diffusion-based methods still face several limitations, including high computational cost during sampling, limited ability to model non-Gaussian noise, and insufficient conditional constraints that may cause discrepancies between generated and real data distributions. To address these issues, this study proposes the Microseismic Wavelet-Conditioned Diffusion Denoising (Microseis-WCDD), a diffusion-based framework integrating wavelet conditioning and adversarial guidance. It systematically improves inference efficiency, modeling capability, while retaining the generative advantages of diffusion models: 1) Constructing an end-to-end signal prediction mechanism, combined with a wavelet domain multi-subband parallelization strategy, to significantly reduce sampling steps and improve inference efficiency; 2) Improving the forward diffusion process by incorporating mixed noise features to enhance the model’s adaptability in handling non-Gaussian noise; 3) Introducing a conditional diffusion mechanism and distribution consistency constraints to guide the model to focus on the key microseismic phase and achieve high-fidelity denoising. Experimental results on synthetic and field datasets demonstrate that the method achieves superior inference efficiency and denoising performance over existing methods, especially under low signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) conditions.

title: Compensation of Bending due to Gravity for MetOp-SG Scatterometer Antenna On-Ground Calibration at the DTU-ESA Antenna Test Facility
authors: Javier Fernández Álvarez, Jeppe M. Bjørstorp, Samel Arslanagić, Michael Mattes
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: The on-ground calibration of the MetOp-SG Scatterometer Antenna Subsystem (SAS) requires spherical near-field measurements of large and heavy antennas mounted on a roll-over-azimuth positioner. Due to the significant mass of the antenna under test, the supporting structure experiences gravity-induced deformation; this results in the near-field signal of the antenna under test to be measured in points that are non-uniformly distributed on the measurement sphere. This paper proposes a compensation technique based on a non-uniform 2D Fast Fourier Transform to compute the spectral representation of the irregularly sampled near-field signal. The near-field values at the nominal sampling locations are subsequently reconstructed from this spectrum by interpolation, enabling recovery of the field corresponding to the ideal measurement geometry. The method was validated using measured and simulated data from an antenna with characteristics comparable to those of the MetOp-SG instruments, demonstrating negligible reconstruction error, and robustness with respect to uncertainty of the bending angle. Then, the technique was successfully applied to real data from the MetOp-SG SAS FM2 near-field measurement campaign conducted at the Technical University of Denmark in 2022, confirming its effectiveness in practical measurement scenarios. Additionally, it is proposed that the method can be adapted to compensate for a variety of systematic measurement setup errors; this is demonstrated by applying the method to real measured data of a standard gain horn affected by an axis-intersection error.

title: Box Embedding With Probabilistic Reasoning for FOL Queries Over Knowledge Graph
authors: Zhongbin Li, Kun Yue, Jianyu Li, Lixing Yu, Zhiwen Tang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Emerging Topics in Computational Intelligence
abstract: The limitations of existing approaches for modeling first-order logic (FOL) queries over knowledge graphs (KGs) have motivated the development of probabilistic reasoning methods. While box embedding techniques provide a flexible geometric framework for complex query answering, they primarily rely on local operator-level transformations. As a result, it becomes difficult to strictly preserve logical constraints, particularly for negation and compositional reasoning under complex query structures. Moreover, approximation strategies such as simple averaging or neural merging operations may introduce accumulated errors during multi-step inference. To address these challenges, we propose an MLN-enhanced box embedding framework that integrates probabilistic logical reasoning into geometric query modeling. Specifically, we construct logical expressions from relations in KG and predicates in FOL queries, and develop corresponding Markov Logic Network (MLN) structures to capture global dependencies among entities and relations. These MLN-derived dependencies are then leveraged to guide the execution of projection, intersection, union, and negation operators in the box embedding space. To ensure scalability on large knowledge graphs with numerous entities, we further introduce a variational expectation–maximization (VEM) based MLN learning strategy, enabling efficient parameter estimation and inference. By jointly modeling the correlations between input boxes and newly generated boxes through MLN parameters, our approach aligns local geometric operations with global relational semantics. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method consistently outperforms existing approaches across multiple benchmark datasets.

title: Joint Antenna Rotation and IRS Beamforming for Multi-User Uplink Communications
authors: Guoying Zhang, Qingqing Wu, Ziyuan Zheng, Qiaoyan Peng, Yanze Zhu, Wen Chen, Penghui Huang
abstract: Rotatable antenna (RA) enhances wireless coverage through directional gain steering, yet suffers from performance degradation under physical blockages. Intelligent reflecting surface (IRS) establishes reflected paths to bypass obstacles, but is limited by angular mismatch when deployed in the side-lobe region of base station (BS) antennas. To address this issue, we propose a new RA-enabled IRS-assisted multi-user uplink system, in which the BS antennas are capable of flexibly adjusting their 3D orientations to align their boresights with the IRS. We formulate a sum rate maximization problem by jointly optimizing the antenna 3D rotations, receive beamforming and IRS phase shifts. To tackle this non-convex problem, we propose an alternating optimization (AO) algorithm. Specifically, we iteratively update the antenna rotations via projected gradient ascent (PGA), compute the receive beamforming via a closed-form solution, and optimize the IRS phase shifts via fractional programming (FP). Numerical results demonstrate that the proposed system yields significant performance gains over conventional fixed-antenna systems, especially under large angular misalignments.

title: Do I Have Your Attention Now? Supporting Attention in Dynamic Virtual Environments_supp1-3693478.mp4
authors: Chenkai Zhang
abstract: There are many problem domains where users have to pay attention to a series of targets. In this paper, we evaluated visual cues to support attention in dynamic situations in virtual environments. Three immersive visualizations (Emphasis, Deemphasis, Emphasis+Deemphasis) have been examined across four different attention types (sustained, alternating, divided, and selective attention) to support the user's attention on targets. Emphasis highlights targets of interest using outlines; Deemphasis removes details with masks, and Emphasis+Deemphasis combines both techniques. An experiment with 34 participants examined the cues through four scenarios that required different attention types in dynamic situations. Results indicated that while Emphasis supported significantly faster reactions in most attention types, the other two visual cues showed superiority across other measurements. Although evaluated in a virtual environment, the findings offer guidance for designing visual cues in other dynamic situations that involve different attention types.

title: Joint Analysis of DRX Power Efficiency and QoE for DASH Streaming over Cellular Networks_supp1-3693483.pdf
authors: Hung-Yu Wei
abstract: Discontinuous Reception (DRX) is a power-saving mechanism standardized by 3GPP and widely adopted in modern smartphones. As mobile networks evolve and service demands grow, rapid battery depletion remains a critical challenge, especially with Dynamic Adaptive Streaming over HTTP (DASH), the dominant application traffic in mobile networks. This paper introduces the first analytical model to capture the complex interplay between DASH Quality of Experience (QoE) and DRX power saving. Unlike existing approaches, our model incorporates crucial video streaming characteristics—including initial buffering, stalling, rebuffering, and video quality variation—providing a realistic representation of user experience. Validated through simulations and real-world experiments, the proposed model provides actionable insights for optimizing cross-layer interactions between DASH applications and DRX strategies, demonstrating the feasibility of achieving device power savings with real-time applications.

title: WEmarker: breast cancer-specific prognostic analysis with weighted multiplex network embedding
authors: Xingyi Li, Junming Li, Zhelin Zhao, Huihui Kong, Min Li, Xuequn Shang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Biology and Bioinformatics
abstract: In clinical trials, prognostic biomarkers have become essential for guiding treatment decisions after breast cancer surgery. Network-based methods have gained notable attention to reveal marker genes, but many existing methods only focus on a single network, which inevitably neglects the incompleteness of interaction relationships within the network. Even when based upon the multiplex network, most of methods directly integrate the multiplex network into an aggregated network and do not take into account the inherent noise in the biological networks, which can not preserve the topological structure of each original network very well. In this study, we propose a novel method, WEmarker, for breast cancer-specific prognostic analysis. WEmarker reduces the noise level of biological networks and quantifies the probability of interactions between genes, and represents the nodes in the weighted multiplex network as vectors while efficiently retaining the structure information of these networks for identifying prognostic biomarkers. The results show that WEmarker outperforms comparative methods and the case study also demonstrates that biomarkers identified by WEmarker have reliable biological interpretability for breast cancer prognosis.

title: Before and After the Switch: Exploring Contributor Levels when Vendor-Controlled Open Source Projects Go Proprietary
authors: Kevin Lumbard, Farhad Mohammad Afzali, Georg Link
publish: Proceedings of the 2026 ACM Southeast Conference
abstract: None

title: Integrated Mechanical–Electromagnetic Design and Experimental Validation of a High-Speed Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor Considering Sleeve Interference Fit and Harmonic Losses
authors: Sang-Hyeop Kim, Kyeong-Won Kwak, Jun-Ho Jang, Yong-Joo Kim, Kyung-Hun Shin, Jang-Young Choi
abstract: High-speed permanent magnet synchronous motors (HSPMSMs) are widely used in compact, high-performance applications, but their design is challenged by rotor mechanical integrity and accurate electromagnetic performance prediction under inverter-fed operation. Conventional approaches often assume ideal sinusoidal excitation, leading to inaccurate performance prediction. This paper proposes a harmonic-current-informed multiphysics design methodology for a surface-mounted HSPMSM with a retaining sleeve. An analytical rotor stress model considering interference fit, centrifugal force, and thermal expansion is validated using finite element method (FEM) simulations. Inverter-driven harmonic current spectra are predicted through circuit simulation and incorporated into electromagnetic analysis. The results show that harmonic currents significantly increase losses and reduce efficiency. Conventional sinusoidal-based analysis results in noticeable prediction errors, whereas the proposed approach achieves good agreement with experimental results. The proposed methodology improves prediction accuracy under realistic operating conditions and provides a reliable design framework for high-speed PMSMs.

title: Incipient Inter-Turn Short-Circuit Fault Diagnosis Based on Spatiotemporal Multibranch Graph Convolutional Network_supp2-3684175.mp4
authors: Qian Chen
abstract: Permanent magnet synchronous motors are widely used in various industrial drive systems. Accurately diagnosing inter-turn short-circuit (ITSC) faults in windings is a crucial prerequisite for ensuring the safe and stable operation of the drive system. Hence, this article proposes a spatiotemporal multibranch graph convolutional network (ST-MGCN) for the diagnosis of incipient ITSC faults. To address the challenge of weak fault features under complex conditions, ST-MGCN employs a three-branch architecture to simultaneously extract spatial, temporal, and global features from multichannel current data, thereby enriching the representation of fault features. A novel causality-driven fusion module adaptively reweights branch contributions and feature significances, preserving subtle incipient fault representations. Experimental validations on two distinct datasets exhibit the superior diagnostic accuracy and noise robustness of the proposed ST-MGCN. It demonstrates exceptional sensitivity to the weak features of the incipient ITSC fault. It maintains high performance under complex conditions, with varying speeds and load levels, outperforming the state-of-the-art methods.

title: CSI Feedback Based on Bi-Directional Channel Reciprocity Using Magnitude and Phase Separation
authors: Dan Liu, Jie Wu, Jindan Xu, Wei Xu, Derrick Wing Kwan Ng
abstract: In frequency division duplex (FDD) massive multiple-input multiple-output (mMIMO) systems, the uplink and downlink channel state information (CSI) exhibits an implicit reciprocal relationship, which can be leveraged for effective CSI compression and feedback. However, directly learning the reciprocity from complex-valued CSI matrices is prone to introducing irrelevant information or noise into the reconstructed downlink CSI, due to the high sensitivity of real and imaginary CSI components to frequency variations across the uplink and downlink. As the CSI magnitude is influenced by the propagation path common to both uplink and downlink, it demonstrates a strong degree of reciprocity than the real and imaginary parts of the CSI. To efficiently extract the reciprocal information, we propose a magnitude and phase separated CSI feedback neural network, named MPSCsiNet. Specifically, the proposed MPSCsiNet adopts a disentangled representation learning (DRL) network to accurately capture the reciprocal relationship between uplink and downlink CSI magnitudes, while applying a filtering approach to compress the essential information in the downlink CSI phases. Numerical results demonstrate that compared with the existing state-of-the-art AI compression feedback methods, MPSCsiNet can reduce the computational complexity by more than 80% while improving the accuracy of CSI recovery by approximately 4 dB, which verifies the advantages of integrating domain knowledge into deep learning (DL).

title: MDP3: A Multimodal 3D Diffusion Policy for Visuomotor Control
authors: Daifeng Li, Junwei Liu, Wei Zhang
abstract: Diffusion-based imitation learning has emerged as a dominant paradigm for robotic manipulation, offering stable training and multimodal action modeling. However, state-of-the-art diffusion policies predominantly rely on isolated sensory modalities—either 2D RGB images or 3D point clouds—limiting their robustness in unstructured environments where visual semantics and spatial geometry are jointly required. To bridge this gap, we propose a Multimodal 3D Diffusion Policy (MDP3), a unified framework that actively synthesizes heterogeneous sensory inputs for robust visuomotor control. MDP3 introduces two core architectural innovations: (1) a Gated Multimodal Unit (GMU) that acts as a context-aware arbitrator, dynamically modulating the fusion of RGB and point cloud features based on their real-time reliability; and (2) a Simplified PointNet++ (SPN2) encoder designed to inject local geometric inductive biases while maintaining the optimization stability required for diffusion training. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MDP3 significantly outperforms existing single-modality baselines. Specifically, it improves the average success rate by 10.9% over the leading 3D diffusion policy, exhibiting superior generalization under diverse lighting and geometric variations.

title: Power-Constrained and Quantized MIMO-RSMA Systems with Imperfect CSIT: Joint Precoding, Antenna Selection, and Power Control
authors: Jiwon Sung, Seokjun Park, Jinseok Choi
abstract: To utilize the full potential of the available power at a base station (BS), we propose a joint precoding, antenna selection, and transmit power control algorithm for a total power budget at the BS. We formulate a sum spectral efficiency (SE) maximization problem for downlink multi-user multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) rate-splitting multiple access (RSMA) systems with arbitrary-resolution digital-to-analog converters (DACs). We reformulate the problem by defining the ergodic sum SE using the conditional average rate approach to handle imperfect channel state information at the transmitter (CSIT), and by using approximation techniques to make the problem more tractable. Then, we decompose the problem into precoding direction and power control subproblems. We solve the precoding direction subproblem by identifying a superior Lagrangian stationary point, and the power control subproblem using gradient descent. We also propose a complexity-reduction approach that is more suitable for massive MIMO systems. Simulation results not only validate the proposed algorithm but also reveal that when utilizing the full potential of the power budget at the BS, medium-resolution DACs with 8 ∼ 11 bits may actually be more power-efficient than low-resolution DACs.

title: Root-Protograph-based Anytime Codes for Non-Ergodic Block Fading Channels
authors: Xiaoxi Yu, Zhaojie Yang, Yong Liang Guan
abstract: The study of anytime codes over non-ergodic block-fading (BF) channels remains largely unexplored. To address this gap, we propose a novel root-protograph-based anytime (RP-anytime) coding framework and develop modified density evolution (DE) equations. Leveraging these equations, we introduce two performance metrics to evaluate the asymptotic behavior of RP-anytime codes under the assumption of infinite frame length: the average asymptotic bit error rate (BER), which characterizes anytime reliability, and the anytime outage region, which reflects the code’s ability to achieve full diversity. Using these metrics, we analyze the performance of the proposed RP-anytime codes and compare them with both root-protograph LDPC codes and conventional anytime codes. The results demonstrate the superiority of the RP-anytime structure in terms of reliability and diversity over non-ergodic BF channels. Furthermore, we employ a multi-objective differential evolution (MODE) algorithm to design optimized RP-anytime codes. Simulation results confirm that the newly designed codes achieve excellent anytime reliability, full diversity, and near-outage-limit error performance—significantly outperforming prior-art root-protograph LDPC codes and anytime codes.
