abstract: This paper presents the design and implementation of a system that spots grid failures based on frequency and voltage thresholds. It uses an Arduino microcontroller, for use in buildings and renewable energy setups. The regular SCADA or EMS monitoring systems depend on central control. They take forever to catch faults and cost a ton for all that infrastructure. But this new way does real-time checking right on site. It handles loads automatically and shoots off instant alerts via GSM. Sensors for current and voltage keep tabs on power levels all the time. The Arduino runs the show, using whatever thresholds set for things like too much voltage, not enough, or weird frequency shifts. When something goes wrong, it flips relays to cut loads, sets off buzzers and lights for alarms, and texts operators through SMS. We tested it out in experiments. It caught faults super quick, cut down on downtime, and made everything run more reliably than those old central setups. Overall, this cheap, easy-to-scale, spread-out method boosts how tough the grid is against problems. It fits great for smart buildings and mixed renewable systems with wind or solar hooked in.

title: A 12–25GHz Ring-PLL Clock Generator Achieving 98fs Jitter and <–52dBc Spurs Using a 22fs Quadrupler and Sub-Sampling Error Extraction
authors: Mohamed Badr Younis, Mahmoud A. Khalil, Mohamed O. Selim, Mohamed S. Aly, Ruhao Xia, Ahmed E. Abdelrahman, Yongxin Li, Ahmed E. Hegazi, Pavan Kumar Hanumolu
abstract: This work presents a wideband, low-jitter ring-oscillator-based clock generator that combines a PLL with an edge-combining quadrupler. A novel sub-sampling error extraction method with a three-path LMS adaptation allows low-frequency, high-resolution correction of edge-combining errors. Fabricated in 16 nm FinFET, the prototype outputs 12–25 GHz clocks with worst-case spurs below –50 dBc across the entire band. At 25 GHz, it achieves 97.9 fs of integrated jitter with only 22 fs of quadrupler additive jitter while consuming 39.8 mW.

title: Mixed Reality Visualization of Abdominal Anatomy from CT Images Based on Bidirectional Optical Flow Interpolation
authors: Wei Zhang, Bo Yang, Youlong Lyu, Xing Zhou
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Advanced Algorithms and Control Engineering (ICAACE)
abstract: Mixed reality (MR)-based 3D visualization of abdominal anatomy is widely used in preoperative planning to provide intuitive spatial perception. High-quality and geometrically consistent 3D reconstruction from abdominal CT data is essential for achieving accurate spatial representation in MR environments. However, abdominal CT data often contain large inter-slice spacing, which leads to stair-step artifacts in 3D reconstruction. Existing interpolation methods are susceptible to errors with large inter-slice spacing, often resulting in blurred boundaries and structural discontinuities. To address this issue, an MR visualization method for abdominal anatomy based on bidirectional optical flow interpolation is proposed. The method first generates intermediate slices using optical flow estimation to compensate for sparse sampling, and then reconstructs the surface using an MC algorithm to obtain a high-quality triangular mesh. Experimental results show that the quality of the inter-layer slices generated by this method can meet the requirements for three-dimensional reconstruction of abdominal tissues. The reconstructed model can be visualized in a mixed reality environment, providing intuitive spatial perception of abdominal anatomy.

title: Design and Application of an Automatic Generation System for Primary Network Loss Reports
authors: Shi Liu, Jia Sun, Haitao Cui, Zehui Wang, Liang Zhao, Li Tian
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Advanced Algorithms and Control Engineering (ICAACE)
abstract: This paper introduces a system that uses Robotic Process Automation (RPA) programs to automatically generate and verify primary network loss reports for provincial power grid dispatching, along with the design ideas, system architecture, and application effects of the relevant RPA programs. RPA tools are used to write scripts that automatically capture and format data from the electric energy acquisition system, and obtain supplementary data through automatic We-chat conversations with power plant data managers. The formatted collected data is used to instantiate a copy of the primary network loss report template to realize the automatic generation of the initial report draft. The node and branch balance rates are used to automatically identify missing data in the initial draft, and the data obtained via We-chat is used to supplement the missing data in the initial draft, thereby achieving the automatic generation and verification of the primary network loss report.

title: Explainable Deep Learning for Autism Diagnosis from RNA-Seq Data Using Game-Theoretic Feature Analysis
authors: Tasaddi M. Hanoun, Asaad N. Hashim
publish: 2025 26th International Arab Conference on Information Technology (ACIT)
abstract: Diagnosing autism spectrum disorder (ASD) remains a challenging task due to the high-dimensionality of transcriptomic and sequencing data, such as RNA-Seq. Traditional machine learning models often fail to capture complex gene expression patterns and lack interpretability, limiting their clinical impact. In this study, we propose an interpretable deep learning framework based on a one-dimensional convolutional neural network (IDCNN) combined with a permutation-based feature attribution mechanism to enhance model transparency. The proposed approach effectively captures local and global expression patterns, while providing interpretable insights into the most influential features in the classification process. The following results highlight the potential of combining explainable AI with deep learning to develop data-driven biomedical applications, particularly in big data analytics and health informatics Experimental evaluation on benchmark RNA-Seq datasets (GSE59288 and GSE111176) demonstrated robust performance with an accuracy of 97.1 % and 95.5 % (AUC = 0.98 and 0.96, respectively).

title: Inductive Sensor for Real-Time, Autonomous and Non-Destructive Fruit Size and Growth Monitoring
authors: Lorenzo Mistral Peppi, Alessandro Gallo, Luigi Manfrini, Luca De Marchi
publish: 2025 IEEE International Workshop on Metrology for Agriculture and Forestry (MetroAgriFor)
abstract: This work presents preliminary studies of an innovative and low-cost sensing solution for real-time fruit growth measurement directly on the tree, overcoming traditional mechanical and electrical issues. Implementability of high resolution inductive angular sensing technique on a fruit caliper is evaluated while suitability of the same FR4 substrate as both PCB and structural material for the pliers is analysed. A 71 μm resolution over a 120 mm is achieved and a maximum diameter absolute error estimation lower than 247 μm is obtained exploiting a calibration procedure. Finally, a thermal compensation procedure which takes into account material expansion is proposed.

title: Low-Cost Plant Height Estimation: a Comparison of Image Processing and Deep Learning Methods
authors: Cristian Bua, Lorenzo Bianchi, Stefano Giordano
publish: 2025 IEEE International Workshop on Metrology for Agriculture and Forestry (MetroAgriFor)
abstract: This work presents and compares several methods for automatic plant Height Estimation in a controlled agricultural environment. The proposed approaches involve the detection and bounding of the plant using either image processing based on color segmentation or deep learning method based on the YOLO family architectures. Once the plant is framed, the pixel-to-centimeter conversion is performed using either a geometric model or a corrected formulation that accounts for camera distortions and tilt angle. Experiments were conducted with both horizontal and inclined camera setups, varying the distance and height of the device relative to the plant. The best results were achieved using the neural network combined with the direct conversion method, particularly when the camera was positioned at 3.5 times the plant height and inclined at approximately 25 degrees. This configuration yielded a Mean Absolute Percentage Error as low as 1.61% and a Mean Absolute Error of 1.01 cm. The proposed system shows potential for replacing manual measurements in precision agriculture, enabling real-time monitoring of plant development and optimizing resource management.

title: Intra-Leaf Hyperspectral Analysis Method for Reflectance Variability Identification
authors: Elisa Cioccolo, Pierluigi Rossi, Giuseppe Colla, Mariateresa Cardarelli
publish: 2025 IEEE International Workshop on Metrology for Agriculture and Forestry (MetroAgriFor)
abstract: Characterizations of leaf properties through hyperspectral imaging relies on signatures on specific bands that indicate heterogeneities in certain spots of parts of the leaves. These characteristics can also be affected by presence of pathogens, fertilizations practices and plant stress, that all present traits that can be invisible to the eyes but that can be detected in spectral data analyses. In order to detect such anomalies in specific leaf areas, authors developed a region-of-interest (ROI)–aware pipeline that processes hyperspectral information in partitions that belong to the border of the leaf, core or whole leaf area. A SPECIM IQ hyperspectral sensor was used to gather data in 204 bands over the range of 400-1000 nm on 8 samples of zucchini leaves (Cucurbita pepo L., Tevere hybrid, La Semiorto Sementi srl, Sarno, Italy), extracting a set of indices that are sensitive to pigments or leaf structure. The resulting 24 ROIs of at least 1.600 pixels each displayed different spectral behaviors: results showed that, compared with whole-leaf data, leaf marginal and core ROIs reduced band-wise standard deviation by up to 35%; secondly, spectral analyses revealed wavelength-dependent shifts in green, red-edge and near infra-red (NIR) domains as ROI changed; lastly, a correlation analysis showed that several hyperspectral indices differed significantly between leaf blade and leaf midrib (p < 0.05), where spectral indices of leaf marginal ROIs reported a strong negative correlations with chlorophyll a (r = –0.79) and carotenoid (r = –0.74), plus a positive correlation between nitrogen and VIS-integral or Absolute Derivative Sum indices (r = 0.77). These findings demonstrate that intra-leaf spectral analyses can improve the interpretability of hyperspectral information, also providing a form of in-sample data augmentation that can increase trait prediction under variable plant growing conditions.

title: Reinforcement Learning-based Optimization for Cold Chain Electric Vehicle Routing
authors: Haoran Yang, Wanli Yi, Renqian Zhang
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Advanced Algorithms and Control Engineering (ICAACE)
abstract: To address the range limitations and hard time window constraints in cold chain electric vehicle delivery, this article proposes EGT-TA, an end-to-end deep reinforcement learning framework designed to minimize comprehensive energy consumption costs while satisfying complex constraints. The model utilizes an Edge-augmented Graph Transformer (EGT) encoder to deeply integrate node and topological features. Furthermore, a dynamic urgency-biased mechanism is incorporated into the decoder to dynamically adjust node selection probabilities by perceiving remaining time windows, significantly enhancing the model's control over timeliness. Additionally, a constrained POMO training algorithm based on Lagrangian relaxation is introduced to guide the agent in adaptively balancing solution feasibility and optimality during exploration. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed model outperforms Genetic Algorithms and classic Attention Models in solution quality across instances of varying scales.

title: A DTC-Based Digital PLL Achieving –64.5dBc Fractional Spur and 80fs Jitter with a 2-Track Probability-Density-Shaping ΔΣ Modulator and a Dithered-Threshold TDC
authors: Riccardo Moleri, Daniele Lodi Rizzini, Michele Rossoni, Damiano Fagotti, Pietro Salvi, Stefano Gallucci, Andrea Leonardo Lacaita, Salvatore Levantino, Simone Mattia Dartizio
abstract: A fractional-N digital PLL employing a two-track probability-density-shaping ΔΣ modulator and a dithered-threshold TDC is presented. The first technique suppresses spurs arising from quadratic DTC nonlinearity while minimizing the required DTC delay range. The second technique mitigates spurs induced by TDC nonlinearity and enables accurate calibration of TDC resolution. The prototype achieves – 64.5dBc fractional spurs and 80fs jitter, representing the first DTC-based PLL using a stochastic ΔΣ modulator to achieve sub-100fs jitter.

title: A Simplified Intelligent Bike Alarm System: Cost-Effective and Modular Design
authors: Adnan Shaout, Melanie Kukura
publish: 2025 26th International Arab Conference on Information Technology (ACIT)
abstract: The current market for bicycle alarm systems offers no shortage of options; however, many commercial systems are burdened by a complex array of features and integrations that not only inflate costs but can also compromise reliability. This paper presents a novel intelligent bike alarm system characterized by significantly reduced complexity and straightforward implementation. The proposed design achieves lower system cost and enhanced maintainability, while also providing a modular base architecture that can accommodate future enhancements. Prioritizing such requirements is essential in the design of a soft real-time system tailored for a simple bike alarm application.

title: Operational Safety and Reliability of Wind Power Systems Under Extreme Weather and Lightning Strike Conditions
authors: Zhongbing Duan, Li Zhang, Guan Wang, Huaixiang Wang, Temnikov Alexander, Lysov Nikolay
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Smart Grid and Green Energy (ICSGGE)
abstract: Under the impetus of carbon neutrality goals, wind power has become a core component of renewable energy, with its grid-connected scale continuously expanding and operating environments becoming increasingly complex. This paper systematically investigates the operational characteristics of wind power systems under multiple operating conditions, focusing on grid-connected voltage stability, energy storage integration, and extreme weather impacts. The analysis encompasses the effects of wind speed randomness and grid topology on voltage stability, application modes of energy storage in wind farms, and the influence of meteorological factors during typhoon events on wind power output. The results provide theoretical and technical support for the safe and efficient operation of wind power systems.

title: An Enhanced FLC Based Frequency Regulation Control Scheme for Sending-End System of Hybrid MTDC System
authors: Zhidi Huang, Guangqiang Peng, Jiyang Wu, Ze Gong, Xi Zhang, Qinhao Yang, Hao Ying, Zhen Wang
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Smart Grid and Green Energy (ICSGGE)
abstract: The transmission of renewable energy sources, such as wind and PV generation via UHVDC transmission, has become an important means for renewable energy utilization in China. Thus, how to coordinate multiple power sources to participate in frequency regulation in the hybrid multi-terminal DC (MTDC) transmission system will become a big challenge. In this paper, a sending-end system frequency regulation scheme based on an enhanced frequency limit control (FLC) strategy is proposed, which is composed of a two-level control framework. At the windfarm level, a joint frequency regulation control is developed at the sending-end wind farm by combining a battery energy storage system (BESS) with the wind turbine generator's (WTG) deloading strategy. At the system level, a combination control scheme by integrating FLC with DC-voltage droop control is designed for the LCC-HVDC sending-end converter station to improve frequency decoupling between the sending and receiving ends. The effectiveness of the proposed scheme is validated on a threeterminal hybrid MTDC test system with an aggregated wind farm, and simulation results demonstrate an improved frequency performance and reduce the receiving-end system's frequency deviation produced by the sending-end power disturbances.

title: Evaluation of the Young’s Modulus of Silicon Carbide from Specific Heat Measurements
authors: Vincenzo Vinciguerra, Giuseppe Luigi Malgioglio, Marco Renna
publish: 2026 27th International Conference on Thermal, Mechanical and Multi-Physics Simulation and Experiments in Microelectronics and Microsystems (EuroSimE)
abstract: We present a novel approach to determine the Young’s modulus of silicon carbide (SiC) samples through specific heat measurements. Our study demonstrates that the temperature dependence of the experimentally measured specific heat can be accurately described using the Debye phonon model over a relevant temperature range. By developing the Debye model, we derive a universal relationship that enables the extraction of the Debye temperature directly from the specific heat data. Theoretically, the Debye temperature is intrinsically linked to the longitudinal and transverse (shear) acoustic wave velocities within the crystal lattice. Since the Young’s modulus is also fundamentally related to these elastic wave velocities, we establish a direct analytical correlation between the Young’s modulus and the Debye temperature. Utilizing literature values for the acoustic wave velocities in SiC, we validate our method by showing that the Young’s modulus values obtained via this approach are consistent with those reported in the literature. This work offers a reliable technique for evaluating the mechanical properties of SiC, using bench-top instrumentation, with potential applications in material characterization and quality control.

title: Engineering-Oriented ESG Text Accounting: A WHO-Optimized Machine Learning Framework for Disclosure Quality Diagnostics
authors: Ziyuan Jia, Kaiyuan Bai
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Advanced Algorithms and Control Engineering (ICAACE)
abstract: This paper addresses the engineering challenge of converting large-scale ESG narratives into computable and robust disclosure-quality indicators. Using 8,471 sentence-level Chinese ESG samples with quality and topic annotations, we build a Wild Horse Optimizer (WHO) and machine-learning integrated framework for text accounting analysis. The framework combines character-level TF-IDF features, WHO-based hyperparameter search, dual-task classification, and a three-dimensional disclosure index covering quantity, specificity, and verifiability. Experimental results show macro-F1 scores of 0.824 and 0.801 for quality and topic classification, respectively. Correlation diagnostics further indicate that the disclosure index is strongly and positively associated with specificity and verifiability, while it is strongly and negatively associated with an earnings-management risk proxy. The proposed pipeline provides a reproducible engineering route for subsequent firm-year linkage studies, including accrual quality, abnormal accruals, audit fees, and litigation risk. The revised version further adds an explicit reproducibility protocol, operational threshold governance, and validity-boundary discussion, so that the contribution is not limited to model accuracy but is directly actionable for disclosure monitoring deployment.

title: Invited Talk: From Chip to AI Factory-A Five-Year Roadmap for Silicon Suppliers and Hyperscalers
authors: Gamal Refai-Ahmed, Mohammed Shaheen, CW Chen
publish: 2026 27th International Conference on Thermal, Mechanical and Multi-Physics Simulation and Experiments in Microelectronics and Microsystems (EuroSimE)
abstract: AI infrastructure is entering a critical scaling phase in which sustained, deployable performance is increasingly constrained not by peak compute capability, but by system-level integration across silicon, packaging, cooling, power delivery, facilities, and operations. As accelerator power density rises and heterogeneous integration accelerates, through chiplets, advanced 2.5D/3D packaging, and high-bandwidth memory (HBM) performance bottlenecks shift into memory bandwidth, power delivery networks, and thermo-mechanical interfaces. At the same time, AI deployment is expanding beyond centralized training clusters toward hybrid cloud, regional inference, and edge environments, creating a multi-topology reality that favors repeatable, serviceable reference architectures over bespoke, peak-optimized designs.This paper presents a five-year roadmap (2026–2030) for silicon suppliers and hyperscalers that reframes AI scaling around the concept of the AI factory: a co-designed system spanning chip, package, server, rack, cooling distribution, power, and facility operations. We show that junction-to-surface thermal resistance, interface stability under warpage and clamp-load variation, and manufacturability and serviceability constraints increasingly dominate achievable power density and sustained utilization. As a result, thermo-mechanical integration emerges as system architecture, rather than a secondary validation concern.The paper consolidates industry developments into six overarching themes: (i) system-level integration rules serve as a key competitive advantage; (ii) hardware-software co-design and enhanced observability drive operational efficiency; (iii) memory bandwidth and data movement represent the primary system-level expenses; (iv) thermo-mechanical enablement determines the feasibility of multi-kilowatt packages; (v) the selection of cooling technologies should align with maturity and operational risk; and (vi) considerations regarding energy, water, and carbon constitute fundamental design constraints. A gated execution framework is introduced that aligns cooling modality, interface validation, mechanical robustness, and qualification rigor with increasing power density, emphasizing extension of mature single-phase liquid cooling and KPI-driven pilots for higher-risk technologies.The central conclusion is that future AI infrastructure scaling depends less on nominal silicon advances and more on the ability to industrialize system-level integration rules, instrument the full stack, and scale through disciplined replication. Organizations that adopt this system-centric approach will achieve higher sustained utilization, lower total cost of ownership, and faster deployment velocity, while those that do not risk turning peak silicon capability into stranded or unreliable capacity.

title: Large Language Models in the Diagnosis of Squamous Cell Carcinoma: The State of the Art, Problems, and Future Perspectives
authors: Jaishree Meena, Vishan Kumar Gupta, Harish Shakya, Paras Jain, Harish Tiwari, Kokila Kokila
abstract: Squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) is a common type of cancer that develops in the squamous cells that represent the lining of different organs, such as the skin, lungs, and the digestive tract, and poses a great challenge clinically and in terms of diagnosis as it is a heterogeneous disease with varying courses. Over the past few years, large language models (LLMs)-powered artificial intelligence systems that are able to comprehend and produce human language have also become promising instruments to aid cancer diagnostics, such as the detection and assessment of SCC. This paper gives an overview of the latest development of LLMs in SCC diagnosis, placing them in the context of their development, adoption, and influence on the overall environment of oncology and precision medicine. The important findings of the emerging body of research can be used to justify the clinical applicability of LLMs in SCC diagnosis and promote the use of the technology in personalizing care pathways and optimizing the speed of the diagnostic process. In the future, the next generation of AI in oncology is expected to be more explainable, flexible, and fully integrated into multidisciplinary care teams, which will be a great leap towards precision therapy and the enhancement of patient outcomes in SCC.

title: Fair and Robust Federated Learning Via Evolutionary Incentives
authors: Fatemeh Shabani, Rasool Esmaeilyfard
abstract: Federated Learning (FL) enables privacypreserving collaborative model training in autonomous vehicle networks; however, strategic, self-interested participation, together with data heterogeneity, threatens fairness, efficiency, and long-term sustainability. Most existing solutions do not jointly address adaptive participation, equitable contribution assessment, and robustness against malicious clients. In this paper, we propose a new evolutionary game-theoretic framework for FL that integrates (i) a Shapley value–based incentive scheme for fair contribution measurement, (ii) an Exponential Moving Average (EMA)–based reputation mechanism to promote long-term stability, and (iii) an enhanced replicator dynamic to steer participation strategies toward a stable equilibrium. Experiments under realistic heterogeneous and adversarial settings show that the proposed method improves model accuracy over baseline approaches, effectively reducing the influence of malicious participants, and achieves a more equitable reward allocation. These findings suggest that our framework provides a sustainable and efficient solution for federated learning in intelligent transportation systems.

title: Mapping the Knowledge Structure of Blockchainand AI-Enabled Open Banking: A Co-Occurrence Analysis
authors: Seyyed Ali Doorafshan, Soraya Birami
abstract: The convergence of Blockchain and Artificial Intelligence (AI) is fundamentally reshaping Open Banking, yet the existing literature remains fragmented. This study systematically maps the intellectual structure and co-evolution of these technologies through a bibliometric co-occurrence analysis, providing one of the first integrated mappings of AI-blockchain convergence in Open Banking. After rigorous metadata cleaning using OpenRefine, 100 Scopus-indexed documents (2018-2025) were analyzed. Utilizing VOSviewer and Python, the research identifies publication trends and geographic impact patterns. The analysis reveals six distinct thematic clusters: (1) Decentralized Identity and Access Control, (2) AI-driven Fraud Detection and Risk Management, (3) Smart Contract Governance, (4) Financial Inclusion and Regulatory Frameworks, (5) Cryptographic Security Infrastructure, and (6) Behavioral Adoption and User Experience. The results indicate clear maturation from conceptual models toward systemic integrations. While India and China lead in publication volume, Germany and Australia demonstrate higher citation impact. The study concludes that the field is shifting toward decentralized, regulation-aware financial ecosystems, highlighting future research directions in crossborder data sovereignty, real-time DeFi risk modeling, and behavioral adoption through Nudge Theory and autonomous smart contracts.

title: Correlation Mapping of Sanitation Access and Public Health Outcomes in Tamil Nadu
authors: Deepalakshmi Rajendran, M. Surya, Annie Darling Kanmani A, Dharun R, Isac Reegan G, Danish Sugarstan S
abstract: Provision of proper sanitation is a key factor that defines the health of the general population, especially the developing world, like the Tamil Nadu region of India. The paper will look at how the availability of sanitation infrastructure and major public health indicators relate in the districts of Tamil Nadu using Geographic Information Systems (GIS) and statistical analysis. Public health outcomes of sanitation were combined with the data of the district level on sanitation facilities as well as child mortality rates and waterborne diseases and socio-demographic factors including literacy levels, income, and urban-rural composition. Spatial analysis was used to depict disparities in regions and enumerate areas of high risk needs to be addressed urgently using GIS. The strength and nature of the associations between the access to sanitation and the health outcomes were evaluated with the help of the statistical methods such as correlation and regression analyses. The findings indicate a significant positive correlation between the lack of proper sanitation facilities and increase in disease rates especially in children with statistically significant results <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(p<0.01)$</tex>. Education and income were identified to moderate the outcomes of health however the greatest predictor of the status of the public health in any district was the access to sanitation. The research points out that there is an urgent practical problem of sanitation infrastructure and hygiene education in underexplored areas. Sanitation planning must be incorporated with the overall community health strategies to achieve better disease burden about the well-being of the community in Tamil Nadu.

title: IoT and Cloud Enabled Agriculture Farming Predictions and Monitoring System
authors: Aditya Dubey, Dhananjay Bisen, Vishwas Engle
abstract: The integration of cloud computing technology with IoT devices has become the standard approach to tackling contemporary agricultural issues. The principal intention of this study is the creation and development of an IoT and cloud-based agricultural farming prediction and monitoring system. In real time, it collects data from the environment and its risks through various sensors such as temperature, soil moisture, humidity NPK (nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium), water flow, and fire detection sensors. Using an open-source Blynk IoT platform to track and visualize the sensor and monitoring data, the platform offers farmers insights that are both easy to understand and application-oriented. The paper discusses the methodological side of the making of the IoT and cloud-based agriculture system, including hardware selection, sensor integration, data visualization, and machine learning model creation as aspects. Implementation proves that the system is able to intervene in actual farming settings and thus disrupt the traditional farming practices. Like this research presumes further discussion on the use of the IoT and cloud computing in agriculture, stressing the need for data-driven decisionmaking in sustainable agriculture.

title: Intelligent control of DFIG for variable-speed wind turbine generation system
authors: Ibrahim Yaichi, Aoumri Mohammed, Khayra Roummani, Patrice Wira, Kouddad Elhachemi, Anissa Ticherahine
publish: 2025 First International Conference on Mechanical and Electrical Engineering for Green Energy Technologies (MEEGET)
abstract: In the context of electricity generation, power quality is characterized by waveform integrity, harmonic distortion levels, and service continuity. When interfacing with the electrical grid, the power delivered must meet optimal performance criteria. To address the issue of sensitivity to parametric variations, we have developed control strategies that estimate active and reactive power solely based on electrical quantities measurable at the rotor. Among these strategies, the intelligent control method known as Neural Network-based Direct Power Control (NN-DPC) has demonstrated competitive performance. Conventionally, Direct Power Control (C-DPC) is implemented using a switching table. In contrast, the proposed NN-DPC approach offers a fast and robust power response. Simulation results comparing the conventional C- DPC and the proposed NN-DPC under various step changes in active and reactive power demonstrate the effectiveness of the latter. Additionally, to ensure consistent contribution to primary frequency control regardless of wind speed, a Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT) strategy based on Proportional-Integral (PI) control is introduced to optimize power capture. The simulations are conducted on a 1.5 MW Doubly-Fed Induction Generator (DFIG)-based Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS), and the performance of the proposed control strategies is evaluated under various operating conditions.

title:
Reexamination of the Crystal Electric Field in SmCo
                    <sub>5</sub>
authors: F. De Almeida Passos, G. Nilsen, J. Larrea Jiménez
publish: 2025 IEEE Latin American Conference on Nanotechnology (LANANO)
abstract: In light of recent improvements in the theory of rare earth <sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">magnets</sup>, we reexamine the magnetic single-ion properties of SmCo<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">5</inf> associated with the crystal electric field (CEF) parameters, which are obtained by fitting the existing inelastic neutron scattering data with a model recently used for NdCo<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">5</inf>. Our results show that a higher-order ${{\text{A}}_6}^6 < {{\text{r}}^6} > $ CEF parameter may be also considered motivating additional high-resolution inelastic neutron experiments and new theoretical investigation to unveil the low-energy CEF excitations responsible for the special magnetic properties in rare-earth magnets.

title: Handwritten Test as the Image Data for MS Diagnosis, a New Persian Dataset
authors: Leila Soleimanidoust, Abdalhossein Rezai, Hamideh Barghamadi, Iman Ahanian
abstract: Multiple Sclerosis (MS) is a neurodegenerative disease that impairs motor and cognitive functions, making early detection essential. Handwriting analysis offers a non-invasive and accessible way to capture subtle motor irregularities associated with MS. In this study, our primary contribution is the development of a structured Persian offline handwriting dataset, collected under four cognitive-motor stress conditions to support future research on handwriting-based MS assessment. To demonstrate the usefulness of this dataset, we applied standard image-processing techniques and several machine-learning classifiers to evaluate whether handwriting features can differentiate MS patients from healthy individuals. These preliminary classification results are not intended as a clinical diagnostic system but rather as an initial validation of the dataset's potential. Overall, the findings show that handwriting-based analysis can provide meaningful signals for MS screening and highlight the value of the proposed dataset for advancing research in both clinical and resource-limited environments.

title: Research on Data Anomaly Detection Algorithm Based on Deep Learning
authors: Yuetong Wang, Tian Tian, Hongyan Ge, Haoyu Jiang, Chen Zhang
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Advanced Algorithms and Control Engineering (ICAACE)
abstract: In the era of big data, accurate anomaly detection from massive time series data is a key challenge. Traditional methods have limitations when dealing with high dimensional and nonlinear time series data. A hybrid model combining long-term memory networks and self-encoders is proposed for anomaly detection in multivariate time series. The model constructs encoder-decoder structure by stacking Long Short-Term Memory Network (LSTM) layers, and realizes anomaly discrimination according to reconstruction error. Experiments on multiple public datasets show that the model outperforms traditional methods and benchmark models in accuracy, F1 score, AUC-ROC and other indicators. Robustness analysis further revealed that LSTM-AE exhibited greater stability in the presence of noise or missing data.

title: Fishing Vessel Anomaly Behavior Recognition in Open Waters Based on Active Learning
authors: Tan Luo, Zhaoguo Mai, Jianglin Liao, Yaochi Zhao, Zhuhua Hu
publish: 2025 4th International Conference on Advanced Sensing, Intelligent Manufacturing (ASIM)
abstract: In open water scenarios, fishing vessel behavior recognition models require continuous updates to adapt to dynamic environmental changes, which poses significant challenges for expert annotation costs. To address the high annotation burden in model updates, this paper proposes an active learning method based on Self-Balanced Hard Examples Diversity Sampling (SBHEDS) for fishing vessel anomaly behavior recognition. This method combines margin uncertainty sampling with diversity selection to achieve optimal balance between sample informativeness and diversity. SBHEDS utilizes model prediction probability space as low-dimensional features to calculate sample similarity, reducing computational complexity while ensuring sufficient dispersion of selected samples in feature space. Experiments show that SBHEDS achieves 79.27% recall on the HN_PT_1 dataset for double-trawling detection tasks, improving by 1.22% compared to existing active learning methods while significantly reducing annotation costs.

title: Human Activity Recognition Based on Wi-Fi CSI via Dual-Channel Feature Fusion
authors: Jiaqi Wei, Liang Chen, Ying Liang
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Advanced Algorithms and Control Engineering (ICAACE)
abstract: Wi-Fi channel state information (CSI)-based devicefree human activity recognition (HAR) has shown great potential in smart healthcare and smart home applications because of its low cost, privacy preservation, and non-intrusive nature. However, existing methods are still sensitive to environmental noise and multipath interference, which limits their robustness in recognizing fine-grained similar activities. To address this issue, this paper proposes PA-TCNet, a dual-channel Transformer network that jointly exploits phase and amplitude features. The model introduces learnable positional embedding and relative position attention to enhance temporal and channel dependency modeling, and further employs a Kolmogorov-Arnold Network for nonlinear feature fusion. Experimental results on public and selfcollected datasets demonstrate that the proposed method achieves high accuracy and strong robustness across different indoor environments.

title: Analytical System-Level Model for Optimization of pMUT Based Acoustic Power Delivery for Implants
authors: Oğuz Kaan Erden, Bogdan Vysotskyi, Chris Van Hoof, Xavier Rottenberg
publish: 2026 27th International Conference on Thermal, Mechanical and Multi-Physics Simulation and Experiments in Microelectronics and Microsystems (EuroSimE)
abstract: To achieve efficient ultrasonic power transfer using piezoelectric micromachined ultrasonic transducers (pMUTs), both interdependent electrical matching and distance dependent acoustic coupling must be considered together. Exploring this design space with full-wave multiphysics simulations alone can become computationally demanding, especially when multiple parameters are involved. In this work, we develop a frequency domain system-level model for a single Tx–Rx pMUT link that integrates electrical terminations, electromechanical conversion, acoustic radiation and coupling into a mixed-domain two-port framework. The propagation path is described by a closed-form propagating wave transfer impedance, which allows rapid sweeps over frequency and normalized spacing d/λ. To identify practical matching conditions, we further introduce a power landscape procedure that evaluates the power delivered across the complex load plane on both the Rx and Tx ports. The corresponding target impedances can then be selected and used for Impedance Matching Network (IMN) synthesis. The overall approach is compared with COMSOL simulations for different device radii and wavelength normalized separations. In these cases, the model reproduces the main trends in peak transferred power while reducing the evaluation time by orders of magnitude.

title: Practice of Full-Process Management and Control Technology for Cross-Regional Pumped Storage Projects Based on BIM and Internet of Things
authors: Jipeng Liu, Wangxing Hu, Jian Chen, Tao Li
publish: 2025 4th International Conference on Advanced Sensing, Intelligent Manufacturing (ASIM)
abstract: To address industry pain points in pumped storage project construction, such as difficulties in cross-regional collaboration, challenges in concealed engineering management and control, and poor synchronization between quality and progress, this study takes the Digital and Intelligent Management and Control Platform for Pumped Storage of Xinhua Power Generation under PowerChina as the research carrier, integrates technologies including BIM, GIS, and the Internet of Things (IoT), and constructs an intelligent construction system covering the entire lifecycle of survey, construction, management and control, and operation and maintenance; this system comprises six core modules, namely intelligent dam rolling, intelligent grouting, intelligent underground powerhouse robots, safety management and control for underground powerhouse excavation and support, intelligent ventilation, and dynamic material balance, enabling integrated management and control of five cross-regional power stations including Henan Wuyue, Henan Jiufengshan, Gansu Huanglong, Xinjiang Burqin, and Xinjiang Ruoqiang; by developing key technologies such as a BIM-driven rolling monitoring system, an AI-based core recognition model for grouting quality evaluation, and a 7*24-hour robot monitoring system for underground powerhouses, the system resolves issues in traditional construction like delayed data collection, subjective quality evaluation, and passive safety early warning; application results show that the platform significantly enhances the automatic data collection capability for real-time construction progress, quality, and engineering quantity, reduces manual reporting workload, has been successfully selected into the 2024 Typical Cases of IoT Empowering Industry Development by the Ministry of Industry and Information Technology (one of the five national typical cases in the field of "intelligent construction"), and provides a replicable technical paradigm for the intelligent construction of pumped storage projects.

title: Static and Dynamic Characteristics Analysis of Truck Frame Based on Finite Element Method
authors: Yi Zhang, Bin Zheng, Qiyang Shui
publish: 2025 4th International Conference on Advanced Sensing, Intelligent Manufacturing (ASIM)
abstract: This article takes the truck frame as the research object, adopts finite element analysis method, uses ANSYS as a tool, and uses Solidworks to establish a three-dimensional model of the frame to study its dynamic and static characteristics. Firstly, establish a finite element model of the vehicle frame and perform mesh division. Subsequently, static analysis was conducted, including four working conditions: bending, twisting, braking, and turning. The analysis results showed that the deformation and stress under each working condition were within an acceptable range. However, specific parts such as the second and third crossbeams may fracture under long-term use or specific conditions, and the strength needs to be appropriately increased. Finally, modal analysis is conducted to determine the natural frequency and modal form of the frame, and the modal shape diagram is used to understand the vibration mode form of the frame during free vibration, providing guidance for optimization design. The analysis results show that the first-order modal frequency of the frame avoids the main excitation frequency during car driving, and the later frequencies are also offset from the environmental excitation frequency. The frame basically meets the requirements of dynamic characteristics, and the structural design meets the standards.

title: Research on Friction Power Loss Prediction Method Based on Multilayer Perceptron Regression
authors: Yumin Wang, Yixuan Feng, Li Su, Yi Qian
publish: 2025 4th International Conference on Advanced Sensing, Intelligent Manufacturing (ASIM)
abstract: The friction power loss of specialized equipment is a crucial parameter that affects the equipment's temperature field and separation performance. It is also an essential parameter for designing the motor's output power and one of the measurable values reflecting the operating status of specialized equipment. Currently, the values of friction power loss are mainly obtained through electromechanical experiments or complex theoretical calculations. However, electromechanical experiments consume a large amount of manpower, material resources, and time, while theoretical calculations require numerous parameters and involve complex processes. To address these issues, this study proposes a multilayer perceptron regression algorithm based on the Bayesian optimization algorithm to establish a friction power loss prediction model. This model predicts friction power loss by analyzing the measurement index parameters during the production and assembly of specialized equipment. Through this method, the prediction of the equipment's friction power loss can be achieved without conducting electromechanical experiments, reducing the waste of manpower, material resources, and time, and laying a foundation for equipment quality determination and screening.

title: Integrated Energy Management Models for Hot Rolling Processes Based on Renewable Energy: a Review of Mathematical Methods and Application Conditions
authors: Yancheng Zhou, Xiangtao Li, Jinghan Gu, Chengsheng Wang, Xunhua Yuan
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Smart Grid and Green Energy (ICSGGE)
abstract: This review was intended to analyze the mathematical models for energy management in hot rolling processes within the steel industry, specifically addressing the dual challenges of intensified load fluctuations and reduced grid inertia under the context of large-scale integration of renewable energy. The technical framework spanning from internal energy efficiency optimization of the rolling process to stability control of the external power grids was summarized herein. Following an “insideout” logical thread, a comprehensive discussion was presented on key mathematical methods, including thermodynamic baseline models for hot rolling, physical modeling of rolling mills, machine learning-based load forecasting, and the dynamic dispatching of core components in renewable energy microgrids. Theoretical support was clarified by this work for energy conservation in hot rolling processes, as well as for the stable operation of industrial microgrids with high renewable energy penetration. Finally, it was highlighted that significant room for improvement remained in existing energy management models regarding global multiscale collaborative optimization, real-time uncertainty management, and practical human-machine coordination mechanisms. Future research should be focused on cross-domain model fusion and technological innovation to improve the efficiency of renewable energy utilization in hot rolling processes and facilitate energy conservation and carbon reduction in the steel industry.

title:
Numerical SCAPS Comparison of Sb
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    Se
                    <sub>3</sub>
                    and SnSe Thin-Film Solar Cells
authors: Roby Mohajon, Neha Bhowmik, Md Rabiul Islam Polash, Md. Nabil Shahriar, Anupom Bhowmick, Rampada Rudra
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Trends in Material Science and Inventive Materials (ICTMIM)
abstract: Interfacial recombination and inefficient carrier extraction have historically presented challenges to the efficiency and thermal stability of chalcogenide thin-film solar cells. This study investigates the motivation behind these issues through numerical simulations. Using the SCAPS-1D tool, the effects of absorber layer thickness were examined in devices with the configuration FTO/TiO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>/absorber/NiOx/CuSCN/back contact for both SnSe and Sb<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>Se<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf> thin-film solar cells. The maximum power point characteristics and temperature effects were also investigated. The optimized SnSe solar cell achieved a short-circuit current density of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$41.98 \text{mA}/\text{cm}^{2}$</tex>, a fill factor of 80.34%, and a power conversion efficiency of 26.92%, while the optimized Sb<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>Se<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf> solar cell exhibited a short-circuit current density of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$37.59 \text{mA}/\text{cm}^{2}$</tex>, a fill factor of 77.67%, and a power conversion efficiency of 24.50%. Both cells utilized an absorber layer thickness of 900 nm and 1000 nm, respectively. The introduction of the NiOx nano-interlayer helps to improve the band alignment between the absorber and transport layers. The introduction of the NiOx nano-interlayer reduces the Shockley-Read-Hall recombination and stabilizes the maximum power point voltage. The introduction of the NiOx nano-interlayer improves the thermal stability. It is important to note that the efficiencies reported herein represent theoretical limits and may not fully reflect practical performance. The introduction of the NiOx nano-interlayer is anticipated to improve the efficiencies of both SnSe and Sb<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>Se<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf> thin-film solar cells.

title: Author Index
authors: N/A
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Advanced Algorithms and Control Engineering (ICAACE)

title: Design of Interval Observers for Large-Scale Switched Systems
authors: Rui Qin, Xingzhi Zhou
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Advanced Algorithms and Control Engineering (ICAACE)
abstract: For a class of large-scale switched systems, complex operating conditions and parameter uncertainties pose challenges to state estimation. This paper studies an interval state estimation method based on interval observers, focusing on asynchronous switching caused by transmission delays of switching signals. An interval observer is constructed to achieve the upper and lower bound estimation of system states. By using multiple Lyapunov functions and the average dwell time method, the stability of the observation error system is rigorously proved. Simulation results show that the proposed method can accomplish effective state estimation in the presence of external disturbances and model uncertainties, and is applicable to asynchronous switching scenarios.

title: BEV-RCF: Bird's-Eye-View-Based Radar-Camera Fusion for 3D Object Detection
authors: Xiong Duan, Huiwei Wang
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Advanced Algorithms and Control Engineering (ICAACE)
abstract: Autonomous driving demands robust 3D perception under challenging conditions. Vision-only systems are low-cost but suffer from depth errors in poor lighting or adverse weather such as fog, rain. To overcome this, we propose BEV-RCF, a multimodal framework fusing millimeter-wave radar and multiview cameras for accurate, semantically rich bird's-eye-view (BEV) representations. It refines monocular depth via Multimodal Depth Integration Network (MDIN), which integrates radar depth cues and Radar Cross Section (RCS) heatmaps. Radar points are enhanced by Radar Transformer Encoder (RTE), modeling local pillar structure and long-range spatial dependencies. Both modalities are lifted into BEV space and fused by Multi-scale BEV Fusion (MBEVF) through a hierarchical strategy: grid-level alignment via Multi-head Deformable Cross-Attention (MDCA), and instance-level refinement using selfattention followed by MDCA. On nuScenes with ResNet-50, BEV-RCF achieves 54.4% NDS and 45.9% mAP, establishing a new state-of-the-art among radar-camera fusion methods and demonstrating the effectiveness of our depth-aware, multi-level fusion design in real-world driving scenarios.

title: Smart Energy Management Strategy for Load Prediction Utilizing a Hybrid Energy Management Strategy
authors: Hashem Mustafa Al-smadi, Takialddin Al Smadi, Mousa Bani Baker, Mohammed Hiyassat
publish: 2025 26th International Arab Conference on Information Technology (ACIT)
abstract: Accurate short-term load forecasting (STLF) is pivotal for optimizing energy distribution and enhancing grid reliability in smart grids. This study proposes a hybrid energy management strategy integrating Autoregressive Integrated Moving Average (ARIMA) and Artificial Neural Networks (ANN) to address challenges of fluctuating consumption patterns, particularly in data-scarce regions like Jordan. We analyze 20 months of energy data from 1,500 subscribers in two Jordanian towns-Deir Alliyeat (urban, Zone A) and Maqableh (rural, Zone B)-classifying patterns into linear (20%) and non-linear (80%) categories. ARIMA models linear trends, while ANN handles non-linear complexities; a hybrid ARIMA-ANN model synergizes both approaches. Results demonstrate the hybrid model's superiority, achieving a Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) of 0.7 % and Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) of 10 kW, outperforming standalone ARIMA (MAPE 9.91%, RMSE 38.59 kW) and ANN (MAPE 1.4 %, RMSE 22.6 kW). The hybrid approach effectively manages Jordan's energy challenges, including high non-linear consumption and grid inefficiencies. Recommendations include adopting hybrid forecasting in smart grids, enhancing data acquisition, and integrating renewables to reduce costs. This work offers a scalable solution for regions with similar infrastructural dynamics.

title: A 6–85 GHz Non-Uniform Distributed Variable Gain Amplifier Achieving High Output Power and Efficiency in SiGe HBT Technology
authors: Burak G. Özat, Mathias P. Scharpf, Marc Günter, Maximilian Heckele, Benjamin Schoch, Ingmar Kallfass
abstract: This paper presents a non-uniform variable gain distributed amplifier implemented in 130-nm SiGe HBT BiCMOS technology. The design incorporates five cascode gain cells (GCs) with transistor sizes systematically scaled down along the signal path. Initial GCs utilize larger devices to extend the linear operation range and improve power efficiency. Further linearity enhancement and a reduction in effective input capacitance are achieved by introducing 15Ω resistive emitter degeneration in each GC, which also mitigates the need for highly inductive transmission lines, especially for the larger GCs. The design was validated by on-wafer measurements up to 110 GHz. The distributed amplifier demonstrates an average gain of 13 dB with a 79 GHz 3-dB bandwidth. Large-signal continuous wave measurements between 10 – 47 GHz yield a peak output-related 1-dB compression point (OP1dB) of 13.3 dBm and a power-added efficiency (PAE) of 11.62% at 17 GHz. The chip occupies only 0.62 mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> and consumes 176.4 mW power.

title: SiGe HBT Optimizations for Power Amplifiers in G-band
authors: Nicolas Eyer, Sébastien Sadlo, Philippe Ferrari, Sylvain Bourdel, Didier Belot, Alexandre Siligaris
abstract: In this paper, a methodology is given to design the optimal SiGe heterojunction bipolar transistor (HBT) for a power amplifier (PA) in the G-band around 300 GHz. The methodology is split into two main parts. In the first part, transistor’s topology and emitter’s width are addressed with a small signal study. It is done on common-emitter (CE) and common-base (CB) architectures with theoretical equations and simulations. For both cases, the "Nx(CBEB)-C" topology with a minimal emitter’s width is shown optimal, leading to a higher gain. In the second part, trade-off between emitter’s length and number of emitters is addressed through large signal simulations on a cascode architecture. These simulations show that increasing the total emitter’s area increases the P1dB at the expense of gain. In addition, it is shown that for a same emitter’s area, a transistor with 2 emitters delivers the best performances. All simulations use a "npnxhs" HBT fabricated by STMicroelectronics in 55 nm "BiCMOS55X" technology (F<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">t</inf> / F<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">max</inf> = 350 GHz / 500 GHz) with V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">BE</inf> = 0.88 V and V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">CE</inf> = 1.4 V.

title: An Energy-Efficient Edge-Assisted Monitoring System for Youth Plyometric Training
authors: Qi Niu, Tao Li
publish: 2026 5th Asia Conference on Algorithms, Computing and Machine Learning (CACML)
abstract: Sustainable youth sports call for data-driven, low-overhead training schemes. This study presents a 12 -week edge-intelligent plyometric training system integrating wearable inertial sensors (MPU-9250 IMU) with Raspberry Pi edge nodes. The system enables real-time load monitoring and localized data processing, significantly improving both change-of-direction (COD) and jumping performance in youth athletes. Sixty male adolescent athletes (14-16 y) were randomly assigned to an experimental group (three 45-min plyometric sessions per week) or a control group (standard PE). Illinois Agility, 505 COD, counter-movement jump and standing long jump were measured pre- and post-intervention. The experimental group significantly outperformed the control group in all four tests <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(p<0.01$</tex>, Cohen's <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{d} \geqslant 0.8)$</tex>, with energy consumption reduced by 64% compared to traditional weight-training facilities. Pearson analysis revealed strong correlations between jumping and COD improvements <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(r =0.65-0.68)$</tex>, indicating that enhanced lower-limb explosiveness simultaneously benefits both skills. Edge-localized processing minimized data transmission overhead, demonstrating a scalable, resource-efficient approach for school-based athletic development. The results provide sustainable, green-computing evidence for integrating short-duration, high-efficiency plyometrics into youth athlete development programs.

title: An on-chip Split Ring Resonator for Graphene Dielectric Permittivity Characterization
authors: Zhichu Cao, Naveen Kolluru, Marcel Andree, Arjith Chandra Prabhu, Xinpeng Du, Zhuang Miao, Jonas Woeste, Michael Gensch, Daniel Neumaier, Ullrich R. Pfeiffer
abstract: This paper presents an on-chip split ring resonator (SRR) for graphene dielectric permittivity characterization. SRR shows a permittivity based frequency shift. The SRR is comprised of a LC-tank for resonant frequency tuning and a sensing strip for permittivity measurement. The SRR operates at a resonance frequency of 372.2 GHz, with a transmission loss of -10 dB, a Q factor of 9.8 and an area of 140μm × 250μm. When graphene is placed on the top of SRR, 39.6 GHz resonant frequency shift is measured. By correlating the simulation result from a 3-D full-wave EM solver (HFSS) and the measured frequency shift, the relative permittivity of graphene is characterized. The relative permittivity of graphene at 372.2 GHz is estimated to be ϵ′ = 2.95×10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">5</sup>, while the theoretical absolute permittivity of CVD graphene at 372.2 GHz is in a range of 1.88 × 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">4</sup> and 5.61 × 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">5</sup>.

title: A 129.9-141.1 GHz VCO Quadrature Frequency Generator Using Frequency Division and Mixing
authors: Manuel Koch, Jens Löffler, Chiara Kohl, Andre Engelmann, Sascha Breun, Robert Weigel, Norman Franchi
abstract: This paper presents a local oscillator signal generation scheme at D-band frequencies for homodyne transmitters and transceivers that require quadrature phases and reduced susceptibility to oscillator pulling by the integrated power amplifiers. A voltage-controlled oscillator at two-thirds of the output frequency is used to drive a static frequency divider that generates quadrature phases. Based on the divider outputs, subsequent mixing stages generate quadrature D-band signals. Individual characterization of the W-band oscillator core shows a tuning range from 86.7 to 94.3 GHz while the divider-based quadrature generator’s input frequency range covers the complete W-band and achieves < 2° divider output phase error at most frequencies. The oscillator-enabled quadrature generator operates over a 11.2 GHz (8.2 %) tuning range from 129.9 to 141.1 GHz, achieving a phase noise of -78 dBc/Hz at 1 MHz offset and a quadrature phase error of 6.5°. The circuits are fabricated using 130 nm BiCMOS technology.

title: High-Performance MPPT for Photovoltaic Systems Using Bald Eagle Search-Based Solar Cell Parameter Estimation
authors: Hassan Shokouhandeh, Hossein Gholipour Godarzi, Reza Hassankhani, Mahan Jafarian, Kamran Yousefpour, Mahmood Hosseini Imani
publish: 2026 IEEE Texas Power and Energy Conference (TPEC)
abstract: In this paper, a novel method based on an estimated solar cell model is proposed for maximum power point tracking (MPPT) of a photovoltaic (PV) system. In the proposed approach, the double diodes solar cell model is first estimated using experimental data, with the Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) criterion serving as the objective function, by an improved version of the Bald Eagle Search (BES) algorithm. Then, the estimated model is employed to track the maximum power point (MPP) of the PV system under various operating conditions. Simulation results in MATLAB demonstrate that the proposed MPPT achieves faster tracking and higher power extraction from the PV system but also exhibits lower ripple compared to Perturb and Observe (P&O) method. Moreover, the optimal PV cell parameter estimation results indicate that the proposed BES algorithm achieves better performance in estimating the PV cell parameters compared to other algorithms.

title: Visual Differential-Spatially Projected Transformer for Efficient Hyperspectral Images Super-Resolution
authors: Binfeng Wang, Tao Zhang, Ying Fu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Single hyperspectral image super-resolution aims to restore high-resolution hyperspectral images (HSIs) from a single low-resolution HSI. Existing methods often struggle with inefficient spatial attention mechanisms and inadequate modeling of cross-spectral dependencies, which hinder the simultaneous achievement of high restoration accuracy and computational efficiency. To address these limitations, we propose an efficient yet powerful architecture named Visual Differential-Spatially Projected Transformer (VDSPT). Instead of conventional spatial attention, VDSPT introduces Visual Differential Spatial Attention (VDSA), which computes two distinct attention maps and subtracts one from the other to suppress overemphasized regions. This differential strategy enables more balanced and effective extraction of spatial features. Additionally, we design Spatially Projected Spectral Attention (SPSA), which projects features along the spatial dimension before computing spectral attention while preserving the natural ordering of spectral channels. This leads to more robust and reliable modeling of inter-channel spectral relationships. By stacking only a few VDSA and SPSA modules, VDSPT achieves the state-of-the-art super-resolution performance with remarkable computational efficiency. Extensive experiments on both natural and remote sensing hyperspectral datasets demonstrate that VDSPT consistently outperforms existing methods in terms of quantitative metrics and visual quality. The source code will be publicly released.

title: Plug-and-Play Distributed Fencing of Multi-Vehicle Systems for Multiple Uncooperative Targets With Dynamic Radius
authors: Song Jiang, Jiang Zhao, Luning Yang, Pei Chi, Yingxun Wang
abstract: This paper investigates the multiple target fencing problem of multi-vehicle systems (MVSs), addressing a group of uncooperative targets (GUT) with an unknown dynamic target radius. A plug-and-play distributed multiple target fencing strategy is proposed that operates independently of global information, comprising a distributed fencing center observer, a distributed fencing radius estimator, and a distributed balanced fencing controller. Under this strategy, all vehicles can observe a fencing center within the GUT's convex hull and a dynamic fencing radius in finite time. Moreover, dynamic balanced fencing for the GUT is achieved, ensuring the convergence of all vehicle-center distances to the dynamic fencing radius and an equal angle distribution among vehicles. Furthermore, the plug-and-play feature of this strategy enables vehicles to join or leave the MVS and new targets to join the GUT during the fencing process. In contrast, the remaining vehicles can still achieve the multiple target fencing without requiring parameter adjustments for activated vehicles. The strategy's efficacy is validated through rigorous theoretical analysis and corroborated by numerical experiments.

title: Dynamic Modeling, Selection, and Placement of Mitigation Devices for HEMP/GMD Events
authors: Timothy J. Donnelly, Connor A. Lehman, David G. Wilson
publish: 2026 IEEE Texas Power and Energy Conference (TPEC)
abstract: A high-altitude electromagnetic pulse (HEMP) or solar-geomagnetic disturbance (GMD) can drive low-frequency geomagnetically induced currents (GICs) that force large power transformers into saturation, distorting AC waveforms, increasing losses, and risking thermal damage. This paper develops dynamic models of large synthetic grids that capture the transient response to simulated HEMP insults, then applies multi-objective optimization to quantify the trade-off between mitigation-device type, placement, and resulting transformer saturation. The study shows that flux-blocking devices provide the steepest reduction in relative saturation for a given device count, and that incorporating cost weights enables analysis of trade-offs between competing mitigation technologies and deployment strategies.

title: Design and Development of an End-Effector for Fabric Edge Folding Along a Convex Curve With Multiple Pleats
authors: Akira Seino, Akinari Kobayashi, Fuyuki Tokuda, Kai Tang, Kazuhiro Kosuge
abstract: In this article, we propose a novel end-effector designed for folding fabric edges along convex curves. Edge folding is essential in garment manufacturing for fabric parts, such as pockets, front panel hems, and cuffs before sewing. Existing automated edge folding systems rely on templates or fixtures designed for specific fabric parts. These systems must be reconfigured to perform edge folding of different fabric parts. In order to solve this problem, we developed a robot fabric edge folding system along straight lines using the end-effector for free-form folding (F-FOLD) in our previous study. In this article, we propose a new end-effector designed to fold fabric edges along convex curves and straight lines. When the fabric edge is folded along convex curves, generating multiple pleats is necessary to form a smooth folded edge. In this article, we first analyze the pleats generation mechanism and design a new robot end-effector, F-FOLD2, that actively generates pleats during the folding process along convex curves. Experiments show that fold width, rather than curvature, affects the number of pleats, and the average deviation from the desired folding line is within 1.0 mm. These results demonstrate that the proposed F-FOLD2 enables accurate, template-less folding along convex curves and straight lines, offering an alternative solution for flexible, automated garment manufacturing.

title: Global Optimization of AC Transmission Expansion Planning with Series and Shunt Reactive Power Compensation
authors: Etornam Komla Ahorsu, Mahdi Mehrtash
publish: 2026 IEEE Texas Power and Energy Conference (TPEC)
abstract: This paper presents a method to find the globally optimal solution for AC transmission expansion planning (ACTEP) that jointly optimizes transmission line investments, controllable series (e.g., thyristor-controlled series capacitor), and shunt reactive power compensators. A convex reformulation of the nonconvex power flow equations induced by series compensators is presented. McCormick envelopes are employed to convexify the bilinear interactions between series compensation and voltage-dependent variables in the relaxed lower-bound model. This approach eliminates linearization approximations and metaheuristic dependencies, enabling convex optimization. A hierarchical solution algorithm combines a lower-bound problem, upper-bound problems, and a no-good cut technique to obtain solutions with a guaranteed optimality gap. Validation on a modified 46-bus Southern Brazilian test system demonstrates that joint series and shunt compensation can significantly reduce investment costs, generation costs, and power losses relative to line-only expansion, while achieving global optimality with tractable computation.

title: Lightweight Forensic Triage Framework for Clandestine Operational Instructions: A Two-Case Leave-One-Case-Out Evaluation
authors: Sanghyeob Ko, Gibum Kim
abstract: Digital forensic casework rarely proceeds under laboratory conditions. Under time and resource constraints, analysts often triage thousands of seized text files, with critical documents embedded in large volumes of irrelevant material. This study presents a lightweight triage approach for prioritizing clandestine operational instruction (COI) documents in CPU-only deployment environments. The method combines TF-IDF features, a LinearSVC classifier, a tunable decision threshold, and SHAP-based analyst-facing rationale. Cross-case transfer was evaluated with a leave-one-case-out (LOCO) protocol using 133 court-adjudicated COI documents from two independent investigations and 500 synthetic negatives constructed as a structured stress-test contrast set. Across the two folds, the model achieved a mean accuracy of 99.29% and a mean F1 of 98.28%, with 5 false negatives in total (fold-wise FNR: 2.27% and 4.49%; pooled FNR: 3.76%). End-to-end screening required 0.32 ms per document, with a 1.34 MB model footprint on a single-threaded CPU. Comparisons with transformer-based and lightweight baselines showed that this configuration provided a favorable balance of accuracy, latency, model size, and feature-level auditability under CPU-constrained conditions. A proxy false-positive evaluation on 9,606 Korean Wikipedia paragraphs from KorQuAD v1.0 yielded a false-positive rate of 0.31% (95% CI: 0.21%–0.45%). Work Saved over Sampling reached 79%. The system is intended as a screening aid within a tiered forensic workflow. SHAP outputs are designed to support analyst review rather than serve as standalone evidentiary instruments. The findings are limited to the evaluated Korean-language counterintelligence cases and have not been validated across other languages, crime types, or jurisdictions.

title: The Synergy of Giants and Scouts—Edge-Enabled Collaboration Between Large-Scale and Lightweight Models in Sensor-Cloud Networks
authors: Tian Wang, Yao Liu, Geyong Min, Nektarios Georgalas, Yan Zhang

title: Remote Sensing and Graph Neural Network Reveal Past and Future Glacier Changes in Buha River Basin (1966–2100)
authors: Xinde Chu, Shoukat Ali Shah, Hanxiao Yuan, Yaxuan Jin, Changhong Hu, Songtao Ai
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: The Buha River is the primary tributary to Qinghai Lake, the largest inland lake in China, and its basin glaciers are critical to regional hydrological stability. However, their dynamic evolution and future trends have not been adequately quantified. This study quantifies glacier changes in Buha River Basin from 1966-2022 using remote sensing data and introduces Cryosphere Graph Temporal Model (CryoGTM), a novel hybrid model integrating Graph Neural Networks and Long Short-Term Memory, to project future evolution under four CMIP6 scenarios. Results show that CryoGTM achieved high accuracy in historical reconstruction, with an absolute error of 1.05 km² and a relative error of 9.30%. Between 1966 and 2022, glacier fragmentation increased the total count from 22 to 26, while the total glacier area shrank from 15.39 ± 0.94 km² to 7.34 ± 0.56 km². Similarly, ice volume declined at a rate of -0.0082 ± 0.0019 km³ a⁻¹, with 0.27 ± 0.05 km³ remaining by 2022. Projections under the four CMIP6 scenarios suggest that by 2100, the glacier area will decrease by an average of 60.01% compared to 2022. These findings provide valuable insights for water resource management and glacier change forecasting in the Buha River Basin.

title: Acoustic Sensor-Based Azimuthal Far-Detection with Shear Waves: A Review
authors: Gao X H, Wen Y Y, Zhang M S, Zhang W H, Li Y H, Zhang Z H, Hei C, Liu P
abstract: Dipole shear wave far-detection technology can effectively identify the azimuth of geological bodies outside the wellbore, which is a core technology for fracture characterization and reservoir evaluation in oil and gas exploration and geothermal engineering. However, the inherent structural symmetry of traditional dipole sensors leads to a 180° azimuthal ambiguity in the reflected wavefield, which has long been a fundamental hardware bottleneck restricting the precise imaging of fractures and formation interfaces. To address this limitation, this review systematically examines the innovative acoustic sensor designs and systems developed to resolve azimuthal ambiguity from a sensor-centric perspective, which is fundamentally different from traditional algorithm-focused reviews. The reviewed technologies are classified into four categories: phase difference decoupling through multi-azimuth receiver sensor array innovation, asymmetric acoustic systems via source-receiver spatial layout optimization, beamforming focusing using phased array sensors, and multi-mode excitation sensor systems integrating monopole and dipole sources. For each technology, we systematically analyze its core symmetry-breaking mechanism, hardware-algorithm dependence, quantitative performance, and inherent limitations. On this basis, we establish a unified comparative framework for mainstream technologies, and clarify the evolution law of azimuth identification sensor technology. Finally, we anticipate the future development path of next generation sensor systems towards wideband, intelligent, and multi-physical field fusion, emphasizing that the next-generation sensor system is the core driver for quantitative reservoir evaluation

title: Techno-Economic Optimization of a Grid-Tied PV System with Virtual Storage for a 1 GW Tier IV Data Center in NEOM, KSA
authors: Ali Awad Nasser, Mehrdad Ehsani
publish: 2026 IEEE Texas Power and Energy Conference (TPEC)
abstract: This paper presents a techno-economic optimization of a grid-tied photovoltaic (PV) system for a 1 GW Tier IV data center in NEOM, Saudi Arabia, targeting 50% renewable energy for Vision 2030. Using actual NEOM meteorological data and an NSGA-II optimization, the optimal <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2,050 \text{MW}$</tex> PV system achieves a 50.0 % renewable fraction, a 14.8 % LCOE reduction, and a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$35.0 \% \text{CO}_{2}$</tex> emissions decrease compared to grid-only operation. The system maintains Tier IV reliability via dual grid feeds without battery storage, achieving a PUE of 1.269. Under SEC tariffs and a net-billing framework, annual exports of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1,071 \text{GWh}$</tex> yield $38.6 million in revenue. Results confirm that grid-tied PV with net billing is a cost-effective, reliable, and sustainable pathway for hyperscale data centers in solar-rich regions, even when accounting for significant grid interconnection infrastructure costs.

title: APaC: a Context-aware and Availability Policy as Code Framework for Elastic Object Storage
authors: Ignacio Castillo-Barrios, J. L. Gonzalez-Compean, Ivan Lopez-Arevalo, Jose Juan Garcia-Hernandez
abstract: Replication strategies provide availability for cloud storage, yet existing policies typically rely on static popularity metrics. These works struggle to react dynamically to contextual workload variations, specifically the temporal increments and decays in user concurrency and data consumption. This paper introduces APaC, a framework that combines a context-reactive replication method with an Availability Policy-as-Code programming model for storage systems to autonomously update availability policies and adapt at runtime to workload fluctuations. The APaC model defines replication policies through primitives that specifies who, what, where, how, and when should replicate data, which is interpreted and executed by a configurable and reactive replication system without modifying the storage system basis code core. Under scenarios of read/write intensity and concurrency variation, dynamic adjustments to availability policies enabled effective responsiveness of storage to workload changes, which yielded high throughput rates, and lower capacity consumption. APaC outperformed state-of-the-art policies, achieving gains of 16.6% over time-distribution-based, 26% over object-lifetime-based, 34.6% over popularity-based strategies, and more than 70% over the baseline configuration that produce three replicas for each five nodes. It was also observed that performance gains increased with higher levels of concurrency, underscoring the effectiveness of dynamically managing availability policies.

title: Assessing Impact of PV Forecasting Accuracy and Data Redundancy on Kalman Filter-Enhanced Economic Dispatch: A Comparative Study
authors: Ahmed M. Abouassy, Mahdi Mehrtash, Sami Fadali, Hanif Livani
publish: 2026 IEEE Texas Power and Energy Conference (TPEC)
abstract: Renewable energy resources (RESs) are essential components of modern power systems, offering substantial benefits in reducing carbon emissions and improving system reliability if their generation is forecasted accurately. However, their performance is highly dependent on the accuracy of ambient condition measurements and forecasts, which are often corrupted by noise.
