The findings show that an ensemble-based random forest consistently outperforms the other models (mean F1 0.88) while balancing accuracy and interpretability, whereas SVMs deliver competitive accuracy on reduced feature sets and ANNs capture non-linear patterns but require larger training samples and explainability safeguards. Feature-importance analysis highlights that previous appraisal scores, goal-completion rate, absenteeism days and training hours are the most predictive variables across models. We conclude with a roadmap for responsible adoption of ML-driven performance prediction and highlight research avenues in fairness, crosscultural generalisability and integration with other HR functions.

title: Automated Brain Tumor Classification Using ConvNeXt-Tiny and Colorized MRI: A Deep Learning Approach With Enhanced Interpretability
authors: Mohammad Arabiat, Khaled Hamad, Rayan Abuqaddom, Lana Alissa, Nawaf Alshdaifat, Suhaila Abuowaida, Ahmad Aburomman
publish: 2025 26th International Arab Conference on Information Technology (ACIT)
abstract: Brain tumors are among the most lethal and aggressive forms of cancer, making early and accurate diagnosis all the more crucial for improving treatment responses. In this study, we introduce a deep learning-based methodology to classify brain tumors based on colorized MRI images by using the ConvNeXt-Tiny model with four different categories of glioma, meningioma, pituitary tumor, and normal. In particular, we trained the model with a public dataset from Kaggle, removing the original preprocessing steps and instead using CLAHE for histogram equalization and balancing data of 2 classes. The proposed technique was able to yield up to 98 % classification accuracy using the test set. Another beneficial technique to generate feedback is visual inspection–Grad-CAM was used to visualize the discriminative regions from the heatmap, which ensures that models are making decisions in a predictable way and also increases our confidence in model outputs. This was a strong suggestion towards the potential of transformer-based CNNs for the medical imaging arena.

title: Cloud Computing Technology Innovation for SMEs Women Entrepreneurs: A Study on SLA Violation
authors: Barah Al-Awasa, Mokhled S. AlTarawneh, Abla Suliman Husien, Mohammad Rasmi Al Mousa
publish: 2025 26th International Arab Conference on Information Technology (ACIT)
abstract: Cloud Computing (CC) has emerges as one of the most discussed topics among enterprise Information Technology (IT) professionals. Cloud Computing Services (CCS) are increasingly affecting business innovation resources for women-led Small and Medium Enterprises (SME). But women entrepreneurs sometimes face concerns when adopting CC technology in their business because there are many cases where there is a violation of Service Level Agreement (SLA) with customers and they are providing service at a lower level than what was agreed upon. Therefore, this paper will propose a requests redistribution mechanism using a cloud-analyst simulator based on the Eclipse IDE for the Java platform to check for violations that prevent cloud requests from reaching the cloud user during the Response Time (RT) agreed onto in the SLA. This paper will use simulation and modeling techniques based on the results of a descriptive analysis of the required SLA factors. Thus, the broker specification for the simulated parts will be extracted by the requirements of SMEs in terms of response time commitment to cloud requests. In line with this, 626 cloud requests were reallocated to four data centers (DCs) during the one hour simulation period, and it was found that the rate of violation requests in DC1 is 9 %, and the rate of a violate in DC2 is 11 %, the rate of a violate in DC3 is 13 %, and the rate of a violate in DC4 is 7 %. According to the obtained results, the proposed algorithm has proven its ability to achieve Cloud Service Broker (CSB) which considering the SLA violations to avoid sending incoming requests to the offending data centers

title: FEM-based Design Guidelines for the Thermal Management of PCBs with Paralleled GaN HEMT Devices
authors: Antonio Pio Catalano, Ciro Scognamillo, Vincenzo d’Alessandro, Paweł Górecki
publish: 2026 27th International Conference on Thermal, Mechanical and Multi-Physics Simulation and Experiments in Microelectronics and Microsystems (EuroSimE)
abstract: In GaN HEMT-based power electronics with paralleled device configurations, proper PCB thermal design is critical to mitigate self-heating and mutual thermal coupling between devices, both of which limit current density and assembly reliability. This paper presents simulation-based guidelines for the optimization of geometrical and layout features of PCBs. Extremely detailed 3-D thermal-only FEM simulations in COMSOL Multiphysics are adopted to study realistic PCB layouts for energy conversion and quantify the impact on thermal resistances of (i) spacing between devices, (ii) copper trace thickness, and (iii) layout topology. The proposed guidelines enable thermal-aware PCB design in order to improve the reliability of the assemblies.

title: Physics Informed Neural Network (PINN) for Multi-Scale Thermal Transport in AlGaN/GaN HEMT Heterostructures
authors: Shehryar Imtiaz, Francesco Stranieri, Lisa Mitterhuber-Gressl, Marco Mauri, Stefano Mariani
publish: 2026 27th International Conference on Thermal, Mechanical and Multi-Physics Simulation and Experiments in Microelectronics and Microsystems (EuroSimE)
abstract: Understanding of thermal behavior in high electron mobility transistors (HEMTs) is critical for device reliability and performance at nanoscale dimensions where thermal resistance creates significant temperature discontinuities at material interfaces. Traditional analytical methods are employed to capture this behavior in multi-layer semiconductor heterostructures as a common practice. This research addresses the question: can a Physics Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) accurately predict temperature profiles through a multi-layered AlGaN-GaN HEMT stack while respecting interface physics? Using the PINNs, we have developed an architecture that employs a fully connected feedforward neural network. The model was trained for 773K using stratified sampling across the complete stack consisting of GaN, AlGaN and AlN layers through physics-based loss function that incorporates steady state heat conduction equation. The interface conditions use flux continuity and interface conductance (which is represented by hBD) to calculate temperature discontinuity. Another novelty in this model is the adoption of a three-stage curriculum learning strategy. This implementation of PINN for a thermal conductance problem has resulted in encouraging progress with losses exhibiting a stable convergence pattern, without any kind of biased behavior. The resulting temperature field captures the overall thermal behavior and thus converts a purely analytical task being accomplished by a physics informed neural network.

title: Neural Network-Based Reduced-Order Model for Wind Pressure Prediction in Structural Engineering
authors: Bohan Jia, Yuhang Dong, Zhiwei Zhang
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Advanced Algorithms and Control Engineering (ICAACE)
abstract: Wind pressure prediction is essential for the structural design of high-rise buildings, yet traditional methods such as wind tunnel testing and Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) simulations are time-consuming and computationally expensive. This paper proposes a neural network-based reduced-order model (NN-ROM) for rapid wind pressure prediction on high-rise building surfaces. The proposed model takes building geometry parameters and wind conditions as inputs and outputs the surface pressure coefficient distribution in real-time. A fully-connected deep neural network architecture is designed and trained on CFD simulation data, then fine-tuned with wind tunnel experimental data using a transfer learning strategy. The input features include aspect ratio, building height, wind direction, reference velocity, surface roughness, and turbulence intensity. Comprehensive experiments are conducted to evaluate the model performance. Results demonstrate that the proposed NN-ROM achieves a coefficient of determination (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$R^{2}$</tex>) of 0.976 with a root mean square error (RMSE) of 0.078, significantly outperforming conventional machine learning methods including Random Forest, Support Vector Regression, and XGBoost. More importantly, the computational time is reduced from several hours to less than 0.1 seconds, achieving a speedup ratio exceeding 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">5</sup>. The proposed method provides an efficient and practical tool for preliminary wind-resistant design of buildings, particularly suitable for rapid assessment and disaster prevention planning in typhoon-prone regions.

title: Implementation and Prototyping of Self-Aiming Unmanned Vehicles on Dynamic Decision-Making and Cascade PID Control
authors: Zhiyuan Pan, Zhenning Tan, Lei Chen, Xi Cao
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Advanced Algorithms and Control Engineering (ICAACE)
abstract: Unmanned vehicles have broad applications in military, security, and industrial inspection. The design of self-aiming unmanned vehicles faces difficulties in automation and high precision. Traditional single-stage PID control often suffers from large response delays, severe overshoot, and high steady-state errors. This paper proposes a cascade PID control method based on dynamic decision-making. We established an inner-outer loop cooperative control structure by integrating data from the vehicle's sensors and the camera's feedback. Moreover, a piecewise function is introduced to save the computing cost, enabling adaptive adjustment of PID parameters. Numerical simulation demonstrates the proposed methods reduce overshoot, shorten regulation time, and enhance system robustness and real-time performance in complex environments. This study provides an effective engineering solution for a high-precision autonomous aiming vehicle on low-cost embedded platforms.

title: Visual Assessment of Metallic Mesh Wheel Condition Integrating Terramechanics Testing with Semantic-Enhanced Deep Learning
authors: Hongjun Wang, Jia Guo, Chao Guo, Linlin Teng, Weijia Zhou, Haitao Luo
publish: 2025 4th International Conference on Advanced Sensing, Intelligent Manufacturing (ASIM)
abstract: The traversability and traction efficiency of lunar rovers across complex, soft lunar terrain directly determine their exploration range and mission success. Slip rate, as the core parameter characterizing wheel-ground interaction, has traditionally relied on complex dynamic models and physical sensors for estimation, making real-time, non-contact, intuitive assessment challenging. This paper proposes an innovative approach integrating Terramechanics experiments with hierarchical semantic image recognition for evaluating metal mesh wheel performance and classifying traversal states based on wheel track images. The research first designed and constructed an experimental platform simulating lunar gravity (1/6g) and multiple slip conditions for wheel-soil interactions. It systematically collected wheel track images at varying slip ratios (10%-60%) alongside corresponding dynamic data such as traction force and torque. Dynamic model and experiments revealed intrinsic correlations between slip rate and traction performance: traction force peaks at 50% slip rate, while traction efficiency is optimized at 30%, defining an energy-efficient, high-performance safe operating range. Addressing issues such as low contrast, uneven illumination, and edge blurring at high slip rates in footprint images under poor lighting conditions, this study innovatively constructed a Hierarchical Tree Distance Constrained Semantic-Enhanced Residual Network (SE-ResNet-50). This model not only learns fine-grained slip rate categories via cross-entropy loss but also introduces a hierarchical tree semantic distance constraint based on prior knowledge. This forces the network to bring semantically similar categories (e.g., different slip rates under ‘safe passage’) closer together and push semantically distant categories further apart in feature space. Consequently, it significantly enhances the model's discrimination capability and robustness for coarse-grained passage semantics (safe, cautious, prohibited). Validation results on a self-constructed dataset comprising 6,000 images demonstrate that the SE-ResNet-50 model achieves 92.63% accuracy in coarse-grained classification tasks, substantially outperforming both the traditional ResNet-50 (89.15%) and direct coarse-grained classification models (85.71%). This study confirms that leveraging visual semantic hierarchy priors can effectively enhance the performance and interpretability of image classification models in specific engineering applications, providing a novel and reliable technical approach for intelligent perception and autonomous decision-making in future lunar rovers.

title: Dual Active Bridge Converter Design for Solid-State Transformer–Based Power System Applications
authors: Khaled Ghambirlou, Adel Ali Abosnina, Gerry Moschopoulos
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Maghreb Meeting of the Conference on Sciences and Techniques of Automatic Control and Computer Engineering (MI-STA)
abstract: This paper presents the design and modelling of a Dual Active Bridge (DAB) isolated DC-DC converter for use as the galvanically isolated stage in a three-stage Solid-State Transformer (SST). The DAB performs bidirectional power conversion between medium- and low-voltage DC links while providing high-frequency isolation and controllable power flow. A systematic design methodology covering transformer turns ratio, leakage inductance, and switching frequency selection is developed. The converter employs single-phaseshift (SPS) modulation for forward and reverse power transfer. A MATLAB/Simulink model validates the analytical design through primary and secondary voltage waveforms under both directions of power flow. The results confirm accurate phase-shift control, consistent voltage transformation, and smooth bidirectional energy transfer, establishing the DAB as an efficient, compact, and SST-ready interface for future smart-grid systems.

title: A Transformer-Based Bushing Fault Diagnosis Method with Data Balancing
authors: Yian Wang, Meng Huang
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Smart Grid and Green Energy (ICSGGE)
abstract: Aiming at the limitation of insufficient diagnostic accuracy in traditional oil-paper bushing fault diagnosis methods when dealing with the imbalance problem of Dissolved Gas Analysis (DGA) data, this study proposes a novel fault diagnosis method combining an improved Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) with the Transformer architecture. A gradient-penalized Conditional Generative Adversarial Network is introduced to realize dataset equalization. The inter-cluster distance analysis of the expanded dataset is conducted via t-distributed Stochastic Neighbour Embedding (t-SNE) to verify the effectiveness of data augmentation. On this basis, the multi-head attention mechanism is adopted to focus on the influence of abnormal gas ratio values on the diagnostic performance of the model, thereby optimizing the diagnostic effect of the Transformer and significantly improving the fault diagnosis performance based on DGA data. The research results verify the effectiveness of the proposed method in solving the diagnosis of imbalanced small-sample data, and provide a new solution for the intelligent diagnosis of typical faults in oil-paper bushings.

title: A GNN-Based Coordination Framework for Embodied Intelligence in UGV Swarms
authors: Qianwen Li, Qiang Fu, Peiru Yang, Yiheng Yu, Yutian Yu, Yifan Hong
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Advanced Algorithms and Control Engineering (ICAACE)
abstract: To address modality conflicts and task-switching delays in multi-task coordination of unmanned ground vehicle (UGV) swarms, this paper proposes a Graph Neural Network (GNN)-based multi-agent coordination framework. The framework integrates a Graph Convolutional Network (GCN) for spatial feature extraction and an LSTM module for temporal collaboration modeling. A dynamic Graph Attention Network (GAT) is introduced to adaptively update interaction weights among agents, while Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) with meta-learning optimizes cooperative policies and enhances generalization across tasks. Simulation results show that the proposed method achieves an average success rate above 0.95 and improves coordination performance by 10-15% compared with baseline approaches.

title: A HSPSO-ANN Enhanced Framework for Cyber-Resilient Load Frequency Control Against False Data Injection Attacks
authors: Devisri Addagarla, D.M. Vinod Kumar, V. Venkataramana
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Trends in Material Science and Inventive Materials (ICTMIM)
abstract: The increasing integration of communication networks into modern power systems has made Load Frequency Control (LFC) loops highly vulnerable to sophisticated cyber threats. This paper presents a comprehensive framework for modeling, simulating, and detecting cyberattacks in a two-area interconnected power system. Various coordinated attack scenarios, including False Data Injection (FDI) are systematically modeled to emulate realistic intrusions affecting frequency regulation and tie-line power control. The study incorporates practical communication constraints such as network-induced delays and measurement distortions, enabling accurate representation of cyber-physical interactions within the LFC loop. The impact of these attacks on system stability, frequency deviations, and control performance is thoroughly analyzed. To enhance cyberattack detection capability, a novel Hybrid Strategy Particle Swarm Optimization-Artificial Neural Network (HSPSO-ANN) framework is proposed. In this approach, a modified PSO mechanism with adaptive inertia weight and learning factors is employed to optimally tune ANN weights and learning parameters, improving convergence speed and avoiding premature stagnation. The ANN classifier is trained using system dynamic features derived under both normal and compromised operating conditions. The proposed hybrid model achieves superior detection performance, enhanced stability, and faster training convergence compared with conventional ANN and standard PSO-ANN approaches. Simulation results demonstrate that the HSPSO-ANN framework provides a robust and intelligent solution for safeguarding LFC systems against cyber-physical attacks, contributing to improved resilience and secure operation of interconnected power grids.

title: Modeling of nonlinear ferroelectric microactuator with Fourier Neural Operator
authors: B.H. Nguyen, P. Czarnecki, G.B. Torri, V. Rochus
publish: 2026 27th International Conference on Thermal, Mechanical and Multi-Physics Simulation and Experiments in Microelectronics and Microsystems (EuroSimE)
abstract: While offering excellent electromechanical coupling properties, PZT material exhibits highly nonlinear and hysteresis behaviours at large applied electric held, limiting its usage in certain applications. For example, whereas PZT-based ultrasound transducers operate at their resonance frequency within the linear regime using small signals, actuators functioning in the quasi-static regime with large signals are subject to challenges associated with hysteresis compensation. In this contribution, we present a novel approach of neural operator learning for the characterization of ferroelectric micro-actuator under applied large signal and high frequencies. Based upon the LDV measurement where the applied signals at various amplitudes and frequencies and the corresponding deformation as labelled training data, a Fourier Neural Operator (FNO) is trained. The proposed approach can predict the complex deformation of ferroelectric microactuator beyond the capacity of classical model. In addition, the success of neural-operator-based method can extend and exploit further the functionality of the ferroelectric actuator near resonance frequency.

title: QoS Position-Based Routing Protocol for Wireless Body Area Networks
authors: Liana Qabajeh, Lubna Hashlamoun, Mohammad Qabajeh
publish: 2025 26th International Arab Conference on Information Technology (ACIT)
abstract: Wireless Body Area Networks (WBAN)s are gaining popularity, as real-time diagnosis and electronic therapy of patients are two key components driving the healthcare field. In addition to the digital technologies involved in this diagnosis, such as the Internet of Things (IoT). The importance of this network appeared during the COVID-19 pandemic. Routing in WBAN networks is one of the most difficult areas to protect patients in critical situations. Many of the demands of network Quality of Service (QoS) conflicts with the demands of mobile nodes. Our research has focused on the reliability and latency of services provided by static or mobile nodes. Our newly proposed protocol is a hybrid QoS Positionbased Routing (QPR) introducing a classification of data priority and nodes mobility. QPR classifies the type of data sensed as critical or normal. When data is critical and nodes are static, data are directly transmitted to the sink to guarantee low latency. Additionally, it tries to improve network reliability by employing a multipath strategy when the data is critical and the nodes are mobile. The performance of QPR has been compared with the Ad hoc On-demand Distance Vector Routing (AODV) and the Multipath Rings Routing (MPRR). This comparison has been conducted using the OMNeT++/Castalia simulator. Simulation results showed that QPR protocol improves reliability and latency compared with AODV and MPRR even when considering large number of nodes

title: Using Big Data to Improve the Effectiveness of Go-to-Market Strategies
authors: Amer O. Abu Salem
publish: 2025 26th International Arab Conference on Information Technology (ACIT)
abstract: This paper examines how big-data analytics (BDA) can make go-to-market (GTM) strategy more precise, faster to learn, and easier to scale in digital commerce. We present an end-to-end framework that treats the “five Vs” of big data as design constraints across the pipeline: governance and identity resolution; warehousing with ETL/ELT; feature engineering; and model layers for discovery and decisioning. The analytical toolkit combines k-means clustering for behavior-based segments, decision-tree models for interpretable prediction, short-horizon time-series forecasts for pacing, uplift modeling to target incremental response, and multi-touch attribution to assign credit across journeys. These models are tied to controlled experiments and a simple budget-optimization step so that findings translate directly into actions on audiences, messages, channels, and spend. Using realistic Omni channel data, we illustrate how the approach improves targeting precision, stabilizes channel efficiency, and raises conversion while respecting privacy and maintaining MLOps discipline. The results suggest that organizations that institutionalize this loop-measure, learn, and reallocate-enter markets with fewer surprises, scale winners earlier, and retire weaker motions sooner. We conclude with practical guidance for adopting the framework and outline research opportunities in creative-level testing and long-term value optimization.

title: Network Forensic Investigations and Machine Learning Techniques for Early Detection of Ransomware
authors: Hamdi Abu-Helo, Mohammad M. N. Hamarsheh, Nur Izura Udzir
publish: 2025 26th International Arab Conference on Information Technology (ACIT)
abstract: In recent years, the propagation of computer malware has been accelerated at a rapid rate, with ransomware emerging as a particularly powerful threat. Organizations from different sectors have suffered large financial losses due to the fast spread of ransomware. To address this issue, our study focuses on using network behavior analysis to proactively identify ransomware incidents and provides an effective solution to this problem. In this research we built an isolated and controlled testbed environment and analyzed 145 ransomware samples from four different families to give an extensive analysis of their network behavior with a particular focus on identifying ransomware patterns. We capture the network traffic and extract more information from the infected host devices; the collected data gives valuable information about the communication characteristics. The investigation employs three machine learning algorithms - Random Forest (RF), k-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), and Gradient Boosting - to analyze the captured data. The results show an exceptional accuracy in binary and multiclass detection with notable execution time efficiency. The study also explores a diverse range of communication protocols used by ransomware such as HTTP, and TCP with focus on the use of the TCP flags SYN, ACK, and RST.

title: Brain Stroke Classification Using YOLOv8
authors: Mahmoud Elhussieni, Sleiman Alhajj, Lama Alhajj, Kashfia Sailunaz, Mehmet Kaya, M. Kemal Özdemir, Jon Rokne, Reda Alhajj
publish: 2025 26th International Arab Conference on Information Technology (ACIT)
abstract: Brain stroke is a leading cause of mortality and long-term disability worldwide, underscoring the critical importance of rapid and accurate diagnosis for effective clinical intervention. This paper presents an automated classification approach for identifying stroke types, specifically ischemic stroke, hemorrhagic stroke, and normal brain tissues from computed tomography (CT) images using the YOLOv8 deep learning architecture. The study employs the Brain Stroke CT Dataset from the TEKNOFEST-2021 Artificial Intelligence in Healthcare Competition, which contains 6,653 anonymized and expertlabeled axial CT slices. The methodology employed in this study includes a comprehensive data preprocessing model, a fine-tuning stage using transfer learning, and performance evaluation using key metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score. Experimental results demonstrate that YOLOv8 achieves high classification accuracy across all the three categories mentioned above. This is supported by a detailed confusion matrix and visual examples of model predictions. These findings suggest that YOLOv8 can serve as a powerful tool for assisting clinicians in the rapid triage and diagnosis of patients with a brain stroke, particularly in time-sensitive scenarios. This work contributes to the growing application of artificial intelligence in medical image analysis for effective and timely decision making by offering a practical and efficient solution for stroke classification, with potential applications in both resource-limited and high-volume healthcare settings.

title: Load Forecasting Based on Decoupling and Causal Inference Considering Demand Response
authors: Wenbin Liu, Yang Liu, Haidong Yu, Fayang Tian, Luyang Guo, Qianyu Zhao
publish: 2026 5th International Conference on Smart Grid and Green Energy (ICSGGE)
abstract: With the widespread implementation of demand response (DR) mechanisms in power systems, load forecasting faces new challenges arising from the heterogeneity and uncertainty of user response behaviors. This paper proposes a load forecasting framework based on load decoupling and causal inference. First, a variational autoencoder (VAE) is employed to decouple total load into base load and response load, while graph convolutional networks (GCN) enable spatiotemporal feature separation. Second, nine response pattern recognition models are constructed based on dual-dimensional classification of temporal dimensions and user types. Then, a graph attention network (GAT) is designed to achieve multimodal feature fusion, and structural causal models (SCM) with counterfactual reasoning are utilized to quantify the causal impact of electricity price changes and adaptively optimize model parameters. Finally, a Bayesian deep ensemble learning framework is constructed to generate prediction intervals at different confidence levels. Case studies demonstrate that the proposed method achieves average MAPE of 2.46% across three DR scenarios, representing 20.1% MAPE reduction and 20.7% RMSE reduction compared to the best baseline method, with improvements most pronounced under critical peak pricing scenarios.

title: Dual-Space Similarity Search: Collaborative Indexing and Density-Aware Pruning
authors: Siyu Jia, Jingxiu Su, Bingnan Ma
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Advanced Algorithms and Control Engineering (ICAACE)
abstract: To resolve graph construction overheads and quantized retrieval throughput cliffs, we propose a discrete-continuous collaborative architecture that elevates binary hashes to topological priors for physical memory routing. By shifting execution from random fetching to sequential I/O, billion-scale benchmarks demonstrate a 26-fold indexing speedup (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{2 2. 4 h}$</tex> to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{0. 8 6 h}$</tex>) at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{0. 9 4 5}$</tex> Recall@100. At 30,000 QPS, our operator pushdown mechanism reduces P99 tail latency by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{7 0 \%}$</tex> by eliminating network shuffling. Furthermore, the architecture ensures topology-lossless updates with a negligible <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{- 0. 1 1 \%}$</tex> recall fluctuation after <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 0}$</tex> million streaming injections, providing a scalable foundation for industrial-scale multimodal retrieval.

title: Multi-Dimensional Leadership and Dynamic Weight Adaptation for UAV Collaborative Path Planning
authors: Jinyao Lv, Mingliang Hou, Yuan Li
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Advanced Algorithms and Control Engineering (ICAACE)
abstract: Multi-UAV collaborative path planning is essential for safe and efficient autonomous flight in complex multiobjective environments. To address the unbalanced search and weak collaboration in traditional Grey Wolf Optimizer (GWO), this paper proposes MCAGWO, featuring a multi-dimensional leadership structure (path, formation, time) and a dynamic weight fusion mechanism. This design enhances collaborative optimization and adaptability through environmental feedback. Simulations show that MCAGWO outperforms GWO, improved GWO, and PSO in path length, time synchronization, and formation consistency. Ablation studies confirm the effectiveness of the proposed mechanisms.

title: Secondary Effects Causing Early Solder Fatigue Failure of QFN Components Subjected to Test- and Field Cycling
authors: Rainer Dudek, Peter Fruehauf, Lisa Kreher, Susana Richter-Trummer, Jan Albrecht, Sven Rzepka, Lutz Scheiter, Andreas Weigert
publish: 2026 27th International Conference on Thermal, Mechanical and Multi-Physics Simulation and Experiments in Microelectronics and Microsystems (EuroSimE)
abstract: Studies on solder fatigue failure of quad flat nonleaded (QFN) packages subjected to test cycling (e.g., -40/125°C, 1 hour) and field cycling (18/83°C, 7.1 hour) indicated unexpectedly early failures. Daisy chained packages QFN36 and QFN40 dummy components were used on FR-4 test boards. Thermal cycling had been made up to 4,000 test cycles and 17,850 field cycles (15 years). The paper addresses questions as to why this early failure occurs. Microstructural analyses revealed that SAC357 solder degradation in test- and field cycling follow standard processes and cannot explain the deviations. "Secondary effects" are therefore studied. Results on multiple effects are reported based on metallographic analyses, microscopic/SEM analyses, fluorescence microscopy, optical out-of-plane and inplane thermal deformation analyses, and comparison to FEM. It is shown that considering cure induced package shrinkage somewhat affects solder joints loadings, while formation of electronic molding compounds (EMC) surface layers due to thermooxidative cross-linking cannot be proven, even after 15 years of field cycling. Two additional secondary effects were observed, which seriously affect solder fatigue: internal package delamination between leadframe and EMC, and standoff filling (peripheral solder gap) by solder mask and flux residues. A parametric FE study revealed that both delamination and gap filling can reduce solder fatigue life by 1.5X and more. Even more important, by including these effects in FE-based predictions, they come in reasonable agreement with testing results and previous predictions for other components.

title: Adaptive Fuzzy-Based Three-Dimensional Impact Angle Control Guidance Law
authors: Kaiyi Jin, Yuanyuan Li, Fan Bai, Zhengcheng Zhang, Yitong Qin, Jingjing Li
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Advanced Algorithms and Control Engineering (ICAACE)
abstract: Aiming at the three-dimensional interception terminal guidance problem of maneuvering targets, a threedimensional impact angle control guidance law based on adaptive fuzzy is proposed. Firstly, a three-dimensional guidance model of the aircraft-target is established, and the guidance problem is transformed into a second-order multivariable system control problem about the line-of-sight angle. The system disturbance caused by the target maneuver is approximated by a fuzzy logic system. Secondly, the obstacle Lyapunov function is utilized to ensure that the line-of-sight angle and the line-of-sight angle rate in the normal plane and the lateral plane do not violate the constraints. Finally, the effectiveness of the proposed method is verified through numerical simulation experiments.

title: Integrated Multi-Physics Optimisation of MOSFET Power Modules for Reduced Parasitic Inductance and Enhanced Thermal Reliability
authors: Thibault Jacobs
publish: 2026 27th International Conference on Thermal, Mechanical and Multi-Physics Simulation and Experiments in Microelectronics and Microsystems (EuroSimE)
abstract: Modern power modules face concurrent challenges driven by miniaturization, high power density, and thermal reliability requirements. Conventional design approaches, often fragmented across separate tools and physical domains, fail to capture crucial electro-thermal and electromagnetic interactions early in the development cycle. This leads to delayed identification of parasitic effects, reduced design efficiency, and extended development times. To address this, we present an approach that integrates schematic-driven layout, parasitic extraction, and electro-thermal co-simulation into a unified, automated workflow. Orchestrated by an Efficient Global Optimization (EGO) strategy, the methodology systematically varies key parameters, such as MOSFET placement and wire bond thickness. The sensitivity-guided exploration achieved an 18.4% reduction in total parasitic inductance and a 5.3% decrease in maximum junction temperature. This automated parametric approach provides deeper insights into electrical-thermal trade-offs, enabling earlier, reliable design evaluations for next-generation power electronics.

title: Fed-AE: Privacy-Preserving and Zero-Day Intrusion Detection for IoT Edge Networks
authors: Arun Kumar B R, Sai Harshit Balaji
abstract: Internet of Things (IoT) deployments have increased rapidly in the recent years, and so have the risks of cyberattacks on IoT systems. Conventional Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) which depend on centralized data collection and fully labelled datasets are not safe in IoT environments due to privacy and resource constraints. This paper proposes Fed-AE, a privacy-preserving intrusion detection approach based on federated and semi-supervised learning. Each edge device trains a lightweight autoencoder using normal network traffic, while a central server aggregates model weights without accessing raw data. The framework is designed to operate under heterogeneous, non-IID data distributions. The proposed system was evaluated on the UNSW-NB15 dataset using 20 federated clients with Dirichlet-based partitioning (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\alpha=0.5$</tex>). Experimental results show an ROC-AUC of 0.90 and a PR-AUC of 0.51, with stable convergence observed within a few communication rounds. These results indicate that Fed-AE can support decentralized and privacy-aware intrusion detection at the IoT edge.

title: NexaLearn: A Generative AI-Based Multimodal Framework for Interactive Video Learning Assistance
authors: Harshit Srivastava, Disha Srivastava, Janhavi Agrahari, Aayush Pratap Singh, Anuj Singh
abstract: The meteoric increase in popularity of video-based e-learning platforms has made learning more accessible; nevertheless, due chiefly to their passive nature, these platforms fail to foster interactivity between learners themselves, actual contextualization of information, and retention. In this paper, we present a multimodal generative AI learning assistant based on interactivity and personalized learning from videos. The working framework comprises automatic speech recognition for extracting transcripts aligned with video over time, transformer models for embedding-based semantic representation, and FAISS-based retrieval from an indexed corpus of dense vector representations supporting retrieval-augmented generation (RAG). Multimodal content understanding gets realized by the application of optical character recognition and computer vision for extracting textual and visual information from lecture slides and diagrams. An interactive chatbot built using a large language model provides context-aware question answering using retrieved video segments tied to the exact timestamps. The system was built on scalable web architecture, with React.js as a front-end technology, Python Flask/FastAPI service as a back end for processing, and a cloud-hosted vector database for efficient indexing and retrieval. Experimental evaluation based on retrieval relevance and response accuracy metrics proves reduced information access time and increased learning efficiency. The proposed system converts the passive consumption of videos into an active intelligent and learner-centered educational experience.

title: Welcome from the MPCON 2026 Program Chairs
authors: N/A
abstract: We are pleased to welcome all the participants of the 2026 IEEE Madhya Pradesh Section Conference (MPCON), held at Rustam Ji Institute of Technology, Tekanpur, Gwalior, India, on 14-15 Mar 2026. This conference is flagship conference of Madhya Pradesh Section and is being organized in various Institutes as per bid of the Institutes of the Section.. We are to say how glad we are to have such a rich collection of works. The papers you will see during the presentation represent a careful selection of the current researches in theories and applications of computational intelligence, sensor Technology, Image processing, AI and intelligent Systems, Networks, Soft Computing and other tracks, an important step forward the development of the next generation intelligent systems.

title: Reconstruction and Analysis of Tumor-Specific Gene Regulatory Networks from scRNA-seq Data
authors: Liqun Chen
publish: 2026 5th Asia Conference on Algorithms, Computing and Machine Learning (CACML)
abstract: Gene regulatory networks (GRNs) systematically describe transcription factor (TF)-target gene (TG) interactions and are essential for studying cellular state transitions and disease mechanisms. However, the stability and reliability of GRN reconstruction are often compromised by two factors: the noisy, sparse, and heterogeneous nature of single-cell data, and the divergent outputs of algorithms built on different assumptions. Consequently, integrating complementary inference methods to construct robust GRNs and identify biologically and clinically relevant regulators is of great research value. To address this, we propose BCFG (Borda Consensus Framework for GRN inference), a consensus GRN inference framework based on improved Borda ranking. It integrates two complementary methods, SCENIC and DeepSEM, which respectively capture regulatory interactions via co-expression and deep generative modeling, and incorporates TF prior knowledge to screen and rank TF-TG regulations, yielding a stable consensus network. Using single-cell data from eight tumor and six normal tissues, we construct GRNs for malignant and normal epithelial cells separately. To exclude epithelial background, we remove shared regulatory edges between tumor and normal reveals tumor-specific GRNs for each of the eight cancer types. On these specific GRNs, the HITS (HyperlinkInduced Topic Search) algorithm evaluates node importance to identify hub genes. Cross-cancer overlap analysis then detects common and cancer-specific hubs. Finally, classification experiments on TCGA data assess the biological significance of these hub genes, and the results demonstrate that hub genes derived from tumor-specific GRNs effectively discriminate cancer subtypes, also providing indirect evidence for the relevance of the reconstructed tumor-specific GRNs.

title: Synchronisation Challenges in Radar Networks
authors: Anum Pirkani, Muge Bekar, Natalie Reeves
abstract: The fusion of measurements from multiple sensing nodes in a distributed radar network is essential for reliable 360° situational awareness, but it depends on precise inter-sensor synchronisation. This work analyses how timing, carrier-frequency, and sampling-rate offsets map into structured inter-sensor phase distortions that reduce coherent gain and defocus images. We develop a unified error-to-phase model to derive mitigation strategies that maintain coherence under representative timing, jitter, and intermittent decorrelation due to occlusion and shadowing.

title: Task and Data Complexity in Gamified Text Labelling: When Do Real and AI-Generated Images Help?
authors: Fatima Althani, Chris Madge, Massimo Poesio
abstract: This paper examines whether AI-generated images improve user experience and performance in gamified text-labelling tasks. Across two experiments, we investigate how images interact with both task complexity and data ambiguity. In Experiment 1, participants completed either a simple part-of-speech (POS) tagging task or a more complex natural language inference (NLI) task, with or without AI-generated images. Results showed that task complexity strongly influenced accuracy, engagement, enjoyment, and cognitive load, while AI-generated images provided no consistent benefit. Notably, in the POS task, engagement was higher in the absence of images. Experiment 2 focused on data complexity within NLI, comparing AI-generated images, Flickr images, and a no-image condition across low- and high-ambiguity items. Even when images were designed to be semantically aligned with the text, visual context did not improve engagement or accuracy, nor mitigate the effects of ambiguity; accuracy was often highest without images. Together, these findings suggest that in linguistic annotation tasks, performance and experience are driven primarily by textual complexity rather than visual support, challenging assumptions about benefits of adding images to gamified text annotation.

title: Corrections to “Hybrid CNN–BiLSTM Approach for Wildlife Detection Nearby Railway Track in a Forest”
authors: D. S. Parihar, Ripul Ghosh
abstract: Presents corrections to the paper, (Hybrid CNN–BiLSTM Approach for Wildlife Detection Nearby Railway Track in a Forest).

title: Near-field Physical Layer Security: Robust Beamforming under Location Uncertainty
authors: Chao Zhou, Changsheng You, Cong Zhou, Chengwen Xing, Jianhua Zhang
abstract: In this paper, we study robust beamforming design for near-field physical-layer-security (PLS) systems, where a base station (BS) equipped with an extremely large-scale array (XL-array) serves multiple near-field legitimate users (Bobs) in the presence of multiple near-field eavesdroppers (Eves). Unlike existing works that mostly assume perfect channel state information (CSI) or location information of Eves, we consider a more practical and challenging scenario in this paper, where the locations of Bobs are perfectly known, while only imperfect location information of Eves is available at the BS. We first formulate a robust optimization problem to maximize the sum-rate of Bobs while guaranteeing a worst-case limit on the eavesdropping rate under location uncertainty. By transforming Cartesian position errors into the polar domain, we reveal an important near-field angular-error amplification effect, i.e., under the same location error, the closer the Eve, the larger the angle error, which severely degrades the performance of conventional robust beamforming methods based on imperfect channel state information. To address this issue, we first establish the conditions for which the first-order Taylor approximation of the near-field channel steering vector under location uncertainty is largely accurate. Then, we propose a two-stage robust beamforming method, which first partitions the uncertainty region into multiple fan-shaped sub-regions, followed by the second stage to formulate and solve a refined linear-matrix-inequality (LMI)-based robust beamforming optimization problem. In addition, the proposed method is further extended to scenarios with multiple Bobs and multiple Eves. Finally, numerical results validate that the proposed method achieves a superior trade-off between rate performance and secrecy robustness, hence significantly outperforming existing benchmarks under Eve location uncertainty.

title: End-to-End Adaptation of LLMs for Low-Resource Languages
authors: P. K. Udith I. Sandaruwan, Nimesh M. A. Fonseka, Pamith C. Salwathura, Don Sanjeewa Alwis, Chathura Wanigasekara, Logeeshan Velmanickam
abstract: While Large Language Models (LLMs) have revolutionized information processing, their benefits are disproportionately skewed toward high-resource languages, leaving languages like Sinhala behind. Building on our earlier work, An Approach to Training and Fine-Tuning Large Language Models for Low-Resource Languages, this extended version presents the complete development and evaluation of a Sinhala Large Language Model (LLM) obtained by training a LLaMA 3.1 base model for this under-represented language. Developing the Sinhala LLM required addressing challenges inherent to low-resource language modelling. The scarcity of online Sinhala text and the prevalence of printed-only literature required extensive efforts in corpus creation, translation, and noise reduction. Sinhala’s complex orthography further necessitated the design of a custom tokenization strategy aligned with existing pre-trained architectures. Additionally, the process of continued pre-training, fine-tuning, and quantization was constrained by limited hardware and memory resources. Despite these limitations, the trained SinhalaLLMdemonstrates significant progress in adapting large-scale architectures to a low-resource context. Experimental results highlight consistent improvements in text generation quality, contextual understanding, and response coherence. This study demonstrates that effective LLMs can be built for low-resource languages even with limited hardware, providing a reproducible framework for researchers facing similar constraints.

title: A Domain-Incremental Learning Framework based on Defect Replay and Gaussian Mixup for Industrial Defect Detection
authors: Zhen Zhao, Jinhai Liu, Huanqun Zhang, Fengyuan Zuo, Xiangkai Shen, He Ren
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: Incremental learning is a critical yet challenging problem in automation engineering, especially across heterogeneous domains. Existing incremental learning methods utilize mixup and mosaic techniques to replay previous knowledge. However, the saliency discrepancy between replayed samples is often ignored, leading to suboptimal accuracy. To address this problem, we propose a novel replay-based incremental learning framework that replays previous defect samples based on mixup and mosaic. To dynamically assign samples to different image augmentation techniques, a saliency score calculation mechanism is proposed by using posterior probability and spatial location. To eliminate the step noise brought by mixup, we propose a Gaussian operator-improved mixup to smoothly merge previous foregrounds with current images. Additionally, a domain-attentive distillation loss is proposed to further alleviate forgetting. Experiments on three scenarios verify the improved accuracy of the proposed method, achieving improvements of 2.4% mAP on weld defect detection, 2.7% mAP on steel defect detection and 4.0% mAP on no-service rail defect detection, respectively.

title: Complex-Valued Signal Recovery in Interferometric Electron Paramagnetic Resonance Spectroscopy
authors: Selina Eckel, Ahmet Çağrı Ulusoy
abstract: Magnetic resonance spectroscopy is widely used in biomedical research, and electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) is part of this toolbox as a microwave technique that probes unpaired electrons in an external magnetic field. Broadband EPR spectrometers are particularly valuable for studying frequency-dependent effects. Interferometric detection offers a high usable dynamic range for broadband EPR spectroscopy but can distort the absorptive and dispersive line shapes through residual amplitude and phase imbalances. We show that this behavior can be understood in the complex plane and corrected by a post-processing rotation. The resulting method restores the physical EPR signals without additional hardware or measurement time, enabling robust broadband EPR measurements.

title: AI Based System for Mass Screening of Kidney Diseases Using Ultrasound Images
authors: Geeta Rani, Nidhi Kundu, Vijaypal Singh Dhaka, Anish Sharda
publish: 2026 5th Asia Conference on Algorithms, Computing and Machine Learning (CACML)
abstract: Kidney ultrasound imaging is widely used for the initial assessment of renal abnormalities due to its safety, low cost, and real-time capability. However, manual interpretation of ultrasound images is time-consuming and highly dependent on radiologist expertise. It incurs high cost, and long waiting time for primary screening of kidney diseases, particulary in hospitals with a large patient base. This study proposes an automated pipeline to assist radiologists for mass screening of kidney ultrasound images associated with reports into normal and abnormal categories using real-life hospital data. The abnormal class in this study includes clinically relevant renal conditions such as renal calculi, simple cysts, and mild hydronephrosis, as identified from corresponding ultrasound reports. The proposed pipeline focuses on kidney segmentation and classification on the dataset comprising 1,347 ultrasound images acquired from two hospitals over a two-month period. The dataset suffers from artifacts, variability in resolution, acquisition conditions, and requires annotations. After extensive data cleaning and preprocessing, deep learning-based segmentation was performed using both Mask R-CNN and U-Net models. On a test set of 25 images, Mask R-CNN achieved a mean Intersection over Union score of 0.64. Another model U-Net outperformed it with a mean IoU score of 0.85. Further, texture features were extracted from the segmented kidneys using the Gray Level Co-occurrence Matrix followed by classification. Logistic Regression and Support Vector Machine achieved the highest accuracy of 75.86 % on the reeallife test dataset. Thus, this work highlights the potential of combining deep learning-based segmentation with texture-based classification to assist radiologists during primary screening and reduce manual workload.

title: Implementation of synthetic inertia emulation in the WECC Generic Model for Large-scale PV Plants
authors: András István Babai, Regina Török, Csaba Farkas
publish: 2026 IEEE Power and Energy Conference at Illinois (PECI)
abstract: The accurate modeling of photovoltaic power plants is essential for maintaining safe system operation as renewable energy penetration increases. This paper presents a review of current modeling approaches and provides a detailed analysis of the WECC Generic Model for Large-scale PV Plants, specifically regarding active power and frequency regulation.A significant challenge in modern grids is maintaining frequency stability, which is traditionally dependent on the inherent inertia of synchronous generators, a property naturally absent in PV systems. To bridge this gap, this study proposes and implements a control strategy for synthetic inertia emulation within the industry-standard WECC Generic Model.The proposed model is tested through simulations on a single-node system based on the Central European system separation event of January 8, 2021. Results demonstrate that the integration of synthetic inertia into the PV model significantly enhances frequency stability during disturbance scenarios, offering a viable solution for low-inertia power systems.
