title: Soft and Hard Scaled Relative Graphs for Nonlinear Feedback Stability
authors: Chao Chen, Sei Zhen Khong, Rodolphe Sepulchre
abstract: This article presents input-output stability analysis of nonlinear feedback systems based on the notion of soft and hard scaled relative graphs (SRGs). The soft and hard SRGs acknowledge the distinction between incremental positivity and incremental passivity and reconcile them from a graphical perspective. The essence of our proposed analysis is that the separation of soft SRGs or hard SRGs of two open-loop systems on the complex plane guarantees closed-loop stability. The main results generalize an existing soft SRG separation theorem for bounded open-loop systems which was proved based on interconnection properties of soft SRGs under a chordal assumption. By comparison, our analysis does not require this chordal assumption and applies to possibly unbounded open-loop systems based on their hard SRGs.

title: Q-MA3DQN: Quantum-Secured Scheduling for Contact-Constrained Decentralized Satellite Federated Learning via Multi-Agent Quantum-Dueling Double Deep Q-Networks
authors: Bikash K. Behera, Sarah M. Alhammad, Ahmed A. Khalifa, Shahid Mumtaz, Hussein Abulkasim
abstract: The proliferation of satellite constellations and onboard AI capabilities has given rise to Decentralized Satellite Federated Learning (DSFL), enabling collaborative model training without raw data exchange. However, DSFL faces unique challenges in contact-constrained environments, where limited inter-satellite communication windows, heterogeneous data distributions, and emerging quantum-capable adversaries threaten both model performance and communication security. This paper introduces Quantum-Secured DSFL (QS-DSFL), a framework that integrates Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) and quantum-resilient encoding into DSFL scheduling to ensure confidentiality and integrity of model updates against quantum-enabled attacks. The DSFL scheduling problem is formulated as a hierarchical Markov game under limited observability, capturing both the multi-agent nature of satellites and the hierarchical decision-making between communication scheduling and local training. To solve this, we propose a Multi-Agent Quantum-Dueling Double Deep Q-Network (Q-MA3DQN) architecture, combining classical and quantum computation to accelerate policy learning and adapt to dynamic contact topologies. Simulation results, conducted on both realistic orbital contact patterns and synthetic benchmarks, show that QS-DSFL achieves higher federated model accuracy, lower communication overhead, and enhanced resilience against quantum adversaries compared to classical DSFL approaches. QS-DSFL additionally demonstrates robust performance under severe non-IID data distributions, communication constraints, and Byzantine threat models. The Q-MA3DQN scheduler maintains stable final accuracy even at strong heterogeneity levels (Dirichlet α = 0.1–1.0), achieving up to 4.2% higher accuracy than classical RL-based scheduling in skewed regimes. Pareto analysis reveals that QS-DSFL consistently lies on the accuracy– communication efficient frontier, achieving near-optimal accuracy while keeping total transmitted bits comparable to lightweight greedy strategies. Under quantum-adversarial and Byzantine conditions, QS-DSFL retains a 1.1–2.0% accuracy advantage over classical MA3DQN at corruption levels up to 30%, demonstrating resilience in hostile environments. These results highlight the potential of hybrid quantum-classical reinforcement learning for secure, scalable, and efficient coordination in next-generation satellite AI systems.

title: Edge-Deployed AI Optimizing Fractional Frequency Reuse in Multibeam Satellite System
authors: Husnain Shahid, Miguel Ángel Vázquez
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Communications Society
abstract: The integration of native artificial intelligence/machine learning (AI/ML) capabilities into the radio access network (RAN) is rapidly emerging as a key enabler for scalable, adaptive and context-aware resource management in non-terrestrial networks (NTNs). Achieving efficient spectrum utilization and maintaining stable network capacity, however, depends as much on the design of control-loop network architectures as on the intelligence of the algorithm themselves. In this context, the convergence of AI and Open RAN (O-RAN) provides a transformative pathway for realizing flexible and powerful deployment of AI-enabled radio resource management (RRM) functions. To complement the overall resource management efficiency, this paper presents an intelligent fractional frequency reuse (FFR) optimization framework for multi-beam satellite system integrating into O-RAN ecosystem. This enables distributed-AI execution at the edge (in-space) to support complete AI/ML life cycle including data acquisition, model training, model transfer, inference and online adaptation. This hierarchical deployment, in fact, not just mitigates the latency, scalability and connectivity constraints inherent to centralized architectures but also a viable alternative to terrestrial networks (TN) in scenarios where terrestrial coverage is limited or overloaded. At the algorithmic level, the proposed intelligent FFR paradigm deployed at the network edge, jointly optimizes inner to outer beamwidth ratio and the power allocation to minimize network capacity deviation across multiple beams. This is achieved through a centralized training for decentralized execution (CTDE) based multi-agent framework, which enables cooperative decision-making among beams. Compared to non-cooperative multi-agent schemes and conventional single agent learning, the CTDE delivers more scalable, stable and effective optimization performance, particularly in heterogeneous multi-beam NTN environment.

title: Gradient-Guided Dual-Stream YOLO Combined With Graph Convolution for SAR Object Detection
authors: Peiyao Chen, Yinghua Wang, Hongwei Liu
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Currently, convolutional neural network (CNN) combined with Transformer has been widely applied in synthetic aperture radar (SAR) image object detection tasks. The CNN-Transformer hybrid models have the ability to model both local and global features simultaneously, however, several challenges remain. On the one hand, for large-size images to be processed, the multi-head self attention (MHSA) mechanism in Transformer aggregates feature information from the entire image, which results in high training costs and makes target features susceptible to interference from clutter features. On the other hand, the characteristics of SAR imagery are not utilized sufficiently. One special characteristic is that the gradient magnitude map of SAR imagery could reflect the target location and contours better than intensity image in some cases, and have been utilized in some existing detection methods. However, the current way of introducing it into the detection model either introduce too many parameters or lose the detailed information in it. To address these limitations, we propose a gradient-guided dual-stream you only look once (YOLO) detector combined with graph convolution. First, we embed the Multi-Head Grapher (MHGrapher) into the YOLO detector. MHGrapher is designed to establish long-range dependencies between features with fewer parameters compared with MHSA, meanwhile it could suppress the long-range interference clutter features. Furthermore, we explore the hybrid embedding position of MHGrapher to ensure effective collaboration between graph convolution and convolutional feature extraction. Moreover, to introduce the gradient magnitude maps of SAR imagery into the model efficiently, a lightweight sub-network producing high resolution gradient feature maps is added into the model to form a dual stream network. The dual streams can extract features from both the intensity images and the gradient magnitude maps, and these features are then fused using the proposed Intensity and Gradient Feature Fusion module within a unified framework to enhance the ability of the model to locate and extract target features. The proposed method achieves 0.906 mAP@50 on the SARDet-100 K dataset, demonstrating its effectiveness.

title: Neuro-Adaptive Integral Sliding-Mode Consensus Control for Multi-ASVs With Unknown Nonlinearity via Dissipative Disturbance Estimation
authors: Sayekat Kumar Das, Pratap Anbalagan, Yibin Tian, Tingwen Huang, Yukang Cui
abstract: This article investigates a dissipative disturbance-estimator (DDE)-based neuro-adaptive integral sliding-mode control (ISMC) for multiple autonomous surface vehicles (ASVs) subject to unknown nonlinearities and mismatched external disturbances. A memory-based integral sliding manifold is first developed by integrating radial basis function neural networks (RBFNNs) and state-dependent input matrices to approximate nonlinear dynamics. A disturbance observer (DOB) is then designed to estimate mismatched disturbances and actively compensate for their effects. On this basis, an adaptive ISMC is constructed with dynamic laws that estimate the upper bounds of neural network approximation errors. Furthermore, an improved integral inequality and a novel asymmetric Lyapunov–Krasovskii functional (ALKF) are introduced, leading to dissipative performance criteria expressed as linear matrix inequalities (LMIs), from which disturbance-rejection gains are obtained. Finally, numerical simulations on multi-ASVs, together with a comparative example, validate the effectiveness and superiority of the proposed approach.

title: A Tensor-Attention and DARTS-Guided Detection Framework for Aero-Engine Blades Surface Defects
authors: Xiao Wang, Peishu Wu, Da Teng, Haonan Chen, Xiaoyang Bi, Nianyin Zeng
abstract: The reliable, real-time detection of surface defects on aero-engine blades is severely limited by complex geometry and lighting variations, which reduce detection precision under strict computational budgets. To address these coupled challenges, we present a lightweight detection framework, termed Tensor-Attention- and DARTS-Guided Network (TAD-Net), featuring a task-tailored architectural design that improves defect detectability while maintaining deployment-level efficiency for aero-engine inspection. TAD-Net consists of three synergistic components. (i) A Tensor-Attention Fusion Block enhances long-range dependency modeling for weak defect cues while suppressing redundant shallow activations. (ii) A DARTS-guided Residual Attentive Large-Receptive-Field Neck improves multi-scale feature fusion by enforcing cross-scale alignment and representation consistency. (iii) A Hierarchical-Rank Tensor Decomposition strategy reduces high-dimensional feature redundancy with minimal loss of semantic capacity, thereby lowering computational cost. These components are coordinated within a unified design to address the interplay between feature representation, multi-scale aggregation, and computational efficiency in aeroengine inspection scenarios. TAD-Net is evaluated on four benchmark datasets and consistently improves the accuracy and efficiency balance. On the Aeroengine-Blade dataset, average precision increases by about 4%, while computational cost can be reduced by over 10% through the proposed design, with comparable recall. These gains support real-time deployment in resource-limited aero-engine inspection systems, demonstrating the suitability of TAD-Net for integration into sensor-driven inspection and monitoring pipelines in safety-critical industrial applications.

title: Reinforcement Learning for Dynamic Neural Architecture Search in Federated EVCS Security
authors: Arif Hussain, Ankit Yadav, Gelli Ravikumar
publish: 2026 IEEE Texas Power and Energy Conference (TPEC)
abstract: Rapid adoption of Electric Vehicle Charging Stations (EVCS) has generated cybersecurity vulnerabilities in smart grid infrastructure, necessitating new anomaly detection systems that can react to emerging threats and protect data privacy. Federated Learning (FL) keeps data decentralized to protect privacy; however, FedAvg and FedProx use static, homogenous neural architectures that cannot adjust to client heterogeneity or changing attack patterns. Even FL Neural Architecture Search (NAS) approaches generate fixed designs per client without dynamic modification. This study proposes FedRNAS, a new framework that combines reinforcement learning (RL) with neural architecture search to enable dynamic model customization in federated environments. Our approach uses a Q-learning agent to continually update neural architectures for each client based on real-time performance feedback and changing threat scenarios. FedRNAS achieves 96.75% detection accuracy, a notable <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$+1.31 {\%}$</tex> improvement over standard FedNAS, according to extensive tests conducted on four EVCS datasets from the IEEE-123 bus system. Our work is the first to demonstrate RL-driven dynamic NAS in federated learning, allowing for autonomous adaptation to emerging cybersecurity threats in critical infrastructure while keeping the privacy benefits of federated methods.

title: An Examination of the Impact of DER Control on Voltage Stability Through QV Analysis
authors: Anirudh C.V. S, Amarasagar Reddy R. M, Tamer Ibrahim, Alberto Del Rosso, Mahendra Patel
publish: 2026 IEEE Texas Power and Energy Conference (TPEC)
abstract: The increased integration of distributed energy resources (DERs) into distribution networks presents new challenges for power system analysis. DERs equipped with volt-var control (VVC) contribute to enhanced voltage regulation and improved transmission and distribution (T&D) performance by providing localized reactive power support. However, assessing the impact of VVC on transmission planning and operational stability remains a complex task. This paper explores the influence of VVC on coupled T&D systems through a comprehensive QV stability analysis using a transmission–distribution co-simulation (TDCS) framework. Case studies on DER-rich networks highlight the strengths and limitations of the proposed approach.

title: TCPE-IRVCS: A Two-Stage Channel Parameter Estimation Framework for IRS-Aided mmWave MIMO for Vehicular Communication Systems
authors: Baojuan Liu, Dong Li
abstract: In this paper, we propose and investigate a two-stage channel parameter estimation problem for an intelligent reflecting surface (IRS)-aided millimeter-wave (mmWave) MIMO vehicular communication system. By exploiting the low-rank nature of mmWave MIMO channels, we develop a novel framework termed Two-stage Channel Parameter Estimation for IRS-aided Vehicular Communication Systems (TCPE-IRVCS). The two-stage design decouples parameter estimation to reduce the complexity and enable the modular implementation. In the first stage, we estimate the direction-of-departure (DOD) angles of the BS-IRS channel, the direction-of-arrival (DOA) angles of the IRS-user channel, and the effective time delays using the discrete Fourier transform (DFT) beamspace ESPRIT method. These parameters are estimated firstly since they appear in the Khatri-Rao structure of the received signal and can be decoupled from the cascaded channel, which reduces the dimensionality of the subsequent estimation problem. In the second stage, using the estimated parameters, we estimate the effective azimuth and elevation angles of the cascaded BS-IRS-user channel at the IRS and Doppler shifts. These parameters are deferred to the second stage as they are intrinsically coupled with the IRS reflection coefficients and the time-varying nature of the vehicular channel. For this stage, we employ the DFT-beamspace ESPRIT (DFE) method and on-grid compressed sensing (CS) techniques. Our TCPE-IRVCS framework is general-purpose, supporting any efficient multi-path or single-path channel estimation method at each stage. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed DFE method achieves significantly better performance under the TCPE-IRVCS framework compared to traditional approaches.

title: IEEE Transactions on Semiconductor Manufacturing Publication Information
authors: N/A

title: A Radial Overcurrent Protection Scheme Design for a Synthetic Distribution Feeder: Methodology of Design and Coordination
authors: Dylan Yi, Haorui Li, Jackie Villanueva, Khalid Al-Assaf, Karen Butler-Purry
publish: 2026 IEEE Texas Power and Energy Conference (TPEC)
abstract: NREL's SMART-DS project introduced realistic yet synthetic large-scale U.S. distribution networks with varying levels of photovoltaic (PV) penetration, offering an advanced testing environment. These synthetic networks provide a valuable opportunity to design and evaluate protection schemes. In this work, a radial overcurrent protection scheme was designed for a 12.47 kV urban distribution feeder (P1UDT18962 from the SMART-DS networks) without including any distributed energy resources. The protection scheme was developed and validated utilizing time-current grading-based coordination using CYME Power Engineering Software. The radial overcurrent protection scheme integrated into a simulation test bed provides a foundation for future work, such as studying the impact on the feeder and identifying issues associated with varying integration levels of inverter-based distributed energy resources and evaluating new protection schemes aimed at addressing issues identified in the impact studies.

title: Cyprus's Transition to Competitive Electricity Market: Toward EU-Aligned Liberalization
authors: Shabab Saleem, Andreas Poullikkas, Zunaib Ali, Ali Hamza, Tehseen Tariq, Nicholas Christofides
publish: 2026 IEEE Texas Power and Energy Conference (TPEC)
abstract: The European Union (EU) has significantly transformed its electricity sector by unbundling generation, transmission, distribution, and retail activities, creating a competitive and transparent market framework that improves efficiency, enables cost-reflective pricing, and supports large-scale renewable energy integration. Cyprus, with a small geographically isolated power system, committed early to adopting this liberalised EU market model. However, its transition has faced significant technical, institutional, and regulatory challenges. The absence of interconnection with the European mainland has imposed substantial constraints on frequency and voltage stability, complicating real-time system balancing. Moreover, the dominance of the historical state utility, limited private sector participation, insufficient smart meter penetration, and persistent regulatory delays have further slowed progress. To address these complexities, Cyprus has initiated a comprehensive electricity market reform program that introduces a structured day ahead market and a dedicated balancing Market, designed to align national operations with the EU target model. In parallel, the Great Sea Interconnector, a high voltage direct current link connecting Cyprus with Greece and Israel, is expected to strengthen system stability, improve energy security, and facilitate cross-border electricity trading, representing a pivotal step toward full market integration. Complementary initiatives, including the deployment of advanced metering infrastructure, the promotion of renewable energy communities, and the rollout of distributed energy storage systems, aim to increase flexibility and strengthen demand side participation. This paper examines Cyprus's transition within the broader evolution of the European internal electricity market and highlights how these ongoing reforms collectively support the island's pathway toward becoming a fully integrated, transparent, and competitive participant in the European internal electricity landscape.

title: Real-Time Implementation of Consensus Tracking Control for Battery Energy Management and SoC Balancing
authors: Hany A. Abdelsalam, Ali Arzani, Satish M. Mahajan, Sohag Kumar Saha
publish: 2026 IEEE Texas Power and Energy Conference (TPEC)
abstract: This paper presents the real-time implementation of a consensus-based tracking control technique for battery energy management systems (BEMS) and state of charge (SoC) balancing. The consensus tracking controller is designed by applying linear quadratic regulator (LQR) control on the battery linearized model in MATLAB, where the state variables are the battery's output power and energy. The LQR weighting matrices are selected to give reasonable overshoot, consensus mismatch, and consensus convergence time. A three-battery model is used from the hardware-in-theloop laboratory testbed and open platform (HILLTOP) to integrate the consensus tracker and its related communication parameters. One battery uses a hardware vendor controller, while the other two are controlled via Typhoon HIL software controllers. The hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) controller for battery 1 acts as the leader, with batteries 2 and 3 functioning as followers in the consensus control process. The results demonstrate that the output powers of all batteries reach consensus, aligning with the dynamic power setpoint, and maintain balanced SoC levels, effectively preventing overcharging or over-discharging.

title: Decentralized Online Rate Optimization in FDD Massive MIMO System With Delayed CSI Feedback
authors: Manidipa Sarkar, Siddharth Deshmukh
abstract: This work considers a massive Multiple-Input-Multiple-Output (MIMO) communication system in Frequency Division Duplexing (FDD) mode, where the signaling overhead due to the pilot broadcast and the subsequent Channel State Information (CSI) feedback negatively impacts the downlink and uplink data rates. Hence, we propose a joint judicious pilot broadcast strategy and a delayed CSI feedback mechanism, with the introduction of a metric Age-of-Channel-State-Information-at-Transmitter (AoCSIT), which characterizes the resulting staleness in CSI. We consider correlation across frames in the downlink channel and the non-ideal feedback channel to express the achievable downlink rate as a function of AoCSIT. Next, we formulate an optimization problem over the time horizon to maximize the sum downlink rate with constraints on the individual user's minimum required uplink data rate. Since the formulation is a sequential mixed integer non-linear program with intractable computation complexity, we first employ a Lyapunov formulation to reformulate the time horizon problem to a single time frame optimization problem. The reformulated problem is still a combinatorial problem; thus, we next employ dual decomposition to decouple the reformulation into subproblems that can be solved efficiently in a decentralized manner on the user devices. The implementation cost of the proposed decentralized scheme is minimal, and the algorithm converges steadily over time. The simulation results demonstrate the overall efficacy of the proposed decentralized solution.

title: Relative RGB-Depth Structure Network for Generalizable Depth Map Recovery
authors: Delong Suzhang, Lulu Zhang, Meng Yang, Nanning Zheng
abstract: Depth sensors are widely utilized to perceive the three-dimensional (3D) scene in autonomous robots and intelligent systems. However, it remains a challenge to acquire high-quality depth maps due to technical limitations of depth sensors. Recent depth map recovery models require a large amount of accurate RGB images and ground-truth (GT) depth maps for supervised training. Directly training with inaccurate GT depth maps may lead to overfitting especially near object boundaries. To overcome this issue, we propose a relative RGB-Depth structure network to leverage both accurate RGB and inaccurate GT depth for supervised training. We first develop a novel relative RGB-Depth structure constraint model to measure their structure similarity while minimizing the modality gap. This model is applied to constrain output depth with the supervision of RGB. When GT depth in training data is not accurate, RGB additionally constrains the structure of output depth as a supplement. We then adopt an adaptive weight strategy to avoid the texture-copy artifacts induced by the modality gap. Our network is realized by combining the designed loss and a modified U-Net, in which all normalization layers are replaced with Rezero. It supports efficient training on mixed indoor and outdoor scenes and generalization in unseen scenes. Extensive experiments on six unseen datasets show that our model consistently achieves superior accuracy and generalization when compared to recent baselines.

title: High-Robustness LiDAR-Based Mapping in Steel Coil Factory Environments Using Fast-LIO and Factor Graph Optimization
authors: Ruibing Yang, Bo Lu, Shifeng Wang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Intelligent Manufacturing, Robotics and Automation (IMRA)
abstract: Complex steel coil factory environments challenge LiDAR-based mapping due to metal reflection, repetitive structures, and vibration, degrading traditional SLAM performance. This study develops a robust method to meet industrial automation’s high-precision, real-time demands. Existing LiDAR-IMU fusion methods suffer from error accumulation or poor real-time loop closure. Our method integrates Fast-LIO frontend (real-time odometry) with factor graph backend (adaptive-weight odometry/loop closure fusion, loop trigger at 0.8 feature similarity) and adds pose-based abnormal frame detection. Experiments in a steel coil factory show: RMSE 0.03 m, 75.3% pose estimates ≤5 cm error, 1–4 cm reconstruction accuracy within 50 m. It enhances robustness and provides reliable technical support for industrial automation.

title: A Robust Principal Component Analysis Method for InSAR Atmospheric Correction Based on M-Estimation
authors: Xinwei Jian, Yuhan Su, Junhuan Peng, Xu Ma, Xiaoman Qi, Yueze Zheng, Xue Chen, Xu Li, Wei Xiong, Chenglei Hu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Tropospheric delay is one of the major error sources in interferometric synthetic aperture radar (InSAR) deformation monitoring. Conventional spatiotemporal filtering tends to oversmooth deformation signals, elevation-dependent phase models are mainly applicable to stratified atmospheric delay, and the direct use of external atmospheric products may introduce additional errors because of spatiotemporal mismatches and data-quality limitations. To address these issues, this article proposes a robust PCA-based atmospheric correction method that integrates multisource external atmospheric data. First, the interferograms are preprocessed to reduce the effects of the flat-earth term, elevation-dependent phase, and orbital errors. Then, the preprocessed interferometric phase and external atmospheric data, such as GACOS and ERA5, are jointly assembled into an observation matrix. A maximum-likelihood robust M-estimator and the median absolute deviation (MAD) are introduced to obtain robust estimates of the variances and covariances of the joint matrix, on which robust principal component analysis (RPCA) is performed to extract the common atmospheric component while suppressing the influence of outliers, nonshared components, and residual noise. Finally, the robust small-baseline subset (RSBAS) method is incorporated to further refine the time-series results. Simulation experiments and real-data analysis over the Jinsha River Basin demonstrate that the proposed method can effectively mitigate atmospheric delay effects and improve the stability of deformation-rate estimation and time-series inversion, providing an effective solution for high-precision InSAR deformation monitoring in regions with complex atmospheric conditions.

title: Directional Object Detection in Aerial Images
authors: Szilard Molnar, Levente Tamas, Zoltan Kato
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Object detection in remote sensing consists of recognizing and localizing objects of interest in a quasi-standard bird-eye view with an arbitrary orientation on the ground plane. This naturally leads to the question: Can we detect the direction of an object along with its location and object category? Herein, we propose a novel method, the Directed Object Detector (DOD), which is capable of detecting an object’s direction together with its minimal enclosing OBB, i.e., a full object pose within the ground plane. This is integrated into an object detector to create a single end-to-end neural network. Experimental validation confirms the state-of-the-art performance on both close and far-range remote sensing images in man-made and natural environments. Furthermore, we prove the advantage of DOD over the OBB approach in an image rectification application, that is not solvable with OBB. Code and data are available: https://github.com/molnarszilard/DOD.

title: An Event-Driven Dual-Mode Multilingual RAG Framework for Industrial Maintenance
authors: Ya-Jou Cheng, Shang-Liang Chen
abstract: Industrial equipment fault diagnosis faces critical challenges in non-English operational environments. This paper presents an Event-Driven Dual-Mode Multilingual Retrieval-Augmented Generation (EDM-RAG) framework for industrial maintenance. Unlike conventional query-driven RAG systems, EDM-RAG organizes diagnostic reasoning through event-triggered pathways that coordinate diagnostic signal interpretation, equipment context profiling, historical maintenance case retrieval, and severity-aware retrieval parameter adjustment. A proof-of-concept evaluation on 45 curated maintenance cases indicates promising diagnostic behavior and bilingual report generation under controlled conditions. Additional robustness experiments under fault-code corruption and textual ambiguity, together with prompt-invariance analysis for AI-assisted report assessment, further characterize system behavior under non-ideal conditions. Taken together, these results support viewing EDM-RAG as a proof-of-concept system-level diagnostic framework for controlled industrial maintenance evaluation rather than merely a simple retrieval-generation integration.

title: Accurate segmentation of pine wilt disease in UAV remote sensing imagery: From dataset construction to vision-language segmentation framework with coarse-fine dual-label supervision
authors: Zhiqiang Liu, Jizheng Yi, Zijie Wang, Aibin Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Pine wilt disease (PWD) poses a serious threat to the global security of pine forest resources. In complex field scenarios using unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) for remote sensing image segmentation, the early diseased and small lesion targets often manifest as weak features consisting of several pixels. Existing visual language segmentation models primarily focus on extracting features of the disease itself and often fail to capture such subtle pixel-level information, leading to a relatively high missed detection rate. Therefore, a coarse-fine layered semantically coupled segmentation network (CF-SCSNet) is proposed in this study, in which a core coarse-fine dual-label loss (CFL) strategy is incorporated as the key supervision module. Fine labels are used to precisely delineate the actual lesion areas and guide the model in learning detailed texture features. Coarse labels are generated by extending based on the fine labels and are used to cover both the lesion and its immediate surroundings (not the entire image), enabling the model to learn spatial correlation patterns between the lesion and the adjacent environment. Furthermore, to accurately capture the spatial distribution patterns of the disease, a pixel-aware context aggregation (PACA) module is designed based on text priors, which enhances local spatial relationship learning. Simultaneously, a high semantic fusion (HighSF) module is proposed. Through complementary differential receptive field designs, it effectively captures disease features at various infection phases. Finally, a PWD remote sensing image dataset is constructed, covers multiple geographical regions and is adaptable to complex field imaging scenarios. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that CF-SCSNet achieves outstanding performance with an mIoU of 83.55% on the self-built PWD dataset. To evaluate its generalization capability, further validation is conducted by using two public remote sensing segmentation datasets (FarmSeg and RRSIS), confirming the effectiveness and robustness of CF-SCSNet across different scenarios and tasks. Code is available at https://github.com/TechJots-Liu/CF-SCSNet.

title: Dominated Sorting Optimization Algorithm (DSOA) Implementing Digital Twin Power Grid
authors: Sandesh Acharya, Prakash Adhikari
publish: 2026 IEEE Texas Power and Energy Conference (TPEC)
abstract: The Digital Twin System's most crucial infrastructure systems undergo a radical transformation with the help of a multimodal language algorithm. A power system model can accurately anticipate future demand increases by leveraging a high-fidelity digital twin of the regional grid, powered by a forecasting framework that uses the Dominated Sorting Optimization Algorithm (DSOA). This study examines energy sources, including solar and coal, and microgrid/DER integration by applying the DSOA to capacity indices over many years. While coal capacity steadily shrinks, dispersed assets and renewables are expected to match the rising demand, according to the report. The digital twin makes projections that align with real and expected shifts in generating portfolios by modeling real-world dispatch, congestion patterns, and resource sufficiency across various technology scenarios. Power grid planners may use DSOA-derived load profiles to inform data-driven decisions towards decarbonization by including them in the twin. This allows for systematic testing of control schemes, resilience measures, and investment alternatives.

title: Toward Safety-First Human-Like Decision Making for Autonomous Vehicles in Time-Varying Traffic Flow
authors: Xiao Wang, Junru Yu, Jun Huang, Qiong Wu, Ljubo Vlacic, Changyin Sun
abstract: Despite the recent advancements in artificial intelligence (AI) technologies showing great potential in improving transport efficiency and safety, autonomous vehicles (AVs) still face great challenges when driving in time-varying traffic flow, especially in dense and interactive situations. Meanwhile, humans have free will and usually do not make the same decisions even situated in exactly the same scenarios, leading to data-driven methods suffering from poor migratability and high search cost problems, decreasing the efficiency and effectiveness of the behavior policy. In this article, we propose a safety-first human-like decision-making (SF-HLDM) framework for AVs to drive safely, comfortably, and with social compatibility and efficiency. The framework integrates a hierarchical progressive architecture, which combines a spatial–temporal attention (STA) mechanism for other road users’ intention inference, a social compliance estimation (SCE) module for behavior regulation, and a deep evolutionary reinforcement learning (DERL) model for expanding the search space efficiently and effectively to make avoidance of falling into the local optimal trap and reduce the risk of overfitting, thus make human-like decisions with interpretability and flexibility. The SF-HLDM framework enables autonomous driving AI agents to dynamically adjust decision parameters to maintain safety margins while adhering to contextually appropriate driving behaviors at the same time. Extensive experiments in car learning to act, an open-source autonomous-driving simulator (CARLA) validate the framework’s superior performance, which enlarges the minimum time to worst-case hazards (TWHs) by 41.8% to keep a safer distance away from others, while improving the average velocity by 2.5%, reducing the average acceleration and yaw rate by 23.5% and 60.5%, respectively. The results highlight the potential of SF-HLDM to bridge the gap between machine-driven precision and human-like flexibility in AV systems, paving the way for more interpretable and socially acceptable autonomous driving solutions.

title: Variational Ultrasound Channel Data Compression
authors: Beatrice Federici, Massimo Mischi, Ruud Jg Van Sloun
abstract: Software-defined ultrasound enables high-quality imaging and advanced applications, but real-time streaming of raw channel data over standard digital links requires dedicated compression methods. To address this need, we introduce an end-to-end trainable, non-linear transform coding model that uses variational inference to map each ultrasound channel data frame into latent variables that can be efficiently encoded using entropy coding. We incorporate a scale hyperprior, transmitted as side information, which adapts the entropy model to each input and enables it to handle the large dynamic range and rapidly varying statistics typical of ultrasound channel data. We train the model using ultrasound channel data frames of human carotid arteries and evaluate the empirical rate-distortion performance at different stages of the processing pipeline using both in vivo and phantom data. Our results show that non-linear transform coding consistently outperforms traditional methods such as JPEG without degrading reconstructed images or downstream measurements. The scale hyperprior further improves generalizability and coding efficiency without notably increasing computational cost. We achieve compression ratios of up to 30:1, potentially enabling channel data rates of digital ultrasound interfaces to match those of standard digital links.

title: A 24V-to-1V Double Step-Down Converter Using Differentiated Inductor-Currents with Steady Common-Mode-Level Control for 1.5V/μs Line Transient Response
authors: Ruichen Zhu, Hanshu Zhou, Yingping Chen, Ming Liu
abstract: To enhance the line transient response of double step-down (DSD) converter, this paper reports a control scheme of differentiated inductor-currents with steady common-mode-level (DISC) and a dynamic power-path resistance modulator (DPRM). The DISC control is able to stabilize the output (VO) and rebalance the flying capacitor’s voltage (VCF) simultaneously, while the DPRM can effectively suppress the intrinsic LC oscillation. A prototype chip was fabricated in a 0.18μm BCD process to construct a GaN-based DSD converter. Operating at 2MHz, the design accomplishes 24V-to-1V 5A power delivery with a peak efficiency of 89.8%. The measurements under 1.5V/μs line transient verify that the DISC control maintains VO variation <30mV and rebalances VCF synchronously with input change. The DPRM suppresses the intrinsic LC oscillation amplitude and duration by at least 38% and 35%, respectively.

title: Sparse Signal Blind Deconvolution using Bayesian MAP Estimation
authors: Jack Bourdin White, Abdeldjalil Aissa-El-Bey, Matthieu Arzel, Jean-Marc Leveau
abstract: Blind deconvolution tackles the issue of recovering a signal from a convolution between an initial signal and a filter with an unknown kernel. To address the ill-posed nature of blind deconvolution, we leverage the sparse characteristics of the signals in a pre-existent dictionary. Rather than imposing sparsity directly on the signal using L0 or L1 penalties, we express it as a prior on the signal’s covariance matrix. The hierarchical prior acts like a decoupling between the signal and its sparsity, making estimation a classical a posteriori problem. The proposition revolves around a maximum a posteriori estimation in an Expectation - Maximization framework for alternate optimization of the signal and the filter. We give simulation results in comparison with MAP oracle values for any sparse basis.

title: FUSleepNet: A Dual-Branch Time-Frequency Fusion Network for Sleep Stage Classification
authors: Zhaonian Guo, Xiang Li, Zhiheng Zhang, Xin Wang, Ke Zhang, Shixiong Chen
abstract: Automatic sleep staging plays a crucial role in diagnosing sleep disorders and understanding brain dynamics during sleep. Traditional manual scoring is time-consuming and prone to inter-rater variability, motivating the development of data-driven solutions. In this paper, we propose a dual-path deep learning model for sleep stage classification based on single-channel EEG. The FUSleepNet (Fusion U-Sleepnet) model extracts temporal features using Bi-LSTM and spectral features via spectrograms, which are then fused using a U-Net architecture to leverage both local and global information. Experimental results on the Sleep-EDF-78 and Sleep-EDF-20 dataset using 20 -fold cross-validation demonstrate that our model achieves improved performance across multiple metrics, with an Accuracy of 90.2%, Macro F1-score of 77.7%, and Cohen’s Kappa of 76.2% on Sleep-EDF-78, Accuracy of 92.1%, Macro F1-score of 80.4%, and Cohen’s Kappa of 79.7% on Sleep-EDF-20.

title: MEMS Magnetoelastic Sensor Design for Sensitive Exosome Detection: A Conceptual Modeling
authors: Sumedha Nitin Prabhu, Guozhen Liu
abstract: This work introduces a conceptual framework for the design and performance assessment of a micro-electromechanical systems (MEMS)-based flexible magnetoelastic sensor tailored for ultrasensitive exosome detection. Exosomes, nanoscale extracellular vesicles implicated in diverse physiological and pathological processes, represent a critical frontier in biomarker-based diagnostics. The proposed sensor calculations utilize properties of a ribbon-shaped METGLAS ${ }^{\circledR} \mathbf{2 8 2 6 M B}$ Alloy and rely on mass-induced changes in magnetization for detection. The model evaluates the sensor’s Limit of Detection (LOD) at ±0.65 magnetic bias fields, demonstrating an LOD of $149.38 \mathrm{pT} / \sqrt{\mathrm{Hz}}$ at +0.65 mT and $201.11 \mathrm{pT} / \sqrt{\mathrm{Hz}}$ at $-0.65 \mathrm{mT}(1 \mathrm{~Hz})$. Additionally, Sensor Detection Mass Sensitivity (SDMS), amplification factor, and the impact of fluid damping are conceptually evaluated to establish the feasibility of discriminating minute exosomal masses from background noise. This study highlights the sensor’s potential for highly sensitive, label-free detection of exosomes, paving the way for advanced Point-of-Care diagnostics in oncology, nephrology, and personalized medicine. Future research directions include sensor optimization, integration of multimodal transduction, and validation in complex biological samples.

title: Effects of Meander Configuration and Joule Heating on Thin-film Magnetoimpedance Properties
authors: Hiroaki Kikuchi, Yuta Tanaka
abstract: In the miniaturization of thin-film magnetoimpedance elements, the application of a meander structure is considered effective for efficient in-plane space utilization. To suppress the demagnetizing field distribution inherent in such structures, a design incorporating extended ferromagnetic parts was proposed, and its effectiveness was demonstrated. Furthermore, a simple method for controlling magnetic anisotropy was applied to the meander structure, and its usefulness was also confirmed.

title: Bridging AI and IoT for Early Risk Detection in Flood-Prone Urban Zones
authors: Federico José Machado Olivares, José Alberto Aguilar Marroquín, Denis Alfredo Altuve Santamaía, Edward Alfonso Arévalo Monge
publish: 2025 IEEE 43rd Central America and Panama Convention (CONCAPAN XLIII)
abstract: This paper presents the development and implementation of an early warning system (SIMA) in Central America, designed to mitigate the impact of natural disasters such as floods and landslides in densely populated and high-risk areas. The system integrates a network of monitoring stations equipped with environmental sensors (e.g., temperature, humidity, gas concentration, rainfall and others) and camera-based computer vision modules for real-time scene analysis. Some of these stations are powered by solar energy and support various communication protocols including LoRa, Wi-Fi, MQTT, and TCP, ensuring data transmission even in remote or infrastructure-limited regions. The architecture incorporates cloud computing through AWS services and FIWARE-based microservices to process, store, and visualize data, enabling timely alerts and public access to relevant information via a web interface. Experimental deployments, such as those in Apopa, El Salvador, demonstrate the potential of combining IoT (Internet of Things), artificial intelligence, and cloud infrastructure for disaster prevention and response. The results show effective data acquisition, storage, and visualization, including AI-driven detection of high-risk human activity. The study concludes that scalable early warning networks, when powered by modern technologies, offer a cost-effective approach to reducing human and material losses from environmental hazards.

title: Copyright Page
authors: N/A

title: Lightweight CNN-Based In-Loop Filtering for Video Coding with Hardware-Aware Optimizations
authors: Xiaolong Zhang, Yanchen Zhao, Xuewei Meng, Jiaqi Zhang, Haocheng Tang, Lin Li, Qi Wang, Siwei Ma
abstract: Neural network-based in-loop filtering significantly enhances video compression efficiency. However, high computational complexity hinders their deployment in real-time and ultra-high-definition scenarios. To address this, we propose a lightweight CNN-based in-loop filter for the luma component. In terms of model design, we utilize a U-Net-like architecture to learn the residual signal, incorporating depthwise separable 3 × 3 convolutions and 1 × 1 convolutions to reduce computational complexity, which results in a low complexity of only <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$37.707 \text{kMACs} /$</tex> pixel. For deployment optimization, we implement memory linearization to improve cache efficiency and combine blocked matrix multiplication with SIMD to maximize parallelism, ensuring cross-platform compatibility without third-party libraries. Experimental results on AVS4 EVM-0.9 (All-Intra) on a CPU platform show that the proposed method achieves BD-rate reductions of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1.36 \%, 0.35 \%$</tex>, and 0.34% for <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{Y}, \mathrm{U}$</tex>, and V components, respectively. Furthermore, the optimizations lead to a 91.5% reduction in decoding time, resulting in a decoding complexity of 7757% compared to the anchor.

title: Thermal Performance Evaluation of a Natural Bamboo Barrier as a Passive Cooling Strategy in Tropical Weather: Impact on Indoor Thermal Comfort
authors: Wilfredo Monroy, Rudy Wilfredo Merlos Ortiz, Carlos Roberto Pacas Herrera, Mauricio Orlando Gómez Argueta, Christian Alexander Rivas Trinidad, David Ernesto Cerritos Valdizón, Anselmo Valdizón Evangelista, Jose Luis Franco
publish: 2025 IEEE 43rd Central America and Panama Convention (CONCAPAN XLIII)
abstract: This study documents research aimed at improving thermal comfort in formal educational spaces, particularly classrooms exposed to direct solar radiation during afternoon hours. The project focuses on the implementation of a natural barrier composed of vertically stacked bamboo poles, measuring 12 meters in height and 5 meters in width, installed at the rear of a classroom. The purpose of this bamboo installation is to mitigate the effects of extreme heat and direct sunlight, which negatively impact the normal development of academic activities. The research compares thermal conditions between a classroom equipped with the bamboo barrier and another without this protective element, to evaluate the effectiveness of this bioclimatic solution. Temperature and humidity data are collected using a Fluke-brand measuring device, with readings taken both inside the classroom and at the rear door, which is identified as a critical point for heat infiltration. Based on the collected data, the study also explores the need for insulating materials to be applied to the doors, further enhancing thermal control. The findings offer practical recommendations for enhancing thermal comfort in school environments, promoting the use of natural elements like bamboo, which provide sustainable, functional, and aesthetically pleasing benefits. This proposal aims to contribute to the design of healthier and more efficient educational spaces, adapted to local climatic conditions.

title: High-Fidelity RK4IP Solver for the 2D-NLSE: Simulating Broadband Propagation with Polarization Dynamics in Optical Fibers
authors: Napoleon Gutierrez, Edward Arevalo, Calixto Rodriguez, Karen Stephanie Pimentel Hernandez, Wenceslao Rivas
publish: 2025 IEEE 43rd Central America and Panama Convention (CONCAPAN XLIII)
abstract: We present a rigorously implemented Runge–Kutta 4 in the Interaction Picture (RK4IP) solver for the two-dimensional nonlinear Schrödinger equation (2D-NLSE), incorporating higher-order dispersion and distributed propagation losses. Previous implementations often relied on simplified treatments of these effects, limiting physical accuracy in broadband or long-haul regimes. The proposed solver provides a self-consistent and numerically stable framework for simulating nonlinear pulse propagation in birefringent optical fibers, where dispersion, attenuation, and Kerr nonlinearity interact strongly. Losses are integrated directly into the frequency-domain linear operator, enabling continuous power evolution throughout propagation, while arbitrary dispersion orders capture broadband pulse dynamics. Polarization effects are modeled through a stochastic birefringent plate formulation. Validation against analytical and semi-analytical benchmarks confirms the solver’s accuracy and robustness, making it a practical and extensible tool for studying nonlinear and polarization-dependent phenomena in realistic optical transmission systems.

title: Gamification and Interactive Learning for STEAM: A Case Study Using UAVs to Foster STEM Interest in Secondary Education
authors: Ing. David Cerritos, Br. Gabriela Monterroza
publish: 2025 IEEE 43rd Central America and Panama Convention (CONCAPAN XLIII)
abstract: This paper presents the design, implementation, and evaluation of a $\mathbf{1 0}$-session extracurricular course for secondary education that employs gamification and interactive learning with Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) to foster interest in STEM disciplines. The methodology integrates a progressive agenda from UAV fundamentals to CAD design, assembly, configuration, and flight testing, with pre/post assessment instruments and satisfaction surveys. Expected outcomes include increased interest in STEM, development of technical skills (basic electronics, CAD, assembly, flight controller configuration), and soft skills (teamwork, problemsolving). The relevance of using UAVs and gamification elements is discussed, as well as limitations and future work.

title: Darkfield Optical Imaging and Image Processing for Nanoscale Defect Inspection on Patterned Wafers
authors: Jiaqi Hu, Yihong Huang, Tao Liu, Yuanyang Yao, Wenze Wang, Wei Li, Yurang Li u, Jiaqi Ye
abstract: A laser scattering-based darkfield imaging method is used to inspect nanoscale defects on patterned wafers. Noise contamination and background pattern residue in images can weaken or even obscure defect signals. In this paper, an image processing approach for scattering signals in the darkfield optical inspection system is described. By constructing an inspection framework that integrates spatial and frequency domain feature analysis, we effectively suppress noises and enhance defect features. Various patterned wafer defects with a line width of 55 nm are successfully extracted from optical scattered darkfield images, demonstrating an improved signal-to-noise ratio. The image processing approach achieves mapping from the raw scattering light intensity distribution to a high-contrast defect feature spectrum, providing an efficient image information processing solution for nanoscale defect inspection in semiconductor manufacturing.

title: A 6V-to-1V ReSC-Hybrid Two-Stage Merged Buck Converter with an Ultra-Small Inductor for VPD
authors: Zhibang Song, Zhiguo Tong, Junwei Huang, Zhongyao Zhu, Sai-Weng Sin, Yan Lu
abstract: This work presents a compact 6-to-1 V resonant switched-capacitor (ReSC) hybrid two-stage merged buck converter targeting point-of-load vertical power delivery (VPD) in XPU systems. The proposed topology merges a low-frequency ReSC stage and a low-voltage high-frequency inductor-first buck stage, enabling efficient regulation with simple duty-cycle control and greatly reduced volume of the passive components. The two stages (chips) are fabricated in a 180-nm BCD and a 65-nm CMOS process, respectively. Compared with conventional ReSC, The ReSC-Hybrid converter maintains high efficiency across most of the regulation range. With a compact 33nH inductor, it achieves 92.4% peak efficiency at 0.2 VCR and delivers 1.3 W/mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup> power density and 2.35 A/mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> vertical current density.

title: Optimized Deep Learning and IoMT-based Clinical Decision Support System to Detect Chronic Kidney Disease
authors: S. Veni, Nivethitha S
abstract: Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD) is a severe and worsening health condition, which needs to be diagnosed early to avoid complications and minimize spending on health care services. The conventional forms of diagnosis struggle to keep pace and are prone to mistakes particularly during the initial stages of the illness. To overcome the given constraints, this paper suggests a smart CKD monitoring device, which integrates data collection through the Internet of Medical Things (IoMT) with the deep learning methods. The system also does data preprocessing to deal with missing representations and normalizes the features to enhance the quality of data. Effective feature extraction in a Capsule Network is employed to extract the complex relationship between the clinical parameters. A dimension reduction and cost-effective feature selection algorithm is optimized. Lastly, Deep Separable Convolutional Neural Network, which is optimized using a nature-inspired optimization algorithm is deployed in order to correctly classify CKD and non-CKD cases. The results of the experiments conducted on a test CKD dataset indicate that the given approach offers a higher accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity than the traditional machine learning models. The results suggest that optimized deep learning with the help of the IoMT-based monitoring can assist in detecting CKD in time and enhance clinical decisionmaking.

title: Traditional Keyword Matching Versus AI-Based Resume Analysis in Recruitment Systems
authors: Mukul Yadav, Mohd. Zaid Ilyas, Manya Gautam, Nidhi Mishra
abstract: The booming number of job applications on the internet has created the need to have more effective resume screening. Conventional Applicant Tracking Systems are largely based on matching on keyword, and it does not tend to provide contextual relevance and transferable skills. In this paper, an AI-based screening system is suggested as part of a MERN stack recruitment system, which uses a dual-scoring system: lexical MatchScore and semantic AIScore. The system was tested on 100 resume job evaluations in five job postings. According to the results, 68 percent of applicants received varying ratings during the AI-based assessment as opposed to the conventional approaches. Moreover, recruiters favoured AIScore-based rankings in three out of every four, which is even better aligned with human judgment. The semantic approach offers a more detailed evaluation of the suitability of the candidates by minimizing the dependency on absolute overlap of keywords. This paper concludes that a hybrid model of screening is more accurate and transparent, which will promote human-based hiring decisions.

title: A Late Acceptance Hill Climbing for the Electric Vehicle Routing Problem with Adaptive Penalties
authors: Mansour Essgaer, Rabia Lamami, Aisha Abuhajar, Manar Arif, Weiam Saheri, Asma Agaal
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Maghreb Meeting of the Conference on Sciences and Techniques of Automatic Control and Computer Engineering (MI-STA)
abstract: The transition toward Green Logistics has necessitated the development of efficient optimization techniques for the Electric Vehicle Routing Problem (EVRP), a complex variant of the VRP constrained by battery capacity and recharging infrastructure. This paper presents a Late Acceptance Hill Climbing (LAHC) metaheuristic designed to balance solution quality with operational feasibility. The proposed framework integrates a penalized objective function with an adaptive penalty mechanism, a diverse suite of perturbation operators, and robust repair heuristics to manage energy constraints effectively. We evaluate the algorithm on standard benchmark instances and compare its performance against a state-of-the-art improved Harmony Search Algorithm. While the Harmony Search demonstrates slightly superior distance minimization on smaller topologies, the proposed LAHC exhibits competitive performance, specifically outperforming the benchmark by 5.67% on the larger E-n60-k5s9 instance. Furthermore, the analysis reveals that the proposed method optimizes charging frequency effectively, achieving a $\mathbf{4. 1 7 \%}$ reduction in total recharging stops across the dataset. Detailed topological analysis confirms the solver's capability to generate risk-aware solutions with safe battery degradation profiles, making it a robust tool for practical EV fleet management.

title: Sector-Scanning Ultrasonic Radar-Emulation System for Short-Range UAV Intrusion Detection
authors: Vinothkumar U, Nanjundeswari M, Naveen G, Nisita M
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Trends in Material Science and Inventive Materials (ICTMIM)
abstract: Low altitude UAV intrusion is an emerging security threat in remote border areas, whereby constant human manpower and aerial surveillance are costly in terms of operations and are influenced by weather conditions. In this paper, a low-budget radar-emulation prototype sector-scanning surveillance structure based on an ESP32 microcontroller and an ultrasonic time-of-flight (TOF) sensor is described. The system conducts an indexed 360° environmental scanning in eight angular sectors with the help of a stepper-motor-driven sensing assembly. Local processing of distance measurements is done by use of temporal filtering to remove spurious reflections and minimize false alarms. Authenticated cases of intrusion are sent over Wi-Fi to a remote monitoring station, where a visualization program written in MATLAB is used to give a real-time polar plot and a log of events. Under conditions of lowaltitude intrusion experiments, it has been experimentally validated that the directional detection has high reliability at a radius of 4 m, an average alert latency of 0.82 s, and 90% detection consistency in repeated tests. The intended architecture is a scalable prototype platform of radar-assisted border surveillance studies, focusing on modularity, powerefficiency, and cost-efficient implementation on resourceconstrained deployments.

title: PINIC: Discrete Wavelet Transform-Guided Progressive Implicit Neural Image Compression
authors: Chaoy Lin, Yaojun Wu, Yue Li, Junru Li, Kai Zhang, Li Zhang
abstract: Recently, implicit neural representation (INR)-based image compression has attracted growing attention due to its low decoding complexity. However, most existing methods require multiple encoding processes to generate multiple bitstreams and reconstructions at different quality levels, leading to high storage and computational overhead. A more practical solution is progressive compression, which supports multiple qualities within a single bitstream. In this paper, we propose PINIC (Progressive Implicit Neural Image Compression), a DWT-guided framework that integrates progressive functionality into INR-based compression for the first time. Specifically, DWT energy is applied to adaptively allocate the energy of latent channels and latent will be entropy coded in the order of channel energy. Moreover, we adopt reparameterization to decouple network structures between training and inference, retaining the expressive power of complex architectures while reducing parameter count at deployment. Extensive experiments demonstrate that PINIC achieves state-of-the-art rate-distortion performance on the Kodak and CLIC2020P datasets, while simultaneously supporting progressive compression.

title: Digital Twin-Guided SDN Load Balancing for QoS-Aware IoT
authors: Joseph Shine, Antony Taurshia, Jenefa Archpaul, G. Sheeba Merlin, G. Nisha Malini, Catherine Joy R
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Trends in Material Science and Inventive Materials (ICTMIM)
abstract: With IoT networks, mixed streams and high-rate streams are increasingly accompanied with load balancing that should efficiently handle them to ensure the maintenance of QoS when demands are dynamic. SDN allows centralized traffic control, and congestion creation in IoT can rapidly constrain reactive control. Load imbalance occurs due to bursty arrivals, mobility and heterogeneous flows, which result in hot links, queue build-up and enhanced delay and loss. Standard RR-SDN, ECMP and utilization-greedy strategies are more effective in spreading but susceptible to non-stationary traffic, whereas Q-learning baselines are affected by the observation-to-action lag and rule churn. It has been suggested to utilize a Digital Twin a Digital Twin -Guided SDN load balancer to predict short-horizon congestion and rebalance flows in advance with a multi-objective policy made in conformity to periodic telemetry. Experiments consider four scenarios (100–600 IoT nodes, 4–10 gateways, 6–12 switches) with sensing/video/control traffic, burst ON/OFF patterns, and up to 30% mobility over fixed 100/50 Mbps core/edge links. In the stress scenario (600 nodes), DT-SDN achieved 54.8 ms delay, 1.75% loss, 98.25% PDR, and 79.1 Mbps throughput versus RR-SDN at 97.0 ms, 5.90%, 94.10%, and 64.4 Mbps. Averaged across scenarios, DT-SDN obtained 7.4 ms jitter, 4.21 J/MB energy, 74.9% peak utilization, and 0.97 fairness, confirming improved QoS, efficiency, and stable control under large-scale SDN-IoT conditions.

title: Research on Automatic Deployment and Dynamic Update Methods for Intelligent Monitoring Agents
authors: Mingjie Zhang, Ting Wang, Xiangguo Meng, Lixin Shen
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Advanced Algorithms and Control Engineering (ICAACE)
abstract: With the advancement of technological capabilities and continuous breakthroughs in autonomous and controllable technologies, the central business system has gradually evolved into a multi-system coexistence and parallel operation scenario through sustained construction and optimization. This has led to fragmented management of system software and hardware status information, requiring separate monitoring and display of operational status across various systems, which significantly increases manual operational burdens and reduces operational efficiency. Based on the current development status and operational characteristics of the central business system, this paper proposes an intelligent monitoring agent deployment and dynamic update method. This approach not only significantly improves deployment efficiency but also enables centralized real-time monitoring of system software and hardware status, providing an effective solution for enhancing system operation and maintenance efficiency and intelligence.

title: Optimization Design and Performance Study of Multilayer Broadband Absorbing Materials Based on Genetic Algorithm
authors: Song Li, Jinyu Zhou, Rongguo Wang, Peipei Wang, Fan Yang, Wenbo Liu
publish: 2025 4th International Conference on Advanced Sensing, Intelligent Manufacturing (ASIM)
abstract: To address the performance degradation of traditional absorbing materials under varying incident angles, this study proposes an optimization design method for multilayer absorbing materials based on genetic algorithms, aiming to enhance broadband absorption performance under multi-angle incidence. The optimization is conducted in the frequency range of 0.1 GHz to 10 GHz, where an optimization model for multilayer absorbing materials is established. The design variables include the thickness and type of each layer, with constraints on the total number of layers (5 layers), the total thickness (limited to 10 mm), and the thickness of each individual layer (limited to 2 mm). The objective function is defined as minimizing the reflectivity of the material to electromagnetic waves.Using a genetic algorithm to optimize the objective function, a fitness function is set to evaluate the reflection loss of the material under different incident angles, enabling the adjustment of absorption bandwidth between 0.1 GHz and 10 GHz. This approach overcomes the limitation of single materials that can only meet specific frequency ranges. Under electromagnetic wave incidence angles of 0° and 30°, the effective absorption bandwidth reaches 9.9 GHz, with pronounced absorption peaks observed at 2.7 GHz, 1.95 GHz, and 8.6 GHz, where the minimum reflection losses are -29.7 dB, -32.6 dB, and -33.2 dB, respectively.Simulation verification of the optimized design scheme was performed using the CST Microwave Studio electromagnetic simulation software. The results demonstrate that the optimized multilayer absorbing materials maintain excellent absorption performance across multiple incident angles, significantly improving the broadband absorption capability of the materials.

title: A 30kHz-BW 100.6dB-SNDR ΔΣ Modulator with Swapped Sampling-Feedback Operation and Optimized kT/C Noise Cancellation
authors: Xuanhao Zhang, Han Zhao, Congyu Jin, Hongshuai Zhang, Zheng Wang, Zhong Zhang, Ning Ning, Zhenbing Li, Xiangyu Mao, Shiheng Yang, Jiaxin Liu
abstract: The kT/C noise cancellation technique effectively reduces the sampling capacitance in ADCs; however, it typically introduces significant amplifier nonlinearity and high power consumption. To address these issues, this work presents a delta-sigma modulator (ΔΣM) where the sampling and feedback operations are swapped, and kT/C noise cancellation is embedded within the integrator. This approach fundamentally resolves the amplifier nonlinearity issue while simultaneously relaxing its bandwidth requirement, thereby reducing the overall power consumption. A prototype ADC with sampling capacitor of 0.9pF achieves 100.6dB SNDR and 118.3dB SFDR in 30kHz BW. The power consumption is 192μW, leading to a Schreier FoM of 182.5dB.

title: Fully Integrated CSCR-based Single-Stage Piezoelectric Energy Harvesting Interface with Independent Positive-/Negative-Cycle MPPT
authors: Guangshu Zhao, Chenxi Wang, Ningchao Lin, Hao Wu, Litao Zhang, Haoyang Sang, Man-Kay Law
abstract: This paper presents a fully integrated continuous scalable conversion ratio (CSCR)-based single-stage piezoelectric energy harvesting (PEH) interface suitable for arbitrary periodic vibrations. The proposed CSCR switched-capacitor (SC) converter with bi-directional ring-connected register sequence control can provide seamless buck/boost switching under continuous voltage conversion ratios (VCRs) while eliminating the reverse conduction loss. This single-stage PEH interface can also achieve simultaneous fine-grained piezoelectric transducer (PT) biasing, energy extraction and load regulation from distinct inputs/outputs. The capability of independent PT biasing for both the positive and negative half cycles under asymmetric excitations, together with the proposed P<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">IN</inf>-based dual-sided MPPT scheme and energy investment (EI)-assisted bias flip technique, can further enhance the system efficiency. Fabricated in 180nm BCD process, this work successfully demonstrates a fully integrated single-stage implementation with a total on-chip capacitance of only 468pF, while achieving a measured voltage flipping efficiency (VFE) of 84.8% and power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 85.3%. It also demonstrates a high MOPIR of up to 12.67×, corresponding to a 36% improvement over similar prior arts.

title: Ok-Topk-SP: A Novel Sparse Allreduce for Distributed Deep Neural Network Training on CPUs
authors: Miguel Pardo-Navarro, José I. Aliaga, Manuel F. Dolz
publish: 2025 24th International Symposium on Parallel and Distributed Computing (ISPDC)
abstract: Distributed data-parallel training is a widely adopted paradigm in deep learning, wherein each computational node maintains a replica of the model and computes gradients locally. To ensure model consistency, gradients are aggregated across nodes using the Allreduce collective operation. However, as model size increases, the communication overhead associated with this process can substantially degrade training performance. This work presents an implementation of the Ok-Topk algorithm (Li and Hoefler, 2022), which introduces a scalable strategy for performing Allreduce more efficiently using sparse gradients. The new variant proposed, named Ok-Topk-SP, differs in the region-based reduction phase from the original algorithm, and has been integrated within the PyDTNN environment for CPU clusters. The results demonstrate a reduction of over 50% in the training cost of the VGG16 model on the CIFAR-10 dataset compared to standard SGD, without compromising model accuracy. Furthermore, this study evaluates the benefits of increasing sparsity during distributed training, and demonstrate the performance improvements achieved by the proposed variant.
