title: A Multistatic Doppler Radar Measurement of Particulate Matter with a J-Band Radar Sensor
authors: Kennet Braasch, Alexander Teplyuk, Stephan Hauptmeier, Nils Pohl, Michael Höft
abstract: This work presents a novel multistatic measurement approach for monitoring particle streams using a radar sensor with a transmit frequency of 265.6 GHz (J-Band). The system utilizes a single transmitter and two receivers positioned at different angles to exploit the angular dependence of the scattering patterns in the Rayleigh to Mie transition region to obtain further information. A suitable measurement setup and corresponding results with glass spheres with a radius of 0.25mm are shown as a proof-of-concept measurement.

title: Design and evaluation of a bicycle-integrated antenna for prospective V2X connectivity of vulnerable road users
authors: Y. Gillani, M. E. Asghar, M. A. Hein
abstract: Integrating cyclists into V2X communication networks is a crucial step towards enhancing road safety and enabling intelligent, connected mobility solutions. This paper presents a slot antenna integrated into a bicycle handlebar for 5.9 GHz V2X communication of vulnerable road users. The compact design achieves a realized gain of 5 dBi and an impedance bandwidth of 200 MHz. A cylindrical metallic rod was initially employed as a handlebar, with the hollow structure acting as a waveguide. The comparison of measurements with simulations shows good agreement. The radiation patterns reveal an average deviation of approximately 5 dB from the omnidirectional response typically expected for a dipole-like slot antenna. This deviation is attributed to the closed-back configuration of the handlebar structure. The main lobe remains aligned along the forward driving direction, ensuring optimal angular coverage for V2X communication. The results demonstrate the feasibility of practical handlebar-integrated antennas that are easily adaptable to any type of bicycle.

title: Reflection Reduction Effects in Multi Probe Near Field Scanner Systems
authors: Martin Obermaier, Thomas Deckert, Dirk Plettemeier
abstract: Planar near field (NF) scanners are an attractive and versatile tool to measure and characterize large antennas in a relatively compact setup. NF Scanners are able to utilize multiple probes to increase measurement speed and scan area. In this work we investigate the effects of using multiple distinct probes to suppress reflections in the measurement setup.

title: A Compact Four-Way Waveguide Power Divider Optimized for Additive Manufacturing
authors: Frederike Bartels, Dominik Langer, Nico Weiss, Alexander Koelpin
abstract: This work presents a design scheme for a very compact in-phase elliptical waveguide divider specifically optimized for 3D printing. An E- and H-plane divider and a combination EH-plane divider based on this design scheme are presented with 64.1 %, 17.6 %, and 27.6 % simulated relative bandwidth in V-Band. The divider can be constructed using only CST Studio with no need for additional software. The EH-plane divider was manufactured and measured. The measurements show good agreement with the simulation results.

title: A Coarse-to-Fine Metric Learning Framework for Robust Identification of Overlapping Surgical Instruments
authors: Haichuan Li, Jingbo Wang, Wenxi Gan, Chen Jiang, Haocong Zhang, Chi Wang, Hewei Yu
publish: 2026 5th Asia Conference on Algorithms, Computing and Machine Learning (CACML)
abstract: Automated surgical instrument identification is crucial for patient safety but inherently challenged by severe overlapping, specular reflections, and data scarcity. Traditional closed-set detectors struggle with feature entanglement and lack extensibility for novel instruments. To address these issues, we propose a lightweight, coarse-to-fine metric learning framework that decouples the task into class-agnostic localization and openset identification. To support this paradigm, we develop a scalable synthetic data engine to augment detector training and introduce a Distractor Category regularization to autonomously filter background noise. Furthermore, through feature-aligned knowledge distillation, the framework is compressed into a highly efficient architecture. Our approach mitigates spatial ambiguity in overlapping scenes and supports flexible extension to new categories without retraining. It further enables efficient inference suitable for resource-constrained deployment settings.

title: LightGBM-SHAP-Based Clinical ML Feasibility Screening: Experimental Validation via Controlled Cross-Domain Data*
authors: Liting Xiang, Qian Zhou, Saijun Shi, Linfang Ding
publish: 2026 5th Asia Conference on Algorithms, Computing and Machine Learning (CACML)
abstract: Objective: To validate LightGBM-SHAP as a diagnostic tool for clinical ML feasibility via controlled cross-domain experiments. Methods: Identical pipelines applied to TCM rehabilitation (n = 57, positive control) and Parkinson's disease (n=42, negative control). The TCM target was deterministically constructed from predictors <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\Delta$</tex> VAS and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\Delta$</tex> Lysholm) to validate framework functionality under known signal strength, not generalizable prediction. PD represented real-world small-sample challenges. Performance evaluated via AUC-ROC and TreeSHAP, with XGBoost/CatBoost benchmarking. Results: Under deterministic conditions, TCM achieved perfect discrimination (AUC =1.000) with SHAP correctly identifying predefined predictors <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\Delta$</tex> Lysholm 45.7%, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\Delta$</tex> VAS 43.8%), validating explanatory power rather than clinical prediction. PD showed no capability ({AUC=0.500) with uniformly zero SHAP values, indicating feature-outcome dissociation. Algorithmic consensus was perfect in TCM <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\rho=1.00)$</tex> but near-random in PD <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\rho=0.12)$</tex>. Conclusions: SHAP serves dual functions: explaining successful models and diagnosing infeasible tasks via zero-value detection. We propose a preliminary threshold — <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$n<50$</tex> with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$p>10$</tex> risks failure-for clinical AI feasibility screening.

title: Fast World-Model Decoding for Lightweight 3D Autonomous Vehicular Scenes
authors: Charles Louie Siplao, Johnson Agbinya, Mohammad Belayet Hossain, Ajit Jaiswal
publish: 2026 IEEE 13th International Joint Conference on Cybernetics and Computational Cybernetics, Cyber-Medical Systems (ICCC)
abstract: We address a practical failure mode in cameraLiDAR fusion for urban driving: extremely small image disparities that, when converted directly to metric depth, yield unstable estimates and derail the front end before LiDAR cues can anchor the pose. We propose a minimal, intrinsics-aware conditioning step prior to fusion that rescales disparity using the true focal length and baseline and enforces a small minimumdisparity floor to bound depth, complemented by conservative dynamic-object masking and a LiDAR-anchored back end. On representative sequences, we verify that the data operate in a “small-disparity” regime and show that conditioning restores usable depth maps and allows the fusion pipeline to proceed. We further report preliminary trajectory metrics on KITTI Odometry Sequence 00 using standard absolute and relative pose errors: a LiDAR ICP baseline (filtered point-to-plane) achieves APE RMSE <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$28.14 \mathbf{m}$</tex> and RPE (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\Delta=10$</tex>) translation/rotation RMSE <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$17.37 \mathbf{m} / 23.58^{\circ}$</tex>. A disparity-conditioned point-selection variant yields comparable <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\operatorname{RPE}(\Delta=10)\left(17.04 \mathbf{~ m} / 23.42^{\circ}\right)$</tex> but higher APE RMSE (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$46.67 \mathbf{m}$</tex>), suggesting that disparity-based gating alone can reduce long-range geometric constraints needed for global consistency. These results quantify end-to-end operability and highlight where conditioning must be integrated as an explicit residual/prior (rather than selection-only) to improve trajectory accuracy.

title: Impact of Ethernet Network Conditions on Protective Relaying using Sampled Values
authors: Carlos Pimentel, Alexandre R. Aoki
publish: 2026 79th Annual Conference for Protective Relay Engineers (CFPR)
abstract: Electricity sector is experiencing deep changes since renewable energy technology started being adopted, and technology involved in these new energy resources had challenged engineers to adapt Protection, Automation and Control systems, running for over decades, to this new paradigm. In this scenario, IEC 61850 standard has been the basis for digitizing these systems and addressing these new needs, and this led to the widespread utilization of Ethernet communication within these time-sensitive systems. As growth of using communication networks for critical infrastructure has increased, these systems have become a target for cyber-attacks. This study presents the results of assessing a Process bus-based protection system under DoS flooding cyber-attack while executing its expected protection functions, in situations where the process bus network does not control and do filter this flood of messages to the IEDs. The assessment took place by simulating an attack directed at different components of the process bus-based protection system. Results have shown that uncontrolled DoS attack may be an effective way to prevent a Process bus-based protection system operating as expected and include non-deterministic delays on its operating time, even preventing it to operate at all. On the other hand, simple filtering capabilities embedded on IEC 61850 managed switches proved to be effective as countermeasures to be used by protection engineers on real world systems.

title: From Blackboard to Algorithm: AI's Impact on Teaching and Learning Design
authors: György Molnár, Levente Juhász, Gyula Gubán, László Kadocsa, Andrea Tick, Eniko Nagy
publish: 2026 IEEE 13th International Joint Conference on Cybernetics and Computational Cybernetics, Cyber-Medical Systems (ICCC)
abstract: The teaching of professional methodology occupies a central place in the basic curricula for engineering teacher training and vocational teacher training. In the new, changed training structure, there is less time for the transfer, acquisition, and application of new knowledge, and this new situation is transforming the content of the curriculum and the system of consultation tasks, as well as expanding training opportunities with the advent of AI. We are at the beginning of the transformation process, so every opportunity and form of development must be analyzed. At the University of Dunaújváros, the previous academic year's vocational teacher methodology course provided an opportunity to experiment with AI and to collect students' opinions on this topic using a questionnaire. During the experimental semester, we were interested in how AI affects student practice and how familiar our students are with the new opportunities. We have summarized the details of this study in our paper.

title: Research and Application of a Data-Driven Intelligent Construction System for Mountain Photovoltaic Power Stations in Complex Terrains
authors: Ruiqi Ning, Meili Ning, Meijun Ning
publish: 2026 5th Asia Conference on Algorithms, Computing and Machine Learning (CACML)
abstract: Driven by China's national strategic goals of achieving carbon peak and carbon neutrality, mountain photovoltaic (PV) power generation has become a significant component of the country's renewable energy portfolio. However, construction in complex mountainous terrains presents severe challenges, including topographical constraints, foundation construction difficulties, complex cable routing, logistical complications, and safety and environmental concerns. Acknowledging that existing research predominantly focuses on isolated technological aspects and lacks integrated systemic solutions, this paper proposes an intelligent construction system for mountain PV that deeply integrates data-driven and intelligent systems methodologies. The system synthesizes laser point cloud surveying, 3D intelligent layout, structural simulation analysis, wind tunnel testing, precise prestress control, modular construction, and dynamic logistics scheduling, thereby establishing a complete “problem-technology-data-verification” closed loop. Validated through typical projects in the Shaanxi Loess Plateau, Ningxia sandy-gobi-deserts (SGD), Yunnan mountainous areas, and Western Zhejiang, this technical framework indicates a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0 \%}-\mathbf{2 0 \%}$</tex> improvement in land use efficiency, an <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{8 \%} \boldsymbol{-} \mathbf{1 8 \%}$</tex> reduction in construction costs, and a project duration shortened by 20-30 days in these specific projects. It also achieves electrical safety targets, including a grounding resistance <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\leq 4 \Omega$</tex> and a lightningcaused failure rate of zero. This work provides a systematic, data-driven solution for the high-quality and efficient construction of mountain PV power stations under complex topographical conditions, offering substantial value for engineering application and academic reference.

title: Personalized Learning Path Recommendation System Based on Artificial Intelligence
authors: Lijun Li, Ying Chen
abstract: With the increasing popularity of online education and the rapid growth of learning data, existing personalized learning recommendation methods commonly suffer from problems such as single-path generation, inadequate learning state modeling, and low recommendation accuracy. To address this issue, this paper introduces an AI-based recommendation method based on knowledge graphs. By integrating multi-source learning resources such as courses, textbooks, and test questions, a knowledge graph with prerequisite relationships and semantic associations is constructed. This approach, combined with a graph neural network, achieves vectorized representation of knowledge points. A dynamic student modeling mechanism is designed, incorporating a forgetting factor and a time series update function to precisely characterize changes in learners' knowledge mastery. Furthermore, this paper proposes a multi-objective optimization path recommendation algorithm that simultaneously considers minimizing path cost, maximizing learning value, and balancing content diversity, achieving fine-grained control over the individual learning process. Experimental results show that over a 12-week experiment, both the recommendation system group and the control group show an upward trend in academic performance and knowledge mastery, with the improvement in the recommendation system group significantly exceeding that in the control group. Specifically, in week 1, the two groups show relatively little difference, with average scores and knowledge mastery rates of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$64.5 / 42.3 \%$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$63.9 / 41.7 \%$</tex>, respectively, indicating similar initial foundations. By week 4, the recommendation system group's average score has risen to 71.2, and its knowledge mastery rate reaches <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">${5 6. 4 \%}, {2. 5}$</tex> points and 3.6 percentage points higher than the control group, respectively.

title: MMWave Blockage-Aware Handover Strategy Using Mobility and Blockage Models
authors: C. Siva Kumar, Deeya Julka, Md Ankushavali
abstract: Millimeter-wave (mmWave) links enable multigigabit data rates but are highly sensitive to line-of-sight (LoS) blockages, which can trigger frequent handovers and service interruptions in dense small-cell deployments. This paper proposes a low-complexity blockage-aware handover strategy that leverages user mobility and an obstacle model to proactively select the serving base station (BS). At each decision epoch, the user scores candidate BSs over a short prediction horizon using the expected SNR in future time steps and hands over only when the predicted best BS changes. The approach is evaluated in a discrete-time simulator with a single mobile user moving along a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1-\text{km}$</tex> road, static building blockages, and random short-duration dynamic obstacles. Compared with a conventional instantaneous SNR-based baseline (with a hysteresis), the proposed strategy improves average throughput by up to 5.4 %, reduces the outage probability by up to 32 % in moderate BS densities, and reduces the handover event by up to 75 % by avoiding transient blockages. The results highlight that simple prediction using mobility and blockage models is able to improve the mmWave connectivity without the training overhead of learning-based methods.

title: Deep Learning–Based Anomaly Detection for Cancer Analysis Using Autoencoder Models
authors: Anju Mehra, Irfan Khan, Damodar Tiwari, Kailash Patidar
abstract: Cancer diagnosis is still a challenging task because of the high dimensionality of the data and the large class imbalance that exists between various types of cancer. Conventional methods of diagnosis, as well as most of the existing supervised machine learning algorithms, often face difficulties in handling such data characteristics, thereby resulting in decreased analytical efficiency. These difficulties have led to the need for an alternative approach to cancer diagnosis. The proposed approach uses a deep learning framework that involves an autoencoder to effectively identify typical patterns present in normal cancer data and deviations that represent abnormal data. Prior to training the model, the cancer data was preprocessed with utmost care to improve the quality of the data. After that, the autoencoder model was trained, and its performance was assessed. Based on the performance parameters, the model showed excellent predictive power for the normal cancer class with a precision of 0.7568, recall of 0.9495, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{F 1}$</tex>-score of 0.8423. The results validate the effectiveness of reconstruction-based deep learning models for anomaly detection in cancer datasets, especially when there is a large class imbalance between the majority and minority classes. It is worth highlighting that the proposed framework is meant to assist medical professionals rather than replace them. In general, the use of deep learning methods for anomaly detection has a lot of potential as a reliable and efficient method for enhancing outcomes of cancer data analysis.

title: Contemporary Advancements in Nanotechnology Based Epidermal Wearable Device Materials
authors: Koushick Venkatesh, Janani. P.
publish: 2025 IEEE Latin American Conference on Nanotechnology (LANANO)
abstract: Over the past decade, the significance of real-time health monitoring has grown, emphasizing the need for accurate clinical diagnoses and swift access to treatment options. The demand for wearable biosensing modules has surged, becoming a popular method for monitoring and as a preventive healthcare. However, to ensure the widespread adoption of these wearables as mainstream electronics, user acceptability and comfort must be prioritized. Despite the current focus on developing skin-friendly wearable technology, there remains a lack of comprehensive evaluation concerning the integration of smart materials to enhance user adherence. This overview briefs into the principles and practical applications of sensors crafted from environmentally friendly smart materials. Medical equipment's now incorporates soft electronics to improve patient tolerance and compliance, leveraging Nano-materials and Nanotechnology to enable the construction of smart wearable devices.

title: Solar Integrated Contactless Charging Technique for Electric Transportation
authors: D. Rajesh Babu, Vasupalli Manoj, Navya Kella, Vardhan Gunta, BalaChandra Kumar Bandlamudi, Sai Charan Gadagamma
abstract: Traditional conductive charging limits the growth of electric transportation due to mechanical fatigue and cable management problems in addition to concerns about safety when charging in poor weather conditions. A new sustainable and fully automatic alternative to conductive charging is offered through solar-integrated contactless charging (SICC) and consists of: 1) The collection of Photovoltaic Energy (PV) via Photovoltaic Arrays; and 2) The Wireless Power Transfer (WPT) of collected energy. The primary contribution of this paper is the design and experimental validation of a solar-integrated wireless charging system using an LCC-LCC compensation network. Unlike traditional grid-fed systems, this work focuses on optimizing the MPPT-to-Resonant-Inverter interface to maintain high efficiency (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$>90 \%$</tex>) under variable solar irradiance and coil misalignment conditions. Resonant Inductive Coupling enables the transfer of energy to and from EVs via the groundembedded transmitter pads and the receiver coils on the vehicle. A comparative analysis of various WPT topologies demonstrates that the use of LCC-LCC compensation networks delivers higher efficiency and greater tolerance for misalignment of the coils than the use of other compensation networks. Furthermore, experimental results on a 1 kW prototype operating at 85 kHz demonstrate that the integration of Solar Power reduces grid dependence. The system achieves a peak DC-DC efficiency of 91.2% at a 15 cm air gap, maintaining <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$>85 \%$</tex> efficiency under lateral misalignments of up to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0 c m}$</tex>. This research provides the basis for the development of self-sufficient charging infrastructure, making it easier for people to embrace sustainable transportation options.

title: An Edge-Native Machine Learning Framework for Proactive IoT Network Security
authors: Babul P. Tewari, Umair Lari, Poulomi Mukherjee
abstract: The deployment of heterogeneous IoT devices and the traffic generated from them creates a larger landscape in transmitting the data through the network. Often, the data in transit are sensitive and voluminous. In this context, appropriate security analysis in identifying the generated traffic alongside computationally efficient processing is required whcih is the main focus of this manuscript. So, an edge aware IoT security system is addressed using machine learning (ML), where the ML model is integrated with edge server for a reduced cloud overhead and computationally efficient processing. The use of machine learning is backed by extensive initial research demonstrating the limitations of traditional rule and signature-based security mechanisms in IoT networks. Due to the dynamic and heterogeneity of IoT traffic, static rules fail to generalize various devices and applications. Machine learning literature highlights the efficiency of data-driven models in learning baseline behavior, identifying deviations, and adapting to evolving attack patterns. Towards this, a detailed analysis between the ML model and DL model is presented.

title: Intelligent Navigation Control Through Visionbased Hand Gesture Recognition Using YOLOv8n
authors: Sachikanta Dash, Sakshi Kumari Sinha, Kenguva Vineesha, Priyanshu Sekhar Nanda, Naween Kumar
abstract: Advances in human-computer interaction (HCI) have enabled the development of intuitive, contactless navigation systems that enhance usability and accessibility across a wide range of applications. This work presents an intelligent navigation control system based on vision-driven hand gesture recognition using the YOLOv8n deep learning model. The proposed system performs real-time detection and classification of hand gestures through a camera interface, enabling users to issue navigation commands without physical contact. YOLOv8n, a lightweight variant of the You Only Look Once (YOLO) family, is selected for its favorable balance between detection accuracy, inference speed, and computational efficiency, making it well suited for real-time and embedded environments. The model is trained on a custom dataset comprising hand gestures corresponding to directional and control commands such as forward, backward, left, right, and stop. To improve robustness under varying environmental conditions, the framework incorporates preprocessing and data augmentation techniques, including background suppression, image normalization, and illumination variation handling. Experimental evaluation demonstrates that the proposed system achieves a gesture detection accuracy of 97.8 %, outperforming conventional CNN-based approaches in both precision and inference latency. The system operates with minimal delay, supporting seamless gesture-controlled navigation for applications in robotics, automotive systems, and assistive technologies. Overall, the proposed vision-based navigation framework is reliable, scalable, and user-friendly, highlighting the potential of YOLOv8n in the design of safe and accessible intelligent control systems that reduce dependence on traditional input interfaces.

title: Performance Evaluation of REBAR, VBF, and HydroCast Routing Protocols for Underwater Sensor Networks Using ARGO-Aided Simulation
authors: Shubham Kumar Singh, Balakiruthiga B, Akshita Ganesh Putta, Rajaram V
abstract: The need to have underwater acoustic sensor networks (UASNs) was highlighted by recent developments in underwater surveying and the management of natural disasters. Acoustic communication is the most feasible way of transmitting data considering the extreme attenuation of the electromagnetic signal in the marine setting. However, UASNs are faced with several limitations, such as low bandwidth, high latency, signal attenuation, and node mobility to give rise to time-varying network topologies. Although a number of routing protocols have been suggested to address these problems, they have typically been tested only on idealistic or even fixed network models, and these do not adequately reflect reality. In a bid to overcome this weakness, the current simulation uses empirical measurements of an autonomous drifting sensor, which simulates real environmental and mobility conditions. The simulation compares the three routing protocols mostly mentioned, such as the Reliable and Energy Balanced Algorithm of Routing (REBAR), Vector-Based Forwarding (VBF), and the Hydro-Cast routing, and identifies that performance features and constraints that are not apparent in the assessment of statistical models are realized under real operating conditions.

title: Equilibrium Optimizer-Based Optimal Scheduling of Multi-Source Power Systems with Renewable Energy, Electric Vehicles, and Hydrogen Energy Storage
authors: Hardik Modha, Jatin Soni, Deep Patel
abstract: This study describes a method based on the Equilibrium Optimizer (EO) to address the multi, energy economic and emission dispatch problem in an IEEE 57, bus power system that includes not only the conventional power sources but also renewable energy sources, electric vehicles (EVs), and hydrogen energy storage systems (HES). The developed model is capable of optimizing the scheduling of thermal power generators, wind and solar power plants, EVs charging/discharging, and HES operation simultaneously while satisfying multiple technical constraints. The paper considers three different scenarios: cost minimization, emission minimization, and combined economic dispatch. To assess the strong point of the EO algorithm, simulation studies and statistical analysis are carried out based on 50 independent runs. Experiments comparing the EO algorithm with other metaheuristics recently published such as Marine Predators Algorithm (MPA), Sine Cosine Algorithm (SCA), Grey Wolf Optimizer (GWO), and Biogeography, Based Optimization (BBO) showed that EO leads to lower operating costs, less emission, faster convergence, and more robust solutions consistently. The study of the convergence plots also showed that EO maintains a better balance between exploration and exploitation, which facilitates it in finding the optimal solutions more quickly and with fewer iterations. Consequently, the study results demonstrated that the EO, based approach that has been proposed is perfectly suitable for solving the large, scale, nonlinear, and multi, energy power system optimization problems.

title: Advanced Predictive Maintenance for Aircraft Engines Using Multi-Modal Deep Learning
authors: Ayan Ahmed Khan, Gagandeep Kushwah, Ayush Gupta, Prateek Bhadauria, Laxmi Shrivastava
abstract: This paper presents a comprehensive aircraft engine predictive maintenance solution based on deep learning models with optimal early failure detection. This solution addresses the critical problem of catastrophic engine failure focusing on early warning rather than accurate remaining useful life (RUL) predictions. We train and test four neural networks based on different architectures (transformer, LSTM with asymmetric loss function, CNN-LSTM, and ensemble) across four NASA C-MAPSS datasets, with varying operational complexities. Several novel aspects constitute our solution: (1) the asymmetric loss function that NASA encourages for penalizing late predictions, (2) engine-based split validation dataset to avoid leakage, (3) complexityaware feature engineering for per-dataset engine complexity, and (4) multitasking learning for RUL prediction and early warning binary classification. We show that the LSTM with the asymmetric loss function beats all architectures for three out of four datasets, with NASA scores of 5.66 (FD003) and 5.75 (FD001). Our solution achieves an early prediction ratio of 78.0% across all datasets, avoiding “surprise” failures. The transformer model yielded the highest performance on the highest complexity (FD004) dataset, demonstrating that it is well-suited for multi-fault and multi-operational complexity scenarios. We present an industrially applicable solution and theoretical understanding of model architecture selection for time-series-based prognostics.

title: Design of U-Shape and T-Shape Slot Loaded Multiband Square Microstrip Patch Antenna for GSM/Wi-Fi/5G and WLAN Applications
authors: Raj Kumar, Hitendra Singh, Ramesh Kumar Verma
abstract: This article presents a compact multiband square microstrip patch antenna using U-shape and T-shape slots. The overall size of proposed antenna is 42×42×1.6 mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup>. The Ushape slot is responsible for generation of lower and upper bands while the combined slot configuration enhances impedance matching across multiple frequencies. The presented antenna operates in quad band between frequency range 1.74-1.84,2.17-2.55,3.56-3.99 and 5.05-5.39 GHz with impedance bandwidth of 100, 380, 430 and 340 MHz respectively. The quad band antenna resonates at 1.77, 2.40, 3.79 and 5.22 GHz to provide return losses of -16.7, -21.96, 21.31 and -15.27 dB respectively. The presented quad band antenna is suitable for GSM-1800 for cellular mobile communication, Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11 b/g/n) and IoT devices, 5G NR mid-band (n77/n78)/WiMAX, and 5 GHz WLAN applications. The quad band antenna also exhibits peak gains of 2.57, 3.74, 4.72 and 3.22 dB at resonating frequencies 1.77, 2.40, 3.79 and 5.22 GHz.

title: Temporal Facial Dynamics-Driven Audio-Visual Emotion Recognition Using Vision Transformers
authors: Padma E, Mohana Prasath G, Chanthuru S R, Hariprasad M
abstract: Emotion recognition plays a crucial role in systems that understand feelings. It enables smart interactions between people and machines in fields such as healthcare, education, and human-computer interaction. Recent progress in self-supervised learning has greatly improved our ability to gather emotional cues from speech and facial data. However, many current facial emotion recognition methods rely on static facial images taken from a single frame. This approach fails to capture how facial expressions change over time. In this paper, we introduce a new framework for audio-visual emotion recognition based on the dynamics of facial movements. This framework explicitly tracks the changing facial expressions throughout video sequences. We sample multiple facial frames from each video and use a vision transformer to create framelevel visual embeddings. These embeddings are combined over time to capture the dynamics of facial emotions and are fused with speech embeddings obtained from a selfsupervised audio model. A transformer-based network then merges the audio and visual data for emotion classification. We conducted extensive experiments on various datasets. The results show that including temporal facial modeling leads to better recognition accuracy and stability compared to static facial images. Additional evaluations on different datasets and ablation studies back up the effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: Rumor Detection in Social Networks Using Pre-Trained Transformer and LSTM Neural Networks
authors: Maedeh Atighinezhad, Sogol Mortazi, Reza Ravanmehr
abstract: Social networks have become an essential medium for sharing individuals' thoughts and opinions. This has led social media to act as a catalyst for the spread of misinformation and for influencing public opinion and decision-making. Rumors are among the most prominent forms of misleading information on social media and must be identified as early as possible to prevent their harmful effects. In this paper, both user-generated content and user profile-related features are simultaneously utilized for rumor detection. Rumor-related textual features are extracted using the GloVe word embedding method and are then fed into a deep neural network based on LSTM. Finally, the extracted textual features, along with the features obtained from user profiles, are provided as inputs to an MLP network for rumor classification. The evaluation of the proposed approach using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score demonstrates superior performance compared to existing methods.

title: Why Text-Based Emotion Recognition on Social Media Remains Limited: a Data- and Contextcentric Analysis
authors: Kimia Sadat Hashemi Arvanaghi, Anahita Alhouei, Niloofar Mirzaei Chahardeh
abstract: This paper presents a unified data- and context-centric analysis of text-based emotion recognition on social media, focusing on research published between 2020 and 2025. Although recent advances in neural language models have substantially improved benchmark performance, real-world robustness and cross-domain generalization remain limited. We examine the field along three structural dimensions: (1) dataset construction and annotation practices, including subjectivity and annotator disagreement; (2) platform bias and cross-domain generalization across heterogeneous social media environments; and (3) context-related failure modes and evaluation limitations that obscure systematic errors. Across these axes, we identify a shared bottleneck rooted in dataset fragmentation, context omission, and reliance on aggregate metrics. Evidence from cross-dataset evaluations reveals significant performance degradation under domain shift, indicating that models frequently capture platformspecific cues rather than transferable emotional representations. Based on this analysis, we outline concrete research directions, including disagreement-aware learning frameworks, minimal context-rich annotation protocols, and standardized multi-faceted evaluation procedures incorporating calibration and cross-dataset testing. Our findings suggest that advancing emotion recognition requires principled dataset design and evaluation reform to enable robust emotion understanding in heterogeneous web environments.

title: Modeling, Control, and Stiffness Regulation of Layer Jamming-Based Continuum Robots
authors: Yeman Fan, Bowen Yi, Dikai Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Control Systems Technology
abstract: Continuum robots with variable compliance have gained significant attention due to their adaptability in unstructured environments. Among various stiffness modulation techniques, layer jamming (LJ) provides a simple yet effective approach for achieving tunable stiffness. However, most existing LJ-based continuum robot models rely on static or quasi-static approximations, lacking a rigorous control-oriented dynamical formulation. Consequently, they are unsuitable for real-time control tasks requiring simultaneous regulation of configuration and stiffness and fail to capture the full dynamic behavior of LJ-based continuum robots. To address this gap, this article proposes a port-Hamiltonian formulation for LJ-based continuum robots, formally characterizing the two key phenomena—shape locking and tunable stiffness—within a unified energy-based framework. Based on this model, we develop a passivity-based control (PBC) approach that enables decoupled regulation of stiffness and configuration with provable stability guarantees. We validate the proposed framework through comprehensive experiments on the OctRobot-I continuum robotic platform. The results demonstrate consistency between theoretical predictions and empirical data, highlighting the feasibility of our approach for real-world implementation.

title: Copula-Based Analysis of Fluid Antenna-Assisted Over-the-Air Computation
authors: Saeid Pakravan, Mohsen Ahmadzadeh, Wessam Ajib, Ghosheh Abed Hodtani, Ming Zeng
abstract: This letter studies an uplink over-the-air computation (AirComp) framework in which multiple user equipments are equipped with fluid-antenna (FA) arrays and operate over spatially correlated fading channels. By explicitly modeling channel dependence using the Gumbel copula, closed-form analytical expressions are derived for the cumulative distribution function (CDF) of the mean-squared error (MSE) of the aggregated function. The proposed analysis provides a quantitative performance characterization of AirComp under spatial correlation and provides analytical insights into the role of FA-assisted transmission in correlated wireless environments. Numerical results validate the derived expressions and show that FA deployment can substantially reduce the MSE compared with conventional fixed-antenna systems, although the achievable gain decreases as spatial correlation becomes stronger.

title: Wafer-scale estimation of thermal stability with magneto-optical measurement system for STT-MRAM
authors: Kenji Suzuki, Ingi Kim, Shinji Ueyama, Junho Shin, Je Ung Song, Wookrae Kim, Taehyun Kim, Junho Jeong, Sangwoo Bae, Sungho Jang, Jeong-heon Park, Myungjun Lee
abstract: The magnetic anisotropy field of perpendicular magnetic anisotropy films is an important magnetic characteristic directly linked to data retention for Spin Transfer Torque Magnetoresistive Random Access Memory (STT-MRAM). To measure the full-wafer characterization, we develop a polar magneto-optical Kerr effect measurement system incorporating a unique electromagnet with a temperature-controlled chuck and stage. The validity of this system is confirmed by correlation with the conventional technique. The spatial resolution of the system is demonstrated to 20μm, which is on the order of millimeters with conventional in-line MOKE tool. We also measured the uniformity of magnetic anisotropy across the full wafer at 150°C and estimated the temperature stability of patterned MTJ. The magneto-optical measurement system is expected to be promising for in-line inspection of device characteristics.

title: Cross-Frequency Feature Learning for Dual-Band Radar HRRP Target Recognition_supp1-3692758.pdf
authors: Yan-hua Wang
abstract: Radar ground target recognition using high-resolution range profile (HRRP) has attracted considerable attention recently. Existing methods primarily focus on single-band data, yet the HRRP of targets with similar shape structures are highly similar, making this a challenging task in complex scenarios. To address this, we use dual-band HRRPs to exploit target's scattering variations across frequencies, thereby improving recognition. In the paper, a cross-frequency feature learning (CFFL) method is proposed to extract inter-frequency discriminative features via both handcrafted and deep-learning perspectives. The handcrafted features are extracted at raw HRRP levels and their single-band feature level. The resulting heterogeneous feature sets are optimized using sparse coding and coding rate divergence maximization. For deep features, wavelet packet decomposition is integrated into a neural network to enable multi-scale feature extraction. Attention mechanism is employed to characterize the wavelet domain differences between the dual-band data, with training guided by a difference search loss function. Frequency interaction further enhances the deep feature representations. Finally, deep discriminant correlation analysis is applied to jointly optimize both feature types, preserving optimal discriminative information. Extensive experiments on three datasets were conducted to verify the proposed method's effectiveness.

title: Synthetic Priors for Real-World Detection: a Label-Free Framework for Identifying Ultra-Rare Objects
authors: Di Wang, Tongning Wu, Lei Yang, Xianjun Fan, Xin Ye, Renbing Huang, Xing Lu, Xu Xu, Congsheng Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: Detecting ultra-rare objects (frequency < 10-6) without positive annotations remains a critical challenge in computer vision. We propose a Synthesis-Prior Driven paradigm that eliminates the need for real positive samples. Our core insight is that models trained on statistically and perceptually indistinguishable synthetic data can generalize effectively to real-world environments. To achieve this, we introduce a Differentiable Morphological Modeling framework, which embeds explicit physical and geometric priors into a differentiable synthesis engine. Unlike black-box generative models, this approach ensures high-fidelity imagery with perfectly accurate, objective annotations. We validated this paradigm on the safety-critical task of detecting Circulating Genetically Abnormal Cells (CACs). Perceptual assessments confirmed that synthetic data is indistinguishable from clinical imagery (expert accuracy ≈ 50%). Consequently, our model, trained exclusively on synthetic data, achieved state-of-the-art performance on real patient samples (98.43% accuracy, 97.40% sensitivity), outperforming both human experts and existing automated methods. Furthermore, the model demonstrated exceptional robustness across a multi-center cohort (CV < 0.8%) and achieved high-precision zero-shot transfer in industrial defect inspection (DAGM 2007). This study establishes a principled methodology for ultra-rare object detection, offering broad applicability across domains with definable geometric priors.

title: CoreScaler: A Resource-Efficient Hybrid Scaling Framework for Dynamic Workloads in Cloud
authors: Dinghao Zeng, Fagui Liu, Runbin Chen, Jingwei Tan, Dishi Xu, Qingbo Wu, C.L. Philip Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network and Service Management
abstract: Containerized microservices face significant challenges in balancing service quality and resource efficiency under dynamic workloads. Existing approaches suffer from horizontal scaling’s cold start latency, vertical scaling’s resource ceilings, and hybrid methods’ limited adaptability. We present CoreScaler, a resource-efficient hybrid scaling framework based on analysis of CPU usage patterns revealing substantial consumption differences between working mode and waiting mode instances. This insight drives our dual-mode instance management model that distinguishes between working instances actively handling requests and waiting instances maintaining hot standby with minimal resource allocation. CoreScaler employs a master-subordinate distributed architecture where the master node performs capacity planning using multi-confidence interval predictions and contextual multi-armed bandit optimization, while subordinate nodes execute mode-aware CPU quota adjustments. Comprehensive evaluation on a Kubernetes cluster with a typical microservice system under four representative production work-loads demonstrates that CoreScaler maintains SLO compliance while reducing CPU and memory allocation by 22.53% and 30.83% respectively compared to state-of-the-art solutions. The framework achieves substantially higher resource utilization than single-dimension scaling approaches, validating the effectiveness of coordinated hybrid scaling for dynamic cloud environments.
